The DOT number ends with **four digits** in modern tyres. The first two digits indicate the **week** of manufacture, and the last two indicate the **year**. "0224" means the second week of 2024.

> [!WARNING]
> Tyres degrade due to **rubber oxidation** even when unused. KTU examiner expectation: tyres older than **5 years** (or **6 years per many OEMs**) from the DOT date are considered *aged* and should not be fitted, regardless of tread depth.

### Treadwear, Traction, Temperature (UTQG Grades)

For tyres sold in the USA, the sidewall carries a **Uniform Tyre Quality Grading (UTQG)** triplet:

- **Treadwear** — A comparative rating (e.g., 400). Higher = longer life. Reference is 100.
- **Traction** — AA, A, B, or C. AA is best wet-braking.
- **Temperature** — A, B, or C. A resists heat best at sustained high speed.

---

## 1.2 Tyre Removing — Demounting from the Wheel

**Tyre removing** (also called **demounting**) is the controlled disassembly of the tyre from the wheel rim, performed using either manual tools (tyre irons) or a **pneumatic tyre changer**. The objective is to separate the **bead** (the wire-reinforced inner edge of the tyre) from the **rim flange** without damaging the tyre, the rim, or the inboard **TPMS sensor** (if fitted).

### Conceptual Analogy — "Taking Off a Tight Jar Lid"

Removing a tyre bead from a rim is mechanically similar to **opening a vacuum-sealed glass jar**. The bead forms a tight, airtight friction-fit seal against the rim flange. To break this seal, you must (a) **depressurize** the system (deflate the tyre), (b) **break the bead** (loosen the lid), and (c) **peel it off** carefully without cracking the glass (rim).

---

## 1.3 Inspection — The Engineering Health Check

**Inspection** is a structured visual and tactile examination of the tyre to detect serviceability issues. It is performed *before mounting, after removal, before refitting, and during routine maintenance*. Inspection covers the **tread face**, **sidewalls**, **beads**, **inner liner**, and **rim interface**.

---

## 1.4 Cuts, Bulges, and Excessive Tread Wear

These are the **three principal rejection criteria** for a used tyre under any KTU-aligned automotive lab syllabus.

| Defect Type | Description | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Cut** | A breach of the rubber exposing the **ply cords** | Cord exposure invites moisture → **belt separation** → blowout |
| **Bulge** | A localized outward swelling of sidewall | Indicates **broken inner ply cords** → imminent rupture |
| **Excessive Tread Wear** | Tread depth below legal/OEM limit | Loss of **hydroplaning resistance**, longer braking distance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legal Tread Depth Limits (India):** **1.6 mm** for cars (Central Motor Vehicles Rule 91/92). OEM-recommended replacement is typically **2 mm to 3 mm**.

---

## 1.5 Pneumatic Tyre Changer — The Workshop Power Tool

A **pneumatic tyre changer** is a workshop machine powered by compressed air (typically **6–10 bar / 90–145 psi**) that performs:
1. **Bead breaking** — separating the bead from the rim flange
2. **Demounting** — removing the tyre from the rim
3. **Mounting** — refitting a tyre onto a rim
4. **Lubrication assistance** — applying tyre mounting lubricant
5. **Inflation** — seating the bead against the rim flange using a controlled air burst

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Mechanic"

If manual tyre changing is like a human opening a jar with bare hands, a **pneumatic tyre changer is a robotic assistant** that does the heavy pressing, the precise rotation, and the controlled air-burst seating — all while keeping the human away from danger zones.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resetting** the machine means returning all moving parts (turntable, clamping jaws, bead-breaker blade, mounting/demounting head) to their **home/neutral positions** so the next tyre of any size can be set up without interference.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decoding Tyre Codes — The Math Behind the Markings

### 2.1.1 Calculating Sidewall Height (Section Height)

The **sidewall height (H)** is computed from the section width and the aspect ratio:

$$H = \frac{SW \times AR}{100}$$

For a **205/55 R 16** tyre:

$$H = \frac{205 \times 55}{100} = 112.75 \text{ mm}$$

### 2.1.2 Calculating Overall Tyre Diameter

The **overall diameter (D)** of the tyre (unloaded, inflated) is:

$$D = 2H + \text{Rim Diameter (converted to mm)}$$

Since the rim diameter is given in **inches**, convert to mm by multiplying by 25.4:

$$D = 2H + (R_{\text{in}} \times 25.4)$$

For a 205/55 R 16:

$$D = 2(112.75) + (16 \times 25.4)$$

$$D = 225.5 + 406.4$$

$$D = 631.9 \text{ mm}$$

### 2.1.3 Load Index Decoding

The load index is referenced against a standard **LI ↔ kg** table. A few critical values:

| LI | 80 | 85 | 91 | 95 | 100 | 106 | 110 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| kg | 450 | 515 | 615 | 690 | 800 | 950 | 1060 |

### 2.1.4 Speed Symbol Decoding

| Symbol | L | M | N | P | Q | R | S | T | U | H | V | W | Y |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| km/h | 120 | 130 | 140 | 150 | 160 | 170 | 180 | 190 | 200 | 210 | 240 | 270 | 300 |

### 2.1.5 Revs Per Kilometre (Speedometer Calibration Check)

$$N = \frac{1000}{\pi D} \text{ revs/km}$$

For our 205/55 R 16:

$$N = \frac{1000}{\pi \times 0.6319} \approx 503.8 \text{ revs/km}$$

---

## 2.2 The Bead — Anatomy of the Critical Seal

The **bead** is the inboard edge of the tyre, formed by a bundle of **high-tensile steel wires** (often 6–8 strands of 0.96 mm wire) wrapped in rubber. Its job is to **mechanically lock** the tyre onto the rim and **maintain an airtight seal** without an inner tube (tubeless) or with one (tube-type).

**Bead seat angle** is typically **5°** on passenger car rims. The mating geometry is precision-engineered; misalignment greater than **0.5°** causes bead unseat under hard cornering.

---

## 2.3 Pneumatic Tyre Changer — Operating Theory

### 2.3.1 Compressed Air System Requirements

$$F_{\text{bead-break}} = P_{\text{air}} \times A_{\text{ram}}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{air}}$ = air pressure (Pa)
- $A_{\text{ram}}$ = effective piston area (m²)

A typical tyre changer ram of **8 cm² area** at **8 bar** delivers:

$$F = 8 \times 10^5 \times 8 \times 10^{-4} = 640 \text{ N (ram force)}$$

Combined with a **mechanical advantage** of approximately 5–8 in the bead-breaker linkage, the effective force on the sidewall exceeds **3000 N**.

### 2.3.2 Bead Seating Pressure

To "pop" the bead against the rim flange during inflation, a sudden air burst is delivered into the cavity between the rim and the bead:

$$P_{\text{bead-seat}} \approx 2.5 \text{ to } 3.5 \text{ bar (instantaneous)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Never exceed **3.5 bar** during bead seating. Use a **clip-on air chuck** and stand aside — exploding rims have caused fatalities.

---

## 2.4 Tread Wear Patterns — Engineering Diagnostics

| Wear Pattern | Probable Cause |
|---|---|
| **Center wear** | Over-inflation |
| **Edge wear (both edges)** | Under-inflation |
| **One-sided edge wear** | Misalignment (camber) |
| **Cupping / scalloping** | Unbalanced wheel, worn shock absorber |
| **Diagonal feathering** | Toe misalignment |
| **Spot wear** | Wheel imbalance |

### Tread Depth Measurement

A **tread depth gauge** (digital or vernier-based) measures from the tread surface down to the base of the deepest groove. The **TWI (Tread Wear Indicator)** is a small rubber bar moulded across the groove at **1.6 mm / 2 mm** depending on manufacturer.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Table

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{SW \times AR}{100}$ | H, SW, AR | mm | Sidewall height |
| 2 | $D = 2H + (R_{\text{in}} \times 25.4)$ | D, H, R_in | mm | Overall diameter |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{1000}{\pi D}$ | N, D | revs/km | Speedometer cal. |
| 4 | $F = P \times A$ | F, P, A | N | Pneumatic ram force |
| 5 | $W = \dfrac{LI_{\text{total}}}{4}$ (per wheel) | W | kg | Load per wheel |
| 6 | $P_{\text{seat}} \approx 2.5$–$3.5$ | — | bar | Bead seating |
| 7 | $V_{\text{max}} = f(\text{Speed Symbol})$ | — | km/h | Speed limit |
| 8 | $AR = \dfrac{H}{SW} \times 100$ | — | % | Aspect ratio back-calc |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any KTU answer script, always write **units with every numerical result** and **cite the standard** (e.g., "as per BIS IS 1566 / ETRTO / TRA"). This alone can lift a 5-mark answer to a 7.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Decoding "185/70 R 14 88T"

**Given:**
- Section Width (SW) = 185 mm
- Aspect Ratio (AR) = 70 %
- Construction = R (Radial)
- Rim Diameter = 14 inch
- Load Index = 88
- Speed Symbol = T

**Step 1 — Sidewall Height (H):**

$$H = \frac{SW \times AR}{100}$$

$$H = \frac{185 \times 70}{100}$$

$$H = \frac{12950}{100}$$

$$H = 129.5 \text{ mm}$$

*Logic:* The sidewall is 70 % as tall as the section width is wide. Multiplying SW by AR/100 converts the percentage to a direct length.

**Step 2 — Overall Diameter (D):**

$$D = 2H + (R_{\text{in}} \times 25.4)$$

$$D = 2(129.5) + (14 \times 25.4)$$

$$D = 259.0 + 355.6$$

$$D = 614.6 \text{ mm}$$

*Logic:* Two sidewalls (top + bottom) plus the rim's diameter (converted from inches to millimetres) gives the full loaded diameter.

**Step 3 — Revolutions per Kilometre:**

$$N = \frac{1000}{\pi D}$$

$$N = \frac{1000}{\pi \times 0.6146}$$

$$N = \frac{1000}{1.9309}$$

$$N \approx 517.9 \text{ revs/km}$$

*Logic:* Distance covered in one revolution = circumference = $\pi D$. So the number of revolutions to cover 1000 m = 1000 / circumference.

**Step 4 — Maximum Load per Tyre:**

LI = 88 corresponds to **560 kg** (from standard tables).

$$W_{\text{per tyre}} = 560 \text{ kg}$$

$$W_{\text{vehicle max}} = 4 \times 560 = 2240 \text{ kg}$$

*Logic:* The load index is *per tyre*, so a 4-wheel vehicle's maximum gross load equals 4× single-tyre rating.

**Step 5 — Maximum Speed:**

Speed Symbol T = **190 km/h**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Bead-Breaker Force

**Given:**
- Pneumatic supply pressure = **8 bar** = $8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- Ram piston diameter = **40 mm**

**Step 1 — Piston Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.040)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 0.0016}{4}$$

$$A = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Ram Output Force:**

$$F = P \times A$$

$$F = 8 \times 10^5 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F \approx 1005.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Force at Bead (with mechanical advantage MA = 6):**

$$F_{\text{bead}} = MA \times F_{\text{ram}}$$

$$F_{\text{bead}} = 6 \times 1005.3$$

$$F_{\text{bead}} \approx 6032 \text{ N}$$

*Logic:* A 1 kN pneumatic ram, amplified through a 6:1 mechanical linkage, delivers about 6 kN at the bead-breaker blade — more than enough to crack the bead-heel friction seal on a passenger car tyre.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Inspection Decision Tree

| Observation | Diagnosis | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Tread depth = 1.6 mm (touching TWI bars) | Legally worn | Replace |
| Tread depth between 2 mm – 4 mm | Aged but serviceable | Move to rear axle (for stability) |
| Cut exposing cord | Structural failure risk | **Reject / Replace** |
| Sidewall bulge > 5 mm | Broken ply cord | **Reject / Replace** |
| Sidewall bulge < 5 mm with deformation | Localized ply fatigue | **Reject / Replace** |
| Cracks in sidewall rubber (ozone cracks) | Aged rubber | Replace if DOT > 5 yrs |
| Bead damaged / deformed | Mounting safety hazard | **Reject** |
| TWI bars not visible but tread < 2 mm | Borderline | Replace |
| Uniform wear all over | Normal wear pattern | Continue use |

---

## 3.4 Pneumatic Tyre Changer — Resetting Procedure (Sequential)

The "resetting" operation returns the machine to its **home state**. This is critical between tyre jobs to prevent operator injury and tooling damage.

**Step 1 — Release air pressure** from the bead breaker, turntable, and clamp jaws using the bleeder valves.

**Step 2 — Position the mounting/demounting head** at the **far end** of the slide arm (away from the rim centre).

**Step 3 — Open the clamping jaws** fully to maximum diameter position.

**Step 4 — Lower the vertical column** (swan neck) to its **lowest position** and lock the column lock pin.

**Step 5 — Engage the turntable rotation lock** to prevent free-spinning during storage.

**Step 6 — Wipe down the bead-breaker blade** and apply a thin film of light machine oil to prevent rust.

**Step 7 — Cover the machine** with a dust cover if stored for long periods.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Decode the following tyre sidewall marking: "195/65 R 15 91H"**

**Model Answer:**

The marking breaks down as follows:
- **195** → Section Width = **195 mm**
- **65** → Aspect Ratio = **65 %**, meaning sidewall height is 65 % of 195 mm = **126.75 mm**
- **R** → Radial construction
- **15** → Rim diameter = **15 inch** = 381 mm
- **91** → Load Index; per TRA tables, 91 = **615 kg** maximum load per tyre
- **H** → Speed Symbol H = **210 km/h** maximum speed

**Overall diameter** = $2(126.75) + 381 = 634.5$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> Examiner Pitfall: Students often forget to convert **inches to millimetres** when calculating overall diameter, leading to dimensional mismatch and **deduction of 1 full mark**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the three principal rejection criteria for an automotive tyre and briefly justify each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cuts exposing ply cords** — Once the steel/rayon/nylon cords beneath the rubber are exposed, moisture ingress leads to corrosion and belt separation, culminating in a high-speed blowout.
2. **Sidewall bulges** — A bulge indicates one or more broken inner ply cords; the structural continuity is lost and the tyre is on the verge of rupture under inflation pressure.
3. **Excessive tread wear (depth below 1.6 mm)** — Worn tread cannot evacuate water efficiently; hydroplaning risk increases, and wet braking distance rises by up to **40 %**.

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU 2024 Scheme permits an internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the following:

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled diagram, explain the **complete procedure of removing a tyre from a wheel rim using a pneumatic tyre changer**. List the safety precautions to be observed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **DOT date code** and the **Uniform Tyre Quality Grading (UTQG)** system. Why is the DOT date considered the "tyre's birth certificate"? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Tools and Setup:**
- Pneumatic tyre changer with **6–10 bar** supply
- Tyre mounting lubricant (tyre paste)
- Valve core remover
- Clip-on air chuck
- Bead-keeping tool

**Step-by-step Procedure:**

1. **Deflate fully** — Remove the valve core, press the tyre against the ground to expel residual air.
2. **Position the wheel** on the turntable, ensuring the **drop-centre of the rim** faces upward.
3. **Clamp the rim** with the self-centring jaws (external or internal based on rim type). Tighten to specification.
4. **Apply tyre paste** generously on both upper and lower beads at the contact zone with the rim flange.
5. **Activate the bead breaker** — Place the blade against the sidewall, ~5 cm from the rim edge. Press the foot pedal; the blade presses the sidewall inward, breaking the bead from the flange. Repeat on the **opposite side** for the second bead.
6. **Mount the demounting head** on the slide arm. Insert the head between the rim edge and the bead.
7. **Rotate the turntable clockwise** while holding the lever — the bead peels up over the rim flange.
8. **Repeat for the second bead** by flipping the mounting head and rotating.
9. **Lubricate the rim flange** before any remounting.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Wear **safety glasses** — exploding rims can launch fragments.
- **Stand aside** during bead-seating inflation bursts.
- Never inflate beyond **3.5 bar** without a safety cage.
- Inspect rim for **cracks or corrosion** before reuse.
- Keep hands clear of moving turntable.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**DOT Date Code:**
The DOT (Department of Transportation, USA) code is a mandatory marking on tyres sold in the USA and is widely followed worldwide. The trailing **4 digits** encode the week and year of manufacture.

Example: **DOT X9JH 0224** = manufactured in **Week 02 of 2024**.

The **DOT is the tyre's "birth certificate"** because:
- It establishes **age**, critical because rubber oxidizes over time even when unused.
- Industry consensus (and many KTU-aligned syllabi) recommend **replacement after 5 years from DOT date**, regardless of tread depth.
- Used tyres without a legible DOT are routinely **refused registration**.

**UTQG System:**
The Uniform Tyre Quality Grading is a comparative rating mandated by US NHTSA:
- **Treadwear** — e.g., 400 (relative to a control of 100). Higher is longer life.
- **Traction** — grades **AA, A, B, C** for wet braking; AA is best.
- **Temperature** — grades **A, B, C** for heat resistance at sustained speed; A is best.

UTQG does **not** test sidewall durability, noise, or rolling resistance.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** List and describe any **four tyre inspection criteria** a workshop technician must check before declaring a tyre fit for service. State the legal tread depth limit in India. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With reference to a pneumatic tyre changer, explain **bead breaking, bead lubrication, and bead seating**. State the typical air pressure used for bead seating. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Four Inspection Criteria:**

1. **Tread Depth Measurement** — Use a tread depth gauge at the centre, inner, and outer grooves across the full circumference. Replace if any reading is below **1.6 mm** (the legal minimum in India per CMVR). Always cross-check against the **TWI bars**.

2. **Sidewall Examination for Cuts and Bulges** — Run a hand along the sidewall (with gloves) to detect any **bulges or raised areas**. Bulges indicate broken ply cords and necessitate **immediate rejection**, irrespective of tread depth. Cuts deeper than **3 mm** or cuts exposing cords are also grounds for rejection.

3. **DOT Date Verification** — Read the last four digits of the DOT code. Tyres aged over **5 years** should be replaced due to rubber degradation, even if tread is fresh.

4. **Bead Inspection** — Examine both inner and outer beads for **rust, deformation, or wire exposure**. A damaged bead cannot maintain an airtight seal against the rim flange and is unsafe.

**(Additional checks can include valve condition, balance weights, alignment of wear patterns.)**

**Legal tread depth limit in India:** **1.6 mm** across the central three-quarters of the tread, around the full circumference (Central Motor Vehicles Rules 91 & 92).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Bead Breaking:**
Bead breaking is the **first mechanical step** of demounting. A pneumatic bead-breaker blade is pressed against the sidewall of the inflated-tyre (now deflated) at a point near the rim flange. Compressed air at 6–10 bar drives a piston that pushes the blade with several thousand newtons of force. The bead, which was sealed against the flange by friction and air pressure differential, is **popped inward**, breaking the seal. The operation is repeated on the **opposite side** for the second bead.

**Bead Lubrication:**
Before demounting **and before mounting**, a water-based or vegetable-oil-based **tyre mounting lubricant** (tyre paste) is liberally applied to:
- The **rim flange**, to allow the bead to slide over without chafing.
- The **tyre bead and lower sidewall**, to reduce friction.

Lubricant must be **non-petroleum** based, as petroleum attacks natural rubber and can degrade the bead.

**Bead Seating:**
After remounting, the tyre is inflated to **seat the bead** against the rim flange. A sudden air burst (~**2.5 to 3.5 bar**) is delivered via a clip-on air chuck into the cavity between the rim and the bead. The expanding air pushes the bead outward, snapping it into place against the flange with an audible "pop". The operator then deflates to the **recommended service pressure** (printed on the sidewall, e.g., "2.3 bar / 33 psi").

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Many students write "bead seating pressure = 35 psi" — they confuse **bar** and **psi** units. **1 bar ≈ 14.5 psi**. The examiner will deduct a mark for the wrong unit conversion.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **No diagram** — 2 marks reserved for a labelled pneumatic-tyre-changer sketch. Always draw one, even if rough.
> 2. **Mixing up units** (inches ↔ mm, bar ↔ psi) — silent killer of marks.
> 3. **Omitting safety precautions** — examiners specifically look for at least 2 PPE/safety points; missing them costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the legal limit (1.6 mm)** — quoting the wrong tread depth shows lack of CMVR knowledge.
> 5. **Not mentioning the DOT** during inspection — examiners expect the DOT date code check as part of any responsible service.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **tyre code** encodes: Service type, Section Width, Aspect Ratio, Construction (R/D/B), Rim diameter, Load Index, Speed Symbol, DOT date, UTQG.
- ✅ **Aspect Ratio** is a **percentage**, not a ratio in absolute units — sidewall height = SW × AR / 100.
- ✅ **Rim diameter is in inches**, but most other dimensions are in **millimetres** — always convert (× 25.4).
- ✅ **Three rejection criteria**: cuts exposing cords, sidewall bulges, tread depth below **1.6 mm**.
- ✅ **DOT date** = last 4 digits of the DOT marking = week + year of manufacture. Tyres older than **5 years** are typically rejected.
- ✅ **Pneumatic tyre changer** operates on **6–10 bar** compressed air supply.
- ✅ **Bead seating pressure** is **2.5–3.5 bar** — never exceed without a safety cage.
- ✅ **Lubricant** must be **non-petroleum** based to prevent rubber degradation.
- ✅ **PPE** during demounting: safety glasses, gloves, closed footwear.
- ✅ **UTQG** grades (Treadwear, Traction AA-A-B-C, Temperature A-B-C) apply to US-market tyres.
- ✅ **TWI** (Tread Wear Indicator) bars appear as small bridges across the grooves when tread wears to **~1.6 mm**.
- ✅ **Resetting** the tyre changer includes: deflating lines, retracting mount head, opening jaws, lowering column, locking turntable.

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Tyre Codes, Tyre Removing, Inspection & Pneumatic Tyre Changer**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education*
