# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 scheme, Part A questions are typically **2-mark conceptual questions with a 1-mark sketch/diagram element**, totalling **3 marks**. They are compulsory, no choice, and require concise answers of **30–60 words** plus a small labelled diagram where applicable.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a helper spring. Why is it used in conjunction with a main spring in commercial vehicle suspensions?**

**Model Answer (≈ 50 words):**
A helper (auxiliary) spring is a short leaf spring placed on top of the main leaf spring pack. It remains inactive under light vehicle load and engages only when the load exceeds a threshold, increasing the effective spring rate and preventing bottoming-out. This gives a progressive (variable) spring rate, improving both ride comfort and load-carrying capacity.

> **[Diagram element — 1 mark]:** A small labelled sketch showing the main spring, helper spring, and the gap between them, with the engagement direction indicated by an arrow.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List any six components of a leaf spring assembly and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form):**

| Component | Function |
|:---|:---|
| Master Leaf | Carries the primary bending load; has the spring eye at both ends. |
| Center Bolt | Locates the spring pack centrally on the axle housing. |
| U-Bolt | Clamps the spring assembly to the axle seat. |
| Spring Eye Bushing | Provides a low-friction pivot between the eye and the shackle pin. |
| Rebound Clip | Prevents leaf separation during the rebound stroke. |
| Shackle and Pin | Allows longitudinal length change of the spring during deflection. |

> **[Marks split — 2 + 1]:** 2 marks for any four correct components and functions, 1 mark for the additional two.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions are **14 marks each** and offer an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Each long-answer question is split into two sub-parts: typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, although the split may be **(a) 8 + (b) 6** or **(a) 6 + (b) 8** depending on the question. Sub-parts usually carry a 50/50 weightage of theory and diagram/derivation.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q. A (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a main and helper leaf spring assembly used in a commercial vehicle. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A main + helper leaf spring assembly consists of two functionally independent spring packs that operate in series mechanically but combine in parallel once a load threshold is reached. The **main spring** is the longer, primary pack bolted to the chassis through a front spring eye and a rear shackle. It carries the unladen weight of the vehicle. The **helper spring** is a shorter pack placed above the main pack, clamped at the center bolt but free at both ends. The free ends rest on **rebound clips** bolted to the master leaf, maintaining a small **air gap (10–25 mm)** when the vehicle is empty.

**Working principle:** Under light load, only the main spring deflects; the helper remains disengaged. As the load increases, the main spring compresses until the gap closes, after which the helper comes into contact and shares the load. The effective spring rate jumps from $k_m$ to $k_m + k_h$, providing a **progressive (variable) spring rate** that combines ride comfort with load capacity.

**Sketch (must be drawn in the answer script):**
```
   Chassis Frame
   ____________________
   | shackle |    eye  |
   o=========O=========o        <-- Front eye
   [=================]         <-- Master leaf
   [================]          <-- 2nd leaf
   [===============]           <-- 3rd leaf
   [  Helper leaves  ]         <-- Helper pack (with gap above main)
   [================]          <-- Bottom leaf
        | |                <-- Center bolt
       (U-bolt)
        | |
      [Axle housing]
```

**Marks distribution (7):**
- 2 marks — Neat labelled sketch.
- 2 marks — Construction details (parts, materials, geometry).
- 2 marks — Working principle (gap, engagement, progressive rate).
- 1 mark — Application note (commercial vehicles, load range).

**Q. A (b)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for disassembling a main and helper spring assembly from a vehicle in the workshop. What safety precautions must be observed? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step Procedure):**

1. **Preparation:** Park the vehicle on level ground, chock the wheels, and engage the parking brake. Wear PPE (safety goggles, gloves, steel-toe shoes).
2. **Lift and support:** Loosen the wheel lug nuts, lift the vehicle using a hydraulic jack, and support the axle on **axle stands rated for the vehicle's weight**. Never work under a vehicle supported only by a jack.
3. **Mark reference position:** Using a marker or chalk, mark the relative position of the spring, U-bolt, and axle seat. This helps in reassembly alignment.
4. **Disconnect the shackle:** Remove the shackle pin nut and the pin itself. Use a pin punch if seized. The spring eye end is now free at the rear.
5. **Remove U-bolts:** Loosen the U-bolt nuts in a **criss-cross (star) pattern** using a calibrated torque wrench (set to reverse). Remove the top plate and the U-bolts.
6. **Lower the spring assembly:** Carefully lower the complete spring assembly from the chassis. Use an assistant if the spring is heavy (>20 kg).
7. **Bench disassembly:** Clamp the spring in a **spring-compression vise (or use a C-clamp fixture)** to relieve leaf tension. Mark each leaf with a numbered tag for reassembly order.
8. **Remove center bolt:** Use a socket wrench to remove the center bolt nut and pull out the bolt.
9. **Separate leaves:** Carefully separate the leaves, removing the interleaf liners and rebound clips. Lay them in sequence on a clean bench.
10. **Press out the eye bushings:** Use a hydraulic press (NOT a hammer) with a correctly sized mandrel to press out worn bushings.
11. **Cleaning:** Wash all parts in **kerosene** to remove dirt and old grease. Dry with compressed air.
12. **Inspection and measurement:** Inspect every leaf for cracks (using **dye-penetrant test** or **magnetic particle inspection**), measure thicknesses, eye bore ovality, leaf camber. Categorise parts as **serviceable** or **replace**.

**Safety Precautions (3 key points for full marks):**
- Never work under a vehicle supported only by a jack — always use axle stands.
- Wear PPE (goggles, gloves, apron) to protect against kerosene splashes and sharp spring edges.
- Use the **correct tool** for U-bolt nut loosening; do not use extension pipes (cheater bars) as they can break the tool and injure the operator.
- Springs under compression store a large amount of **strain energy**; release clamping slowly to avoid the spring pack flying apart.
- Dispose of kerosene and used oil in designated waste containers — no open flames nearby.

**Marks distribution (7):**
- 4 marks — Correct sequence of 10–12 procedural steps.
- 2 marks — Safety precautions (at least 3 distinct points).
- 1 mark — Tools/equipment mentioned (torque wrench, hydraulic press, vise, feeler gauge, etc.).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q. B (a)** Derive the expression for the maximum bending stress and the deflection of a leaf spring with **n** leaves, each of width **b** and thickness **t**, loaded centrally by a force **W**, with the spring of half-length **L** and Young's modulus **E**. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider the leaf spring as a simply supported beam of span $2L$ with a central point load $W$. The beam has a rectangular cross-section $b \times t$.

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment:** For a simply supported beam with central load:

$$M_{max} = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Section modulus of a rectangular cross-section:**

$$Z = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)} = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}$$

**Step 4 — Bending stress per leaf (load shared equally):** With $n$ leaves, the load per leaf is $W/n$:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z \cdot n} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{W \cdot L}{2}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}\right) \cdot n} = \dfrac{6 \cdot W \cdot L}{2 \cdot n \cdot b \cdot t^{2}} = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Deflection of a simply supported beam at the center:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{W \cdot (2L)^{3}}{48 \cdot E \cdot I} = \dfrac{W \cdot 8 \cdot L^{3}}{48 \cdot E \cdot I} = \dfrac{W \cdot L^{3}}{6 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12}$ and account for n leaves:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{W \cdot L^{3}}{6 \cdot E \cdot \left(\dfrac{b \cdot t^{3}}{12}\right) \cdot n} = \dfrac{2 \cdot W \cdot L^{3}}{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = \dfrac{2 \cdot W \cdot L^{3}}{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^{3}}}$$

**Step 7 — Spring rate:**

$$k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^{3}}{2 \cdot L^{3}}$$

**Marks distribution (7):**
- 2 marks — Correct setup of the beam analogy and bending moment.
- 2 marks — Derivation of bending stress with all intermediate steps shown.
- 2 marks — Derivation of deflection with section modulus substitution.
- 1 mark — Final boxed expressions clearly stated.

**Q. B (b)** A leaf spring of a light truck has the following specifications: number of leaves $n = 8$, width $b = 65$ mm, thickness $t = 9$ mm, half-length $L = 600$ mm, and Young's modulus $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa. The corner weight is $W = 9,\!000$ N. Calculate (i) the maximum bending stress, and (ii) the deflection of the spring. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Given data:**
- $n = 8$ leaves
- $b = 65 \text{ mm} = 0.065$ m
- $t = 9 \text{ mm} = 0.009$ m
- $L = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6$ m
- $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa $= 2.0 \times 10^{11}$ N/m²
- $W = 9,\!000$ N

**(i) Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 \cdot W \cdot L}{n \cdot b \cdot t^{2}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 \cdot 9,\!000 \cdot 0.6}{8 \cdot 0.065 \cdot (0.009)^{2}}$$

Calculate numerator: $3 \cdot 9,\!000 \cdot 0.6 = 16,\!200$ N·m.

Calculate denominator: $8 \cdot 0.065 \cdot 0.000081 = 8 \cdot 0.065 \cdot 8.1 \times 10^{-5} = 0.52 \cdot 8.1 \times 10^{-5} = 4.212 \times 10^{-5}$ m³.

Therefore:

$$\sigma_b = \dfrac{16,\!200}{4.212 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.846 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}^{2} \approx 384.6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_b \approx 385 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Sanity check:** The allowable stress for spring steel (EN45, 60Si7) is typically **450–550 MPa**. A calculated value of 385 MPa is **within the safe limit**, with a factor of safety of approximately 1.2. ✓

**(ii) Deflection:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{2 \cdot W \cdot L^{3}}{n \cdot E \cdot b \cdot t^{3}}$$

Substituting:

$$\delta = \dfrac{2 \cdot 9,\!000 \cdot (0.6)^{3}}{8 \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{11}) \cdot 0.065 \cdot (0.009)^{3}}$$

Calculate numerator: $2 \cdot 9,\!000 \cdot 0.216 = 3,\!888$ N·m³.

Calculate denominator:
- $8 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{11} = 1.6 \times 10^{12}$
- $0.065 \cdot (0.009)^{3} = 0.065 \cdot 7.29 \times 10^{-7} = 4.7385 \times 10^{-8}$
- Product: $1.6 \times 10^{12} \cdot 4.7385 \times 10^{-8} = 7.5816 \times 10^{4}$ N·m²

Therefore:

$$\delta = \dfrac{3,\!888}{7.5816 \times 10^{4}} = 0.0513 \text{ m} \approx 51.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 51 \text{ mm}}$$

**Sanity check:** A static deflection of ~51 mm under load is realistic for a light commercial vehicle leaf spring. Total suspension travel (including dynamic) is typically 80–120 mm, so this corner-weight deflection is sensible. ✓

**Marks distribution (7):**
- 2 marks — Correct data substitution and stress formula.
- 2 marks — Numerical evaluation of stress to ~385 MPa.
- 1 mark — Sanity check / interpretation of stress value.
- 1 mark — Deflection calculation.
- 1 mark — Deflection interpretation / sanity check.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to share the load among n leaves.** Many students write $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 W L}{b t^{2}}$ (single leaf) instead of $\dfrac{3 W L}{n b t^{2}}$. This single error costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in numericals.** Mixing mm and m in the same equation. **Always convert all lengths to metres** before substituting into the formula. The examiner will deduct **1 mark** for unit error.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps.** KTU examiners reward step-by-step working. Do not jump from the formula directly to the final number — show the numerator, denominator, and the unit cancellation explicitly.
> 4. **In the lab record, omitting the safety precautions section.** Lab records without a clearly headed "Safety Precautions" section are marked down by **at least 2 marks** in the **Lab Continuous Assessment (LCA)** component.
> 5. **Calling the rebound clip a "rebound spring".** It is a **clip**, not a spring. Use the correct term.
> 6. **Confusing the master leaf with the helper leaf.** The master leaf is the **longest leaf in the main pack**. The helper leaves are **always shorter** than even the shortest main leaf.
> 7. **Not mentioning the torquing sequence (star pattern).** U-bolt nuts must be tightened in a star pattern, not in a sequence around the U-bolt. Failing to mention this loses **1 mark** in the lab viva.
> 8. **Forgetting to release spring tension before disassembly.** Always clamp the spring in a vise or use a leaf-spring compressor before dismantling. **A spring pack under load can release violently and cause serious injury.**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

Use this section as your **last-10-minutes revision sheet** before entering the KTU exam hall or the lab viva.

- ✅ A **leaf spring** is a simple, rugged suspension spring made of stacked steel leaves; widely used in commercial vehicles.
- ✅ The **main spring** carries the static (unladen) weight of the vehicle.
- ✅ The **helper spring** is shorter, sits on top of the main pack, and engages only under heavy load.
- ✅ Together, the main + helper form a **progressive (variable-rate) spring** — soft initially, stiff after engagement.
- ✅ The **engagement gap** between the helper free end and the main leaf is typically **10–25 mm** when the vehicle is unladen.
- ✅ **Key components to remember:** master leaf, gradient leaves, helper leaves, center bolt, U-bolts, rebound clips, spring eye bushing, shackle and pin, interleaf liners, axle seat.
- ✅ **Bending stress** in an n-leaf spring: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{3 W L}{n b t^{2}}$ — load is shared equally among n leaves.
- ✅ **Deflection** of an n-leaf spring: $\delta = \dfrac{2 W L^{3}}{n E b t^{3}}$.
- ✅ **Spring rate** of an n-leaf spring: $k = \dfrac{n E b t^{3}}{2 L^{3}}$ — proportional to $t^{3}$ and inversely proportional to $L^{3}$.
- ✅ **Young's modulus of spring steel (EN45/60Si7/SUP9):** $E \approx 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ MPa.
- ✅ **Equivalent spring rate after helper engagement:** $k_{eq} = k_m + k_h$ (parallel combination).
- ✅ **Load sharing after engagement:** $W_m = \dfrac{k_m}{k_m + k_h} \cdot W$ and $W_h = \dfrac{k_h}{k_m + k_h} \cdot W$.
- ✅ **U-bolt tightening:** must be done in a **star (criss-cross) pattern** to OEM-specified torque (typically **70–110 N·m** for passenger cars, **200–450 N·m** for LCV/HCV).
- ✅ **Disassembly steps:** jack → axle stands → mark position → remove shackle pin → loosen U-bolts (star pattern) → lower spring → clamp in vise → remove center bolt → tag leaves → separate → press out bushings → clean → inspect.
- ✅ **Inspection checks:** leaf thickness (micrometer), eye bushing ovality (dial bore gauge, max **0.5 mm** ovality), center bolt straightness, rebound clip tightness, leaf camber/sag.
- ✅ **Safety rules:** axle stands mandatory, PPE mandatory, kerosene handled away from flames, strain energy of spring released gradually, hydraulic press (not hammer) for bushing removal.
- ✅ **Inspection methods for cracks:** **visual, dye-penetrant (DPT), magnetic particle (MPI)** — visual alone is insufficient for safety-critical components.
- ✅ **Materials of leaves:** **EN45, 60Si7, 50CrV4, SUP9** — all are high-carbon, silicon-manganese, deep-hardenable spring steels.
- ✅ **Always convert all dimensions to SI units (metres, Newtons)** before substituting into the formula. **Never mix units.**
- ✅ **KTU lab viva favourites:** (1) Why is the helper spring shorter than the main spring? (2) What is the function of the rebound clip? (3) Why is the center bolt also called the "king pin of the spring"? (4) What is the purpose of the interleaf liner? (5) Why is the spring eye bushing made of bronze or rubber and not steel?
- ✅ **Practical tip for the lab record:** Always include a **photograph or neat freehand sketch** of the disassembled leaf stack in the correct order — this fetches easy **1–2 marks** in the continuous assessment.

> **End of Notes — Good luck with your KTU 2024 examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dismantle and Assemble Constant Velocity Joint; Examine the Slip Joint and Universal Joint in Propeller Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Dismantle and Assemble Constant Velocity Joint; Examine the Slip Joint and Universal Joint in Propeller Shaft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Propeller Shaft (Drive Shaft) — The Power Highway

The **propeller shaft** (also called a **drive shaft**) is a rotating tubular steel member that transmits **torque from the transmission to the differential** in rear-wheel-drive (RWD), all-wheel-drive (AWD), and four-wheel-drive (4WD) vehicles. In front-wheel-drive (FWD) and independent rear suspension (IRS) layouts, two short shafts (called **half-shafts**) are used and they commonly employ **Constant Velocity (CV) joints** at both ends.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The student is expected to *dismantle* a CV joint, *identify* each sub-component, *re-assemble* it to working condition, and *examine* the function of a slip joint and a universal joint present in a typical propeller shaft.

To accomplish its job, the propeller shaft must simultaneously handle three geometric challenges:

1. **Angular misalignment** between the gearbox output and the differential input → solved by **Universal Joints (U-joints)** or **CV joints**.
2. **Length variation** as the rear axle moves up and down on the suspension → solved by a **Slip Joint (Splined Sleeve)**.
3. **Dynamic vibrations** caused by length and angle changes → damped by **center bearings** and tuned tubing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three core components of a propeller shaft assembly (KTU viva favourite):**
> 1. **Universal Joints** — one at each end of the shaft (tube yoke end and pinion yoke end).
> 2. **Slip Joint** — a splined section that allows telescopic length change.
> 3. **Tube Shaft** — the hollow torsion member connecting both ends.

---

### 1.2 The Universal Joint (Hooke / Cardan Joint)

**Formal Definition:** A *Universal Joint* (U-joint) is a **four-bar, single-degree-of-freedom mechanical linkage** that transmits rotary motion between two shafts whose axes intersect but are not collinear. The most common form is the **Hooke (Cardan) joint**, which uses two yokes connected by a **cross (spider)** with four needle-roller trunnion bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation:** A single Hooke joint produces a *non-constant* output angular velocity when operated at an angle other than zero. Two U-joints, correctly phased, are required to *cancel* this fluctuation.

**Real-World Analogy — The Wrist Joint:** Think of the universal joint as your **wrist**. Your forearm (input shaft) and your hand (output shaft) can bend in any direction up to ~20–25°, but the rotation of the hand is not 1:1 with the rotation of the forearm when bent. Just like a wrist bent at an angle, the motion is *transmitted but not constant*.

---

### 1.3 The Constant Velocity (CV) Joint

**Formal Definition:** A *Constant Velocity Joint* is a precision-engineered coupling that transmits torque from one shaft to another at a **constant angular velocity ratio (1 : 1)** irrespective of the operating angle between the input and output shafts.

Two principal families used in modern automobiles:

| CV Joint Family | Typical Use |
|---|---|
| **Rzeppa (Ball-type) Joint** | Outboard (wheel-side) joint of FWD half-shafts |
| **Tripod (Tripot) Joint** | Inboard (transmission-side) joint of FWD half-shafts |
| **Double-Offset Joint** | Outboard joints requiring high plunge stroke |
| **Cross-Groove Joint** | Inboard joints with larger angular range |

**Real-World Analogy — The Human Shoulder:** The Rzeppa joint behaves like a **ball-and-socket shoulder joint**. The balls sit in curved grooves that are machined such that the *bisector of the angle* between the two shafts always passes through the contact points of the balls. This geometric trick is what makes the velocity constant — the input and output "feel" the same rotational speed no matter how the joint is bent.

---

### 1.4 The Slip Joint (Splined Coupling)

**Formal Definition:** A *Slip Joint* is a **telescoping splined connection** that allows the propeller shaft to change its effective length axially, thereby accommodating the relative movement between the gearbox (fixed to the chassis) and the differential (mounted on the suspended rear axle).

The two mating components are:
- An **external spline** machined on the slip yoke shaft.
- An **internal spline** broached inside the tube yoke (or vice versa).
- A **grease seal / dust cover** to retain lubricant and exclude contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The slip joint is **not** a torque-transmitting joint by itself; it shares the torque path *with* the universal joint at its end. Its sole purpose is **axial (length) compensation**.

**Real-World Analogy — The Telescopic Ladder:** A slip joint works exactly like the **telescoping section of a roller ladder** or a *retractable pen*. The inner tube slides inside the outer tube, lengthening or shortening the assembly while still keeping it rigid in torsion.

---

### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bold Highlighted)

- **Maximum permissible U-joint working angle:** typically **15°–20°** per joint (combined max ~30°).
- **Rzeppa joint maximum operating angle:** **42°–48°**.
- **Tripod joint maximum operating angle:** **24°–26°**.
- **Slip joint axial travel:** typically **25 mm to 50 mm** depending on vehicle.
- **Number of balls in a Rzeppa joint:** usually **6** (sometimes 8) balls.
- **Grease quantity per CV joint:** around **80 g to 120 g** of molybdenum-disulphide (MoS₂) or lithium-based CV grease.
- **Standard propeller shaft balance grade:** **G 6.3** at the running speed (per ISO 1940-1).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Hooke (Universal) Joint

The Hooke joint consists of two **yokes** (each a forked piece) joined by a **cross (spider)** having four trunnions. Each trunnion carries a **needle-roller bearing** and is retained by a **circlip / snap ring**.

**Why a single U-joint cannot give constant velocity:**
- When the input shaft rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega_1$, the cross spider rotates at the *same* angular velocity only when viewed in its own plane.
- However, the *projection* of this motion onto the output yoke's plane changes as the input rotates.
- This causes the **output shaft to speed up and slow down twice per revolution**, with the magnitude of variation proportional to the square of the operating angle $\alpha$.

**Why two U-joints in series (properly phased) give constant velocity:**
- The two joints are designed such that their yokes lie in the **same plane**.
- Both joints experience the *same* angle $\alpha$ because the center tube is short and parallel.
- The *velocity fluctuation* produced by the first joint is exactly **equal in magnitude and opposite in phase** to that of the second joint, and they cancel.

---

### 2.2 Working Principle — Rzeppa CV Joint

The Rzeppa joint comprises:
- An **inner race (ball hub)** with six curved grooves.
- An **outer race (bell housing)** with six matching curved grooves.
- **Six steel balls** seated in the paired grooves.
- A **retaining cage** that keeps the balls aligned in a single plane.
- A **rubber / TPE boot** clamped at both ends, retaining grease and excluding dirt.

**Geometric "Constant Velocity" Trick:**
For each ball, the line joining the two contact points on the inner and outer grooves always lies in the **plane that bisects the angle between the two shafts**. Because of this, the velocity components along the ball-contact line are equal for both shafts, and therefore the *tangential speeds* match, yielding a **1:1 angular velocity ratio** at every instant.

---

### 2.3 Working Principle — Tripod CV Joint

The tripod joint uses a **three-pronged spider (tripod)** with three trunnion rollers sliding in three longitudinal tracks inside a **bell-shaped outer race**. It is **plunging (sliding)** as well as articulating, which is why it is normally used on the **inboard side** where large axial plunge is required as the suspension moves.

---

### 2.4 Working Principle — Slip Joint

The slip joint is purely an **axial (linear) sliding coupling**. The spline form can be:
- **Straight-sided involute splines** (most common, per ISO 4156).
- **Serrated splines** (older designs).
- **Spline count:** typically **10 to 36 teeth** depending on torque capacity.

The axial force is reacted by the **needle-roller thrust bearing** or a **thrust washer** at the slip yoke end. Lubrication is by **high-pressure grease** packed during assembly.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. Direct 3-mark questions are routinely asked from this set in KTU viva and Part A.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | SI Units | Notes |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|:--|
| 1 | Output velocity ratio of a single U-joint | $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos \alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha \cdot \cos^{2}\phi_1}$ | rad/s | $\alpha$ = joint angle, $\phi_1$ = input shaft rotation angle |
| 2 | Maximum output velocity (single U-joint) | $\omega_{2,max} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\cos \alpha}$ | rad/s | Occurs at $\phi_1 = 0$ or $\pi$ |
| 3 | Minimum output velocity (single U-joint) | $\omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos \alpha$ | rad/s | Occurs at $\phi_1 = \pi/2$ or $3\pi/2$ |
| 4 | Velocity fluctuation (single U-joint) | $\Delta\omega = \omega_{2,max} - \omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cdot \dfrac{\sin^{2}\alpha}{\cos \alpha}$ | rad/s | Two peaks per revolution |
| 5 | Angular twist of propeller shaft under torque | $\theta = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | rad | $G$ = modulus of rigidity, $J$ = polar moment of inertia |
| 6 | Polar moment of inertia (hollow tube) | $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32} \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$ | m⁴ | $D$ = outer diameter, $d$ = inner diameter |
| 7 | Critical speed of rotating shaft | $N_c = \dfrac{30}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\pi^{2}}{L^{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot g}{\rho \cdot A}}$ | rpm | E = Young's modulus, $\rho$ = density |
| 8 | Tangential velocity of a ball in Rzeppa joint at mid-angle | $v_t = \omega_1 \cdot r_1 = \omega_2 \cdot r_2$ | m/s | The "constant velocity" condition |
| 9 | Slip joint axial load capacity | $F_{axle} = p_{proj} \cdot A_{proj} \cdot n$ | N | $n$ = number of engaged teeth, $A_{proj}$ = projected tooth area |
| 10 | Equivalent bending angle in two U-joint system | $\alpha_{eq} = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2$ (if opposite phase) | rad | Must be zero for cancellation |

> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"**C**ompare **C**onstant-velocity joints — *C*onstant ratio = 1, *U*-joint — **U**nsmooth."*

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive drivelines:** RWD/AWD propeller shafts rely on U-joints; FWD half-shafts rely on CV joints.
- **Steering columns:** The collapsible steering shaft uses **two Cardan joints** so the steering wheel stays at a constant height/angle relative to the road.
- **Industrial machinery:** Printing rollers, rolling mills, and robotic arms use CV joints for smooth rotation.
- **Aircraft and ship propulsion:** Universal and CV joints transmit torque across misaligned axes in tail rotors and rudder mechanisms.
- **Agricultural tractors:** The Power Take-Off (PTO) shaft uses a **double U-joint / constant-velocity PTO** to drive implements behind the tractor at uniform speed regardless of angle.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Velocity Ratio of a Single Hooke (Universal) Joint

We need to prove mathematically that a single U-joint does *not* give constant velocity.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Let the input shaft axis be $OX$ and the output shaft axis be $OY$. The two axes intersect at point $O$ at an angle $\alpha$. A point $P$ on the input yoke arm (of length $r$) rotates in the plane $XZ$, where $Z$ is perpendicular to $OX$.

**Step 2 — Express the position of the cross-arm tip in space.**
At input rotation $\phi_1$, the tip of the input yoke arm $P$ has coordinates:

$$ P = \left(\, r\cos\phi_1,\ 0,\  r\sin\phi_1 \,\right) $$

**Step 3 — Express the position of the same point as a tip of the output yoke arm.**
The output yoke arm lies in the plane that contains $OY$ and is rotated by $\phi_2$ about $OY$. After applying the rotation matrix that tilts the plane by angle $\alpha$ about the $Z$-axis, the position of $P$ as seen from the output yoke is:

$$ P = \left(\, r\cos\phi_1 \cos\alpha,\ \ r\cos\phi_1 \sin\alpha,\  r\sin\phi_1 \,\right) $$

**Step 4 — Relate $\phi_2$ (output arm angle) to $\phi_1$ (input arm angle).**
The $Y$-component of $P$ must equal $r \sin\phi_2$ (its projection on the output yoke plane):

$$ r\sin\phi_2 = r\cos\phi_1 \sin\alpha $$

$$ \sin\phi_2 = \cos\phi_1 \sin\alpha $$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to time to get the velocity ratio.**
Differentiating both sides with respect to time (and using $\dot\phi_1 = \omega_1$, $\dot\phi_2 = \omega_2$):

$$ \omega_2 \cos\phi_2 = -\omega_1 \sin\phi_1 \sin\alpha $$

$$ \omega_2 = -\omega_1 \,\dfrac{\sin\phi_1 \sin\alpha}{\cos\phi_2} $$

**Step 6 — Use the identity $\sin^{2}\phi_1 + \sin^{2}\phi_2 = \sin^{2}\alpha$ to eliminate $\phi_2$.**
This is the standard trigonometric identity of the spherical four-bar (which the cross represents). Substituting and simplifying:

$$ \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos \alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha \cdot \cos^{2}\phi_1} $$

> **Result 1 (final velocity ratio):**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos \alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha \cdot \cos^{2}\phi_1}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Find the maximum and minimum of this ratio.**
The denominator is maximum when $\cos^{2}\phi_1 = 1 \Rightarrow \phi_1 = 0$ or $\pi$:

$$ \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\bigg|_{max} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{\cos^{2}\alpha} = \frac{1}{\cos\alpha} $$

The denominator is minimum when $\cos^{2}\phi_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \phi_1 = \pi/2$ or $3\pi/2$:

$$ \frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1}\bigg|_{min} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1} = \cos\alpha $$

> **Result 2 (extremes):**

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_{2,max} = \frac{\omega_1}{\cos\alpha}\quad \text{and} \quad \omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos\alpha\;}
$$

**Step 8 — Velocity fluctuation amplitude:**

$$
\Delta\omega = \omega_{2,max} - \omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \left(\frac{1}{\cos\alpha} - \cos\alpha\right) = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{1 - \cos^{2}\alpha}{\cos\alpha}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\omega = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\sin^{2}\alpha}{\cos\alpha}\;}
$$

**Step 9 — Numerical example (KTU problem standard):**
If $\omega_1 = 1000$ rpm and $\alpha = 10^{\circ}$:

$$
\omega_{2,max} = \frac{1000}{\cos 10^{\circ}} = \frac{1000}{0.9848} = 1015.4 \ \text{rpm}
$$

$$
\omega_{2,min} = 1000 \cdot \cos 10^{\circ} = 1000 \cdot 0.9848 = 984.8 \ \text{rpm}
$$

$$
\Delta\omega = 1015.4 - 984.8 = 30.6 \ \text{rpm} \quad (\approx 3.06\% \text{ fluctuation})
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Constant Velocity Proof of the Rzeppa Joint

**Step 1 — Geometry of contact.**
Consider one ball of the Rzeppa joint. The ball touches the inner-race groove at point $A$ (radius $r_1$ from the inner-shaft axis) and the outer-race groove at point $B$ (radius $r_2$ from the outer-shaft axis). The angle between the two shaft axes is $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Bisector condition.**
The line $AB$ is the *common normal* at the ball-groove contact. By design of the grooves, the line $AB$ always lies **in the plane that bisects the angle** between the two shaft axes.

**Step 3 — Velocity match condition.**
For no slip at either contact, the velocity components of the ball along the line $AB$ must be equal when computed from the inner and outer shaft:

$$ \omega_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot \sin\beta_1 = \omega_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot \sin\beta_2 $$

where $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are the angles between the respective radius vectors ($OA$ and $OB$) and the common normal $AB$.

**Step 4 — Apply the bisector condition.**
Because the bisector plane passes through $AB$, the two angles satisfy $\beta_1 = \beta_2 = \beta$. Therefore:

$$ \omega_1 \cdot r_1 \cdot \sin\beta = \omega_2 \cdot r_2 \cdot \sin\beta $$

$$ \omega_1 \cdot r_1 = \omega_2 \cdot r_2 $$

**Step 5 — Design constraint.**
Rzeppa joints are designed with $r_1 = r_2$ (i.e., the contact circles are equal radius). Hence:

$$
\boxed{\;\omega_1 = \omega_2 \quad \text{(constant velocity, independent of } \alpha \text{)}\;}
$$

> **Conclusion:** As long as the inner and outer contact-circle radii are equal and the bisector-plane condition is satisfied, the angular velocity ratio is *exactly* 1, regardless of joint angle $\alpha$. This is the *defining* feature of all CV joints.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Angular Twist of a Hollow Propeller Shaft

**Given:** Length $L$ (m), torque $T$ (N·m), outer diameter $D$ (m), inner diameter $d$ (m), modulus of rigidity $G$ (Pa, $\approx 80 \times 10^{9}$ for steel).

**Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia for a hollow circular cross-section:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the torsion formula $\theta = TL/(GJ)$:**

$$
\boxed{\;\theta = \frac{32\,T\,L}{\pi\,G\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}\;}\quad \text{radians}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical check (KTU practice):**
A steel propeller shaft, $L = 1.5$ m, $D = 80$ mm, $d = 70$ mm, transmits $T = 500$ N·m. Find the angular twist.

Compute $D^{4} - d^{4} = (0.08)^{4} - (0.07)^{4}$:

$$
(0.08)^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
(0.07)^{4} = 2.401 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
D^{4} - d^{4} = 1.695 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^{4}
$$

Now:

$$
\theta = \frac{32 \cdot 500 \cdot 1.5}{\pi \cdot 80 \times 10^{9} \cdot 1.695 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{24{,}000}{4.260 \times 10^{6}} = 5.63 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{rad} \approx 0.323^{\circ}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A twist of less than 0.5° at full load is acceptable; the propeller shaft is rigid enough to deliver torque without significant lag.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU Viva

| Parameter | Hooke (Universal) Joint | Rzeppa CV Joint | Tripod CV Joint | Slip Joint |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Velocity ratio | Variable, $1/\cos\alpha$ to $\cos\alpha$ | **Constant = 1** | **Constant = 1** | N/A (transmits via U/CV) |
| Max operating angle | 15°–20° | 42°–48° | 24°–26° | N/A (linear) |
| Plunging ability | None | Limited (uses double-offset) | **Excellent** | **Excellent** (primary function) |
| Typical location | Propeller shaft ends | Outboard of FWD half-shaft | Inboard of FWD half-shaft | One end of propeller shaft |
| Number of contact points | 4 trunnions | 6 (or 8) balls | 3 rollers | Spline teeth |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium | Low |
| Lubricant | Grease | CV joint grease (MoS₂) | CV joint grease | High-pressure grease |
| Failure mode | Needle-roller pitting | Torn boot → grease loss | Spalling of rollers | Spline wear, teeth chipping |
| Common car example | Most RWD propeller shafts | Toyota, Honda, Maruti half-shafts | Maruti Swift, Hyundai i20 inner joint | Tata, Mahindra RWD SUVs |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Propeller Shaft Assembly

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional block architecture** of a typical RWD propeller shaft assembly and the interaction between its three sub-systems (Universal Joint × 2, Slip Joint, Tube Shaft).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transmission Output Shaft] --> B[Universal Joint - Front - Slip Yoke Side]
    B --> C[Slip Joint - Splined Telescopic Section]
    C --> D[Tube Shaft - Hollow Torsion Member]
    D --> E[Universal Joint - Rear - Flange Yoke Side]
    E --> F[Pinion Flange of Differential]

    subgraph SJ[Slip Joint Module]
        SJ1[External Splines on Slip Yoke]
        SJ2[Internal Splines in Tube Yoke]
        SJ3[Grease Seal and Dust Cover]
    end

    subgraph UJF[Front U-Joint Module]
        UJF1[Slip Yoke Fork]
        UJF2[Cross Spider with 4 Needle Bearings]
        UJF3[Circlips and Retainers]
    end

    subgraph UJR[Rear U-Joint Module]
        UJR1[Tube Yoke Fork]
        UJR2[Cross Spider with 4 Needle Bearings]
        UJR3[Grease Nipple]
    end

    B -.is composed of.-> UJF
    C -.is composed of.-> SJ
    E -.is composed of.-> UJR
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows (A → F) trace the *torque path* from gearbox to differential. The three subgraph blocks describe the **internal structure** of each sub-module.

---

### 4.2 Dismantling Procedure Flowchart (Rzeppa CV Joint)

The Mermaid block below gives the **step-by-step logical flow** for dismantling a typical ball-type (Rzeppa) CV joint during the lab experiment. The procedure is divided into **8 logical stages** with decision points.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Place shaft on vice with boot end up] --> S2[Stage 2: Remove boot clamps and slide boot back]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Remove circlip from CV joint stub]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Strike stub sharply with brass drift to dislodge inner race]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Tilt inner race to remove balls one by one]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Mark cage and outer race orientation before removal]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Rotate cage window to align with outer race track and lift out]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Inspect all parts for pitting, brinelling, and cracks]
    S8 --> Q1{All parts reusable?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Clean, regrease and proceed to assembly]
    Q1 -- No --> A2[Replace damaged components before assembly]
```

> **Engineering Note for Lab Record:** Always **mark the relative orientation** of the cage and outer race with a paint marker before dismantling. The Rzeppa joint is a *matched set*; reassembling the cage in the wrong orientation causes binding and premature failure.

---

### 4.3 Assembly Procedure Flowchart (Universal Joint + Slip Joint)

The following diagram outlines the **assembly sequence** for the universal joint and slip joint combination — the propeller-shaft-end side of the lab experiment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Start: Clean all parts with solvent] --> T2[Pack U-joint needle bearings with grease]
    T2 --> T3[Insert one bearing into yoke eye]
    T3 --> T4[Insert cross trunnion through bearing]
    T4 --> T5[Insert second bearing into opposite yoke eye]
    T5 --> T6[Install remaining two bearings on the other yoke fork]
    T6 --> T7[Press and seat all four bearings fully]
    T7 --> T8[Install circlips / snap rings in grooves]
    T8 --> T9[Check joint pivots freely without binding]
    T9 --> T10[For slip joint: align external and internal splines]
    T10 --> T11[Apply grease to splines and slide together]
    T11 --> T12[Install dust cover and seal]
    T12 --> T13[Final check: rotate shaft and observe smooth motion]
```

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Comparison — U-Joint vs CV Joint Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UJ[Hooke Universal Joint]
        UI[Input Shaft at constant omega] --> UC[Cross Spider with 2 yokes at angle alpha]
        UC --> UO[Output Shaft with fluctuating omega]
    end

    subgraph CV[Rzeppa CV Joint]
        CI[Input Shaft at constant omega] --> CB[6 balls in bisecting grooves]
        CB --> CO[Output Shaft with omega exactly equal to input]
    end

    UO -. fluctuation .- U1[Period 2 per revolution: amplitude grows with alpha squared]
    CO -. no fluctuation .- C1[Constant ratio 1 to 1 for all alpha]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**  
> *What is a Constant Velocity (CV) joint? Why is it preferred over a universal (Hooke) joint in the front-wheel-drive half-shafts of a modern car?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Constant Velocity (CV) joint** is a coupling that transmits torque between two shafts at a **uniform angular velocity ratio of 1 : 1**, irrespective of the angle between the shafts (3 marks for definition). It is preferred over the Hooke (universal) joint in FWD half-shafts because:
- A single Hooke joint produces **velocity fluctuations twice per revolution**, leading to vibration, noise, and harshness in the driven wheels.
- A CV joint (e.g., Rzeppa or Tripod) maintains **constant wheel rotational speed**, providing smooth driving, better steering feel, and avoiding torsional vibrations in the half-shaft (additional supporting points).

---

> **Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**  
> *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the function of a slip joint in a propeller shaft.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **slip joint** is a **splined telescopic connection** between the slip yoke and the tube yoke of a propeller shaft. Its **function** is to allow **axial (length-wise) movement** of the propeller shaft so as to accommodate the **vertical movement of the rear axle** relative to the gearbox as the suspension deflects (2 marks). It is not used to transmit torque on its own; torque is transmitted through the universal joint at its end (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Question A.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Regular]**  
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Rzeppa Constant Velocity (CV) joint**. List its applications. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Construction (3 marks):**  
  The Rzeppa joint consists of:
  1. An **inner race (ball hub)** splined to the transmission-side shaft, with six curved grooves machined on its outer spherical surface.
  2. An **outer race (bell)** splined to the wheel-side shaft, with six matching curved grooves on its inner spherical surface.
  3. **Six chrome-steel balls** rolling in the paired grooves, transmitting torque between the two races.
  4. A **cage (retainer)** that holds the balls in a plane and keeps them equally spaced.
  5. A **rubber / TPE boot** with two clamps, retaining grease and sealing out contaminants.

- **Working (3 marks):**  
  The grooves are machined such that the line joining the two contact points of each ball always lies in the **bisecting plane** of the angle between the two shafts. This geometric condition forces the tangential velocity components of the ball to be equal from both sides. Since the contact-circle radii on the inner and outer races are designed equal, the angular velocities of the two shafts are forced to be equal at every instant, giving a **1 : 1 velocity ratio** independent of the joint angle.

- **Applications (1 mark):**  
  Used as the **outboard (wheel-side) joint** of front-wheel-drive half-shafts in passenger cars such as Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta, Honda City; also in independent rear suspension half-shafts and propeller shafts of off-road vehicles.

---

> **(b)** A single Hooke (Universal) joint is operated at an angle of $\alpha = 12^{\circ}$. The input shaft rotates at a constant $1500$ rpm. Determine the **maximum, minimum, and average output speed**, and the **percentage velocity fluctuation**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum output speed** (occurs at $\phi_1 = 0$ or $\pi$):

$$
\omega_{2,max} = \frac{\omega_1}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{1500}{\cos 12^{\circ}} = \frac{1500}{0.9781} = 1533.6 \ \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum output speed** (occurs at $\phi_1 = 90^{\circ}$ or $270^{\circ}$):

$$
\omega_{2,min} = \omega_1 \cos\alpha = 1500 \times 0.9781 = 1467.2 \ \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Average output speed:**

$$
\omega_{2,avg} = \frac{\omega_{2,max} + \omega_{2,min}}{2} = \frac{1533.6 + 1467.2}{2} = 1500.4 \approx 1500 \ \text{rpm}
$$

(Confirms that the mean output equals the input — a known property of the Hooke joint.)

**Step 4 — Velocity fluctuation (absolute):**

$$
\Delta\omega = \omega_{2,max} - \omega_{2,min} = 1533.6 - 1467.2 = 66.4 \ \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage fluctuation:**

$$
\%\ \text{fluctuation} = \frac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_{2,avg}} \times 100 = \frac{66.4}{1500} \times 100 = 4.43\%
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check with the fluctuation formula:**

$$
\Delta\omega = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\sin^{2}\alpha}{\cos\alpha} = 1500 \times \frac{\sin^{2}12^{\circ}}{\cos 12^{\circ}}
$$

$$
= 1500 \times \frac{(0.2079)^{2}}{0.9781} = 1500 \times \frac{0.04322}{0.9781} = 66.3 \ \text{rpm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Final Answer:** $N_{2,max} = 1533.6$ rpm, $N_{2,min} = 1467.2$ rpm, $N_{2,avg} = 1500$ rpm, percentage fluctuation = **4.43 %**.

---

> **Question B.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Regular – Alternative Choice]**  
> **(a)** With a labelled sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Hooke (Cardan) universal joint**. Discuss why a single Hooke joint cannot transmit uniform velocity and how two such joints (in proper phase) solve the problem. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**
- **Construction (2 marks):** Two yokes (forks) — one at each shaft end — joined by a **cross (spider)**. Each of the four arms of the cross carries a **needle-roller bearing** retained by a **circlip** in the eye of the corresponding yoke.
- **Working (2 marks):** When the input shaft rotates, the cross spider is carried around; the trunnion tips describe a circle in the input yoke plane. This rotation is transmitted to the output yoke, whose plane is tilted by angle $\alpha$ relative to the input plane. The component of the spider's rotation in the output plane is what rotates the output shaft.
- **Why velocity fluctuates (2 marks):** Because the projection of the spider's circular motion onto the tilted output plane is not constant. Mathematically, the velocity ratio is $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^{2}\alpha \cos^{2}\phi_1}$, which depends on the input rotation $\phi_1$. The output speeds up and slows down twice per revolution.
- **Two-joint cancellation (1 mark):** When a propeller shaft has two Hooke joints in series, with **yoke forks in the same plane and equal working angles**, the second joint *inverts* the fluctuation produced by the first, and the two fluctuations cancel, leaving the differential input at uniform velocity.

---

> **(b)** A propeller shaft of **outer diameter 75 mm, inner diameter 65 mm, length 1.4 m**, transmits a torque of **400 N·m**. For steel, $G = 80 \times 10^{9}$ N/m². Calculate the **angle of twist** in degrees. Comment on the suitability of the design. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32}\left[(0.075)^{4} - (0.065)^{4}\right]
$$

$$
(0.075)^{4} = 3.164 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
(0.065)^{4} = 1.785 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^{4}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32}(1.379 \times 10^{-5}) = 1.354 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Angle of twist in radians:**

$$
\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{400 \times 1.4}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.354 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{560}{1.083 \times 10^{5}} = 5.17 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{rad}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to degrees:**

$$
\theta_{deg} = \theta \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 5.17 \times 10^{-3} \times 57.296 = 0.296^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**
An angular twist of **0.296°** is well below the typical design limit of 1° per metre of shaft length. The propeller shaft is therefore **adequately rigid** for the transmitted torque and is suitable for the application.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Confusing "constant velocity" with "zero vibration."** A CV joint gives constant *angular velocity* but the *torque* may still pulsate slightly due to friction. State *angular velocity* explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the bisector condition.** When asked *why* a CV joint gives constant velocity, students often say "because of the balls." The real reason is the **bisector-plane geometric condition** plus **equal contact-circle radii**. Both are required.
> 3. **Mixing up the formulae.** $N_{2,max} = N_1/\cos\alpha$ and $N_{2,min} = N_1\cos\alpha$ — examiners often test which is which. A memory trick: "**M**aximum has **m**ore — divide by **c**osine (cosine is small at large angles)."
> 4. **Not marking the cage orientation** during dismantling. In the lab record, the examiner will mark you down for "lost orientation" because the Rzeppa joint is a **matched set** that will bind if reassembled randomly.
> 5. **Writing "slip joint transmits torque"** — it does *not* by itself; the U-joint at that end carries the torque. The slip joint only handles *axial* motion.
> 6. **Unit errors in twist calculations.** Always state the answer in *both* radians and degrees when the question says "comment on the design."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before the lab exam — memorize these 15 points:**

- [x] A **propeller shaft** transmits torque from the transmission to the differential in RWD / AWD layouts.
- [x] A **Universal (Hooke) joint** allows angular misalignment but produces **non-constant output velocity** at any operating angle $\alpha > 0$.
- [x] A **Constant Velocity joint** maintains a **1 : 1 velocity ratio** at all angles; examples: **Rzeppa** (ball-type) and **Tripod**.
- [x] The **slip joint** is a **splined telescopic section** that provides **axial (length) compensation** for suspension movement.
- [x] A propeller shaft uses **two U-joints in series, properly phased**, to cancel velocity fluctuations.
- [x] **Velocity ratio of a single U-joint:** $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\alpha / (1 - \sin^{2}\alpha \cos^{2}\phi_1)$.
- [x] **Maximum and minimum output speeds:** $N_{2,max} = N_1 / \cos\alpha$ and $N_{2,min} = N_1 \cos\alpha$.
- [x] **Bisector condition** + **equal contact-circle radii** = constant velocity in CV joint.
- [x] **Rzeppa joint uses 6 (or 8) balls**; **Tripod uses 3 rollers**.
- [x] **Working angle limits:** U-joint = 15°–20°; Rzeppa = 42°–48°; Tripod = 24°–26°.
- [x] **Slip joint axial travel:** typically 25–50 mm.
- [x] **Angular twist formula:** $\theta = 32 TL / [\pi G (D^{4} - d^{4})]$.
- [x] **Standard propeller shaft lube:** high-pressure grease; **CV joint lube:** MoS₂-based CV grease.
- [x] **Lab safety:** Always use **brass drifts**, never steel, when striking CV joint components.
- [x] **Always mark** the cage and outer-race orientation with a paint marker before dismantling a Rzeppa joint.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Checking the Emission of Gasoline Engine with Exhaust Gas Analyser and Checking the Emission of Diesel Engine with Diesel Smoke Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Checking the Emission of Gasoline Engine with Exhaust Gas Analyser and Checking the Emission of Diesel Engine with Diesel Smoke Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Exhaust Gas Analyser (EGA) — Formal Definition

An **Exhaust Gas Analyser (EGA)** is a precision diagnostic instrument used to quantify the concentration of regulated gaseous pollutants in the exhaust stream of **Spark Ignition (SI) / Gasoline engines**. It is compliant with the Indian Standard **IS 14681:1999** and **AIS-004 (Part 1)** under the **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** regulatory framework.

The EGA measures four primary gaseous species in real-time, expressed in the following standard units:

| Pollutant | Symbol | Unit | Sensor Principle |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Hydrocarbons | **HC** | **ppm** (parts per million) | NDIR (Non-Dispersive Infrared) |
| Carbon Monoxide | **CO** | **%** volume | NDIR |
| Carbon Dioxide | **CO₂** | **%** volume | NDIR |
| Oxygen | **O₂** | **%** volume | Electrochemical (Zirconia / Lead-Air cell) |
| Nitric Oxide (optional) | **NO** | **ppm** | Electrochemical |
| Air-Fuel Equivalence | **λ** (Lambda) | dimensionless | Computed (Brettschneider equation) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Lambda (λ):** The ratio of the actual air-fuel ratio supplied to the engine to the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. λ = 1 indicates chemically perfect combustion, λ > 1 indicates lean mixture, and λ < 1 indicates rich mixture. For gasoline, the **stoichiometric A/F ratio = 14.7 : 1**.

### 1.2 Diesel Smoke Meter (DSM) — Formal Definition

A **Diesel Smoke Meter (DSM)** — also known as an **Opacimeter** — is an opto-electronic instrument designed to measure the **opacity** (light-blocking ability) of diesel exhaust smoke. It is governed by **IS 14682:1999** and the **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) BS-VI testing protocol** for diesel vehicles.

The smoke meter quantifies the **light absorption coefficient (k)** of the exhaust gas stream through an opaque sampling chamber, providing readings in three industry-standard units:

| Unit | Full Form | Range | Convention |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **N** (HSU) | Hartridge Smoke Unit | 0 – 100 | Indian/European convention |
| **S** (BSU) | Bosch Smoke Unit | 0 – 10 | Bosch convention |
| **k (1/m)** | Light Absorption Coefficient | 0 – ∞ | SI fundamental unit |
| **% Opacity** | Percentage Opacity | 0 – 100 % | Visual perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Smoke Meter for Diesel but an EGA for Petrol?** Diesel engines operate in **excess air (always lean, λ > 1.2)** and rarely produce significant CO/HC; instead, they generate **particulate matter (PM)** — visible black smoke — due to heterogeneous fuel-air mixing in the diffusion flame. Petrol engines, however, operate near stoichiometry and produce **gaseous CO, HC, and CO₂** rather than visible smoke. Hence, the two engine families require fundamentally different measurement philosophies: **gaseous spectroscopy for petrol** and **optical extinction for diesel**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are at a **busy restaurant kitchen**:

- The **Exhaust Gas Analyser** is like a **chemistry lab technician** standing at the kitchen exhaust hood, taking air samples into test tubes and using infrared light beams to identify *what gases* are present (CO, HC, CO₂) and *how much*. The chemistry tells you if the chef is using too much gas, leaving food uncooked, or producing harmful fumes.
  
- The **Diesel Smoke Meter** is like a **laser pointer security sensor** mounted across a tunnel of black exhaust smoke. It measures *how much light gets blocked* by the smoke. The thicker and blacker the smoke, the more fuel is being wasted as soot. It doesn't care *what* is in the smoke chemically — only *how dark* it appears to a light beam.

Together, these two instruments allow a KTU lab engineer to verify whether a vehicle's emissions comply with the **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** norms enforced by the Government of India from **April 1, 2020** for all new vehicles.

## 1.4 Why This Lab Experiment Matters in KTU 2024

This experiment directly evaluates the following **Course Outcomes (COs)** of PCMUL308:

- **CO1**: Apply knowledge of measurement techniques used in automotive systems.
- **CO2**: Use appropriate modern tools and instruments for testing vehicles.
- **CO3**: Test and evaluate vehicle subsystems for compliance with standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, students must demonstrate hands-on competence in operating the EGA and DSM, recording readings at prescribed engine conditions (e.g., idle, 2500 rpm, free acceleration), interpreting results against BS-VI limits, and plotting characteristic curves (e.g., Opacity vs. RPM).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Pollutant Formation

The four primary regulated pollutants arise from four distinct chemical pathways during combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel (general formula **CₓHᵧ**):

### 2.1.1 Carbon Monoxide (CO) Formation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Complete Combustion:} \quad & \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \tfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \tfrac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \\[4pt]
\text{Incomplete Combustion:} \quad & \text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(\tfrac{x}{2} + \tfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO} + \tfrac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

CO is the signature of **rich mixtures (λ < 1)** or poor flame quenching. It is colourless, odourless, and **highly toxic** (binds to haemoglobin 200× more avidly than O₂).

### 2.1.2 Hydrocarbons (HC) Formation

Unburned hydrocarbons escape the cylinder due to:
- **Flame quenching** at the cold combustion-chamber walls (the "quench layer", ~0.1 mm thick).
- **Crevice volumes** in piston ring grooves and head-gasket depressions.
- **Wall wetting** by liquid fuel films on cold intake runners (during cold start).

### 2.1.3 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) — The Combustion Indicator

CO₂ is the principal product of *complete* combustion. Its concentration in the exhaust is a direct indicator of **combustion efficiency (η_c)**:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{\text{mass of fuel actually burned}}{\text{mass of fuel supplied}} = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + [\text{HC}]} \times 100\,\%
$$

### 2.1.4 Oxides of Nitrogen (NOₓ) Formation

The **Zeldovich mechanism** describes thermal NOₓ formation at peak in-cylinder temperatures above **1800 K**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{O} + \text{N}_2 & \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N} \quad (\text{Slow, rate-limiting}) \\
\text{N} + \text{O}_2 & \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \\
\text{N} + \text{OH} & \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.2 Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Sensor Principle

The EGA's HC, CO, and CO₂ channels use NDIR detection. Each gas has a unique **infrared absorption fingerprint** in the **2–14 µm wavelength band**.

**Operational logic (5 steps):**

1. **Infrared Source** — A heated filament (~600 °C) emits broadband IR.
2. **Optical Chopper** — A motor-driven chopper wheel modulates the IR beam into pulses at a known frequency (e.g., 200 Hz).
3. **Sample Cell** — The modulated beam passes through a chamber filled with the flowing exhaust sample.
4. **Detector Cell** — A sealed reference cell filled with the *target* gas (e.g., CO) absorbs IR selectively. The differential pressure created by differential heating of the two gas volumes is sensed by a **microphone / capacitive diaphragm**.
5. **Signal Processing** — The detected pressure pulse is synchronously demodulated; its amplitude is proportional to the target gas concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Non-Dispersive"?** Unlike a spectrometer that uses a prism/grating to disperse light into a spectrum, the NDIR uses a **broadband source + selective detector (filled with the target gas itself)**. The detector gas acts as a built-in optical filter. This makes the instrument rugged, fast (response < 3 s), and inexpensive — ideal for in-shop EGA units.

## 2.3 Lambda (λ) Calculation — The Brettschneider Equation

The EGA does not measure λ directly; it is *computed* from measured CO, CO₂, O₂, and HC using the empirical **Brettschneider equation**:

$$
\lambda = \frac{[\text{O}_2] + \tfrac{1}{2}[\text{CO}] + \tfrac{1}{2}[\text{CO}_2] + \sum(\tfrac{n}{4}\text{C}_n\text{H}_m \cdot [\text{HC}])}{\tfrac{1}{2}[\text{CO}] + [\text{CO}_2] + \tfrac{1}{2}\sum(m\cdot \text{C}_n\text{H}_m \cdot [\text{HC}]) - \tfrac{[\text{O}_2]}{2}}
$$

For the simplified case where HC is neglected (or measured as **C₃H₈ equivalent**):

$$
\lambda_{sp} = \frac{[\text{CO}_2] + \tfrac{[\text{CO}]}{2} + [\text{O}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + \tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{[\text{CO}_2]\cdot[\text{CO}]}{[\text{CO}_2]+[\text{CO}]}} \quad \text{(Spindt equation, simplified form)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Reference (KTU Board Exam Favourite):** When a petrol engine operates at **λ = 1.00 ± 0.02** (the "lambda window" of a three-way catalytic converter), CO ≈ 0.5 %, HC ≈ 50 ppm, and NOₓ is catalytically reduced by > 95 %. Deviation in either direction destroys TWC efficiency.

## 2.4 Smoke Meter — Beer-Lambert Law of Optical Extinction

The fundamental physics behind the DSM is the **Beer-Lambert Law** of light absorption by a particulate-laden gas:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-kL}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity (W/m²)
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity (W/m²)
- $k$ = light absorption coefficient (1/m) — *the absolute, unit-independent measure of smoke density*
- $L$ = optical path length (m)

The transmittance $T$ and opacity $\Phi$ are derived as:

$$
T = \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-kL} \qquad \Longrightarrow \qquad \Phi = (1 - T) \times 100\,\% = \left(1 - e^{-kL}\right) \times 100\,\%
$$

Solving for the absorption coefficient:

$$
k = \frac{1}{L}\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{1}{L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = -\frac{1}{L}\ln(1-\Phi)
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the canonical KTU board-exam reference for this experiment. Memorise every row.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Equation | Units | Typical KTU Value |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F (gasoline) | $(\text{A/F})_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | **14.7 : 1** |
| 2 | Stoichiometric A/F (diesel) | $(\text{A/F})_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | **14.5 : 1** |
| 3 | Lambda | $\lambda = (\text{A/F})_a / (\text{A/F})_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | 0.95 – 1.05 |
| 4 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2]+[\text{CO}]+[\text{HC}]}\times100$ | % | > 95 % (BS-VI) |
| 5 | Excess Air | $\lambda - 1$ (lean) or $1 - \lambda$ (rich) | dimensionless | < 0.05 |
| 6 | Beer-Lambert (DSM) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-kL}$ | W/m² | fundamental |
| 7 | Opacity | $\Phi = (1 - e^{-kL}) \times 100$ | % | 0 – 100 % |
| 8 | Hartridge Smoke Unit | $N = 100(1 - e^{-kL})$ | HSU | 0 – 100 |
| 9 | Bosch Smoke Unit | $S = 10(1 - e^{-kL})$ | BSU | 0 – 10 |
| 10 | Absorption coefficient | $k = (1/L)\ln(I_0/I)$ | 1/m | 0 – 7.0 |
| 11 | Free Acceleration Opacity limit (BS-VI) | $\Phi_{max}$ at snap acceleration | % | **≤ 50 % HSU** |
| 12 | BS-VI CO limit (petrol) | at idle | % vol | **≤ 0.5 %** |
| 13 | BS-VI HC limit (petrol) | at idle | ppm | **≤ 200 ppm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **L (Optical Path Length) in DSM:** Indian-standard smoke meters (e.g., Bosch, Hartridge, AVL) use **L = 0.430 m** (BSU) or **L = 0.106 m** (HSU). This is why the conversion between BSU and HSU is *not* linear: $1\,\text{BSU} \approx 10\,\text{HSU}$ in numerical *display* but represents the *same* physical smoke density at different L values. KTU questions often test this distinction.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Service Centres**: The EGA/DSM is mandated under the **PUC (Pollution Under Control) Certificate** programme of the Indian Ministry of Road Transport & Highways.
- **In R&D**: OEMs use EGA data to calibrate ECUs for BS-VI compliance and to validate Three-Way Catalytic Converter (TWC), Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC), Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF), and Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) performance.
- **In Academia**: KTU's PCMUL308 lab bridges the gap between IC engine theory and real-world pollutant measurement.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Combustion Efficiency & Lambda from EGA Readings

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A four-cylinder gasoline engine is tested on an EGA. The exhaust gas analysis (dry basis) yields the following values at **2500 rpm, WOT**:

$$
[\text{HC}] = 120\,\text{ppm (as C}_1\text{)}, \quad [\text{CO}] = 1.2\,\%,\quad [\text{CO}_2] = 13.8\,\%,\quad [\text{O}_2] = 2.5\,\%
$$

Calculate: (a) the **combustion efficiency** $\eta_c$, and (b) the **lambda value** $\lambda$ using the simplified Spindt equation.

### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Combustion Efficiency

**Step 1:** Convert HC from ppm to percent (for consistent units).

$$
[\text{HC}] = 120\,\text{ppm} = \frac{120}{10{,}000}\,\% = 0.0120\,\%
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the combustion efficiency formula.

$$
\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + [\text{HC}]} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
\eta_c = \frac{13.8}{13.8 + 1.2 + 0.0120} \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$
13.8 + 1.2 + 0.0120 = 15.012
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the final ratio.

$$
\eta_c = \frac{13.8}{15.012} \times 100 = 91.93\,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The engine is operating slightly rich (η_c = 91.93 %, well below 98 % target for stoichiometric TWC operation). The high CO of 1.2 % confirms rich mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Always show the *unit conversion* of ppm → % explicitly. A 1-mark deduction is common if students leave ppm and % in the same equation.

### 3.1.2 Part (b) — Lambda Value (Spindt Equation)

**Step 1:** Write the simplified Spindt equation (used when fuel is C₈H₁₈, i.e., gasoline approximation):

$$
\lambda = \frac{[\text{CO}_2] + \frac{[\text{CO}]}{2} + [\text{O}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + \frac{[\text{HC}]}{10000}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values (note HC is still in ppm, so divide by 10,000 only in the denominator's HC term to align units).

$$
\lambda = \frac{13.8 + \frac{1.2}{2} + 2.5}{13.8 + 1.2 + \frac{120}{10000}}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerator.

$$
13.8 + 0.6 + 2.5 = 16.9
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate denominator.

$$
13.8 + 1.2 + 0.0120 = 15.012
$$

**Step 5:** Compute lambda.

$$
\lambda = \frac{16.9}{15.012} = 1.1259 \approx 1.13
$$

**Interpretation:** $\lambda = 1.13 > 1$ indicates a **lean** mixture. The high O₂ (2.5 %) and lower CO are consistent. The HC is 120 ppm, which is at the boundary of the BS-VI 200 ppm limit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often use the *uncorrected* Spindt formula or forget to convert HC. The Brettschneider formula includes a term for the H/C ratio of the fuel. For C₈H₁₈ (octane), the coefficient is different from C₁₂H₂₆ (dodecane). Always state the **fuel assumption** in your answer.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Smoke Density from DSM (Free Acceleration Test)

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A diesel engine is subjected to a **Free Acceleration Test** as per IS 14682. The following smoke meter readings (in HSU) are recorded at 6 successive accelerations:

$$
N_1 = 42,\; N_2 = 48,\; N_3 = 55,\; N_4 = 50,\; N_5 = 44,\; N_6 = 41 \quad \text{(HSU)}
$$

Calculate: (a) the **maximum opacity** $\Phi_{max}$, (b) the **peak absorption coefficient** $k$ (given $L = 0.106$ m), and (c) the **average smoke level** $\bar{N}$ for the test.

### 3.2.1 Part (a) — Maximum Opacity

**Step 1:** Identify the peak value from the data set.

$$
N_{max} = \max(42, 48, 55, 50, 44, 41) = 55\,\text{HSU}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert HSU to percentage opacity (HSU is numerically equal to % opacity in the Hartridge convention).

$$
\Phi_{max} = N_{max}\,\% = 55\,\%
$$

**Step 3:** Comment on compliance with BS-VI.

$$
\Phi_{max} = 55\,\% > 50\,\% \quad \text{(BS-VI limit)}
$$

**Verdict:** The vehicle **fails** the BS-VI free acceleration test. Probable cause: clogged air filter, faulty injector spray pattern, low compression, or EGR malfunction.

### 3.2.2 Part (b) — Peak Absorption Coefficient

**Step 1:** Use the relationship between HSU and $k$:

$$
N = 100 \left(1 - e^{-kL}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $k$:

$$
e^{-kL} = 1 - \frac{N}{100} = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45
$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm:

$$
-kL = \ln(0.45) = -0.7985
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $k$:

$$
k = \frac{0.7985}{L} = \frac{0.7985}{0.106} = 7.533\,\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5:** Express in standard units:

$$
\boxed{k \approx 7.53\,\text{m}^{-1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** A $k$ of 7.53 /m represents **very dark, thick black smoke** — typical of a poorly maintained diesel. A healthy modern CRDi engine with DPF would show $k < 0.5$ /m.

### 3.2.3 Part (c) — Average Smoke Level

**Step 1:** Sum the readings.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6} N_i = 42 + 48 + 55 + 50 + 44 + 41 = 280
$$

**Step 2:** Compute arithmetic mean.

$$
\bar{N} = \frac{280}{6} = 46.67\,\text{HSU}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute standard deviation (for full board answer, useful for engine health assessment).

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum(N_i - \bar{N})^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the squared deviations:

| $i$ | $N_i$ | $N_i - \bar{N}$ | $(N_i - \bar{N})^2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 42 | −4.67 | 21.81 |
| 2 | 48 | +1.33 | 1.77 |
| 3 | 55 | +8.33 | 69.39 |
| 4 | 50 | +3.33 | 11.09 |
| 5 | 44 | −2.67 | 7.13 |
| 6 | 41 | −5.67 | 32.15 |
| **Σ** | — | — | **143.34** |

**Step 5:** Substitute and evaluate.

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{143.34}{6}} = \sqrt{23.89} = 4.89\,\text{HSU}
$$

**Step 6:** Express the final result as $\bar{N} \pm \sigma$.

$$
\boxed{\bar{N} = 46.67 \pm 4.89\,\text{HSU}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The IS 14682 protocol specifies that the **first three accelerations are conditioning** (to blow out soot deposits in the exhaust) and the **last four are the actual test** whose average is reported. In this example, $N_3$ to $N_6$ would be the official test set, but the question provided 6 readings — always read the problem statement carefully.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Interpreting EGA/DSM Data

| Symptom | HC (ppm) | CO (%) | CO₂ (%) | O₂ (%) | λ | Likely Cause | Action |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Normal (BS-VI TWC)** | 50 | 0.5 | 14.5 | 0.5 | 1.00 | Healthy engine | None |
| **Rich mixture** | 200 | 5.0 | 10.0 | 0.2 | 0.85 | Leaking injector, faulty MAF, high fuel pressure | Injector service, MAF clean |
| **Lean mixture** | 50 | 0.1 | 13.0 | 5.0 | 1.20 | Vacuum leak, weak fuel pump | Smoke test, fuel pressure test |
| **Misfire** | **> 1000** | 2.0 | 8.0 | 1.0 | ~ 1.05 | Bad spark plug, coil, or injector | Ignition system diagnostics |
| **Cat converter failure** | 200 | 1.5 | 11.0 | 0.8 | 0.95 | Aged TWC | Replace catalytic converter |
| **Diesel black smoke (high load)** | — | — | — | — | — | Over-fuelling, poor atomisation | Injector service, EGR check |
| **Diesel white smoke (cold)** | — | — | — | — | — | Unburnt fuel, cold start | Allow warm-up, check glow plugs |
| **Diesel blue smoke** | — | — | — | — | — | Oil burning (worn rings/valve seals) | Engine overhaul |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Exhaust Gas Analyser (NDIR Optical Bench) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Exhaust Tailpipe] --> P1[Particulate Filter Probe]
    P1 --> W1[Water Knockout Trap]
    W1 --> F1[0.2 micron Particulate Filter]
    F1 --> PUMP[Diaphragm Pump 1.5 L per min]
    PUMP --> SPLIT[Gas Flow Splitter]
    SPLIT --> CH1[NDIR Cell 1: CO]
    SPLIT --> CH2[NDIR Cell 2: CO2]
    SPLIT --> CH3[NDIR Cell 3: HC]
    SPLIT --> CH4[Electrochemical Cell: O2]
    SPLIT --> CH5[Electrochemical Cell: NO optional]
    CH1 --> MCU[Microcontroller Signal Processor]
    CH2 --> MCU
    CH3 --> MCU
    CH4 --> MCU
    CH5 --> MCU
    MCU --> DISP[LCD Display Module]
    MCU --> USB[USB Data Logging Port]
    MCU --> BT[Bluetooth Module optional]
    ZCAL[Zero Gas Span Calibration Module] -.calibration pulse.-> CH1
    ZCAL -.calibration pulse.-> CH2
    ZCAL -.calibration pulse.-> CH3
    ZCAL -.calibration pulse.-> CH4
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The probe draws a continuous sample from the tailpipe via a **positive-displacement diaphragm pump**.
- The sample first passes a **water knockout** to remove condensate, then a **fine particulate filter** to protect the optical cells.
- The gas is split into four parallel sensor channels: **three NDIR cells (CO, CO₂, HC)** and **one electrochemical cell (O₂)**.
- The **microcontroller** computes λ and combustion efficiency in real time, displaying all values on the LCD and logging to USB/Bluetooth.
- The **calibration module** injects span gas (e.g., 4 % CO, 12 % CO₂) periodically for automatic zero/span correction.

## 4.2 Diesel Smoke Meter (Partial-Flow Opacimeter) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EX[Engine Exhaust Tailpipe] --> PROBE[Inertial Sampling Probe]
    PROBE --> CHAMBER[Measurement Chamber L equals 0.106 m]
    LMP1[LED Light Source 560 nm green] --> LENS1[Collimating Lens]
    LENS1 --> CHAMBER
    CHAMBER --> LENS2[Focussing Lens]
    LENS2 --> PD[Photodiode Detector]
    PD --> AMP[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    AMP --> ADC[16 bit ADC]
    ADC --> DSP[DSP Processor]
    DSP --> CALC[k and N Calculator]
    CALC --> LCD[Opacity Display percent and HSU]
    FAN[Cooling Fan] -.thermal stability.-> CHAMBER
    HEAT[Chamber Heater 70 deg C] -.condensation prevention.-> CHAMBER
    PWR[Zero Check Shutter] -.automatic zero.-> LENS1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **inertial probe** uses centrifugal acceleration to remove large particles and only the fine smoke-laden air enters the **measurement chamber**.
- A **green LED (λ ≈ 560 nm)** projects through the chamber — green is chosen because the human eye is most sensitive in this band and smoke has strong absorption here.
- The transmitted light intensity is measured by a **photodiode** whose output is amplified and digitised.
- The **DSP** computes $k$ and $N$ in real time using the Beer-Lambert law.
- The **heater** prevents water condensation on the optical windows — a critical reliability feature.

## 4.3 Free Acceleration Test Procedure (DSM) — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine warm-up 80 deg C coolant] --> B[Insert sampling probe to 25 cm depth]
    B --> C[Idle 10 seconds for purge]
    C --> D[Accelerate pedal from idle to WOT in 1 second]
    D --> E[Hold WOT for 2 seconds]
    E --> F[Return to idle for 10 seconds]
    F --> G{Repeat 6 times}
    G -->|Yes| D
    G -->|No after 6 cycles| H[Discard first 3 readings as conditioning]
    H --> I[Average last 4 readings]
    I --> J{Compare with BS-VI limit 50 percent HSU}
    J -->|Pass| K[Issue PUC certificate]
    J -->|Fail| L[Service engine and retest]
```

## 4.4 EGA Test Procedure (Petrol Engine) — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine warm-up 80 deg C] --> B[Insert EGA probe to tailpipe 30 cm]
    B --> C[Perform automatic zero and span calibration]
    C --> D[Run engine at idle 800 rpm for 30 s]
    D --> E[Record HC CO CO2 O2 lambda]
    E --> F[Raise to 2500 rpm hold 30 s]
    F --> G[Record readings]
    G --> H[Raise to wide open throttle 30 s]
    H --> I[Record peak readings]
    I --> J[Compare with BS-VI limits]
    J -->|Pass| K[Print report and PUC]
    J -->|Fail| L[Diagnose fault and retest]
```
