# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List the four gaseous pollutants measured by an Exhaust Gas Analyser in a petrol engine and state the working principle of the CO sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four gaseous pollutants measured by an EGA are:

1. **Hydrocarbons (HC)** in **ppm** (parts per million).
2. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** in **% volume**.
3. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** in **% volume**.
4. **Oxygen (O₂)** in **% volume**.

**Working Principle of the CO Sensor:**
The CO sensor is a **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR)** detector. The exhaust sample is drawn into a chamber through which infrared light from a heated source is passed. CO molecules absorb IR radiation at their characteristic wavelength (~4.7 µm). The absorbed energy causes differential heating in the detector cell (which is filled with pure CO reference gas), creating a pressure pulse detected by a microphone. The magnitude of the pulse is proportional to the CO concentration in the sample.

*(1 mark for listing all 4 pollutants with units, 2 marks for NDIR explanation)*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Bosch Smoke Unit (BSU) and Hartridge Smoke Unit (HSU) with respect to optical path length.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | BSU (Bosch Smoke Unit) | HSU (Hartridge Smoke Unit) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Optical path length (L) | **0.430 m** | **0.106 m** |
| Numerical range | 0 – 10 | 0 – 100 |
| Origin | Bosch (Germany) | Hartridge (UK) |
| Conversion | $1\,\text{BSU} = 10\,\text{HSU}$ numerically (only at specific $k$) | — |

**Key Distinction:** The same physical smoke density will show *different numerical readings* on a BSU and an HSU meter because of the different path lengths $L$. The two are not directly interchangeable; conversion requires the Beer-Lambert equation. *(3 marks: 1 mark for BSU value, 1 mark for HSU value, 1 mark for distinction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question B.A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) Exhaust Gas Analyser**. Discuss the role of each sub-system in measuring CO and CO₂. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The exhaust gas analysis of a petrol engine gave the following readings at idle: HC = 220 ppm, CO = 1.8 %, CO₂ = 12.5 %, O₂ = 1.5 %. Calculate the **combustion efficiency** and the **lambda value** using the Spindt equation. Comment on whether the engine is running rich or lean. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B.A(a) — Working Principle of NDIR EGA (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** An NDIR Exhaust Gas Analyser is an instrument that uses selective infrared absorption to measure the concentration of CO, CO₂, and HC gases in the exhaust stream of a petrol engine in real time.

**Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**

The EGA consists of the following subsystems in flow sequence:

1. **Sampling Probe & Particulate Filter:** A stainless-steel probe is inserted into the tailpipe. A water knockout and 0.2 µm particulate filter remove moisture and soot to protect downstream sensors.

2. **Diaphragm Pump:** A positive-displacement pump draws the filtered gas at a controlled flow rate (~1.5 L/min) into the analyser.

3. **IR Source & Chopper:** A heated nichrome filament (~600 °C) emits broadband infrared. A motor-driven chopper wheel modulates the beam into a pulsating signal at a known frequency (e.g., 200 Hz), enabling AC-coupled detection (eliminates DC drift).

4. **NDIR Sensor Cells (CO and CO₂):**
   - The modulated IR passes through a *measurement cell* (containing the exhaust sample) and then a *reference cell* (sealed with the target gas, e.g., pure CO).
   - The exhaust gas absorbs IR at characteristic wavelengths: CO at ~4.7 µm, CO₂ at ~4.3 µm, HC at ~3.4 µm.
   - The differential IR intensity causes differential heating of the two gas volumes, producing a pressure pulse in the reference cell.
   - A condenser microphone (or modern micro-capacitive diaphragm) detects the pressure pulse; its amplitude is proportional to gas concentration.

5. **Electrochemical O₂ Cell:** A separate lead-air/Zirconia cell measures O₂ concentration (NDIR cannot measure O₂ because O₂ is homonuclear and has no IR absorption).

6. **Signal Processor & Display:** A microcontroller computes HC, CO, CO₂, O₂, λ, and combustion efficiency, displaying on an LCD with data logging.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The NDIR technique is robust, has a fast response (< 3 s), does not require consumable reagents, and provides direct digital output. The modular design allows field calibration via span gas and supports BS-VI compliance testing.

#### Model Solution B.A(b) — Combustion Efficiency and Lambda (7 marks)

**Given Data (1 mark):**
HC = 220 ppm, CO = 1.8 %, CO₂ = 12.5 %, O₂ = 1.5 %

**Step 1: Convert HC to % (0.5 mark)**

$$
[\text{HC}] = \frac{220}{10{,}000} = 0.0220\,\%
$$

**Step 2: Compute Combustion Efficiency (2.5 marks)**

$$
\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + [\text{HC}]} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_c = \frac{12.5}{12.5 + 1.8 + 0.0220} \times 100 = \frac{12.5}{14.322} \times 100 = 87.28\,\%
$$

**Step 3: Apply Spindt's Equation for λ (2.5 marks)**

$$
\lambda = \frac{[\text{CO}_2] + \frac{[\text{CO}]}{2} + [\text{O}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2] + [\text{CO}] + \frac{[\text{HC}]}{10{,}000}}
$$

$$
\lambda = \frac{12.5 + 0.9 + 1.5}{12.5 + 1.8 + 0.0220} = \frac{14.9}{14.322} = 1.0404
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (0.5 mark)**

Since λ = 1.04 > 1, the engine is running slightly **lean**. However, the high HC of 220 ppm (above BS-VI limit of 200 ppm) and high CO of 1.8 % (above BS-VI 0.5 %) indicate **misfire or partial combustion**, possibly due to:
- Fouled spark plug
- Weak ignition coil
- Vacuum leak causing lean misfire
- Failed ignition event

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert HC from ppm to % (loss of 0.5 mark).
> 2. Using wrong formula — students sometimes apply the Brettschneider equation when only the Spindt is permitted (loss of 1–2 marks). Always use the formula *stated* in the question.
> 3. Not commenting on rich/lean (loss of 0.5 mark) — KTU board requires a *conclusion* statement.

---

### Question B.B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** What is **opacity**? Explain the **Beer-Lambert Law** as applied to diesel smoke measurement. Derive the relationship between **Hartridge Smoke Unit (N)**, **opacity (Φ)**, and **absorption coefficient (k)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel engine is tested on a smoke meter during a **free acceleration test**. The recorded peak HSU values for 6 accelerations are: 38, 42, 50, 47, 43, 40. The path length is $L = 0.106$ m. Determine: (i) the **maximum opacity**, (ii) the **peak absorption coefficient** $k$, and (iii) whether the engine complies with **BS-VI norms (limit = 50 % HSU)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B.B(a) — Opacity & Beer-Lambert Derivation (7 marks)

**Definition of Opacity (1 mark):** Opacity ($\Phi$) is the fraction of incident light that is prevented from passing through a smoke-laden gas sample, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\Phi = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\,\%
$$

**Beer-Lambert Law Statement (2 marks):** When monochromatic light of intensity $I_0$ passes through a homogeneous absorbing medium of thickness $L$, the transmitted intensity $I$ decreases exponentially with the absorption coefficient $k$:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-kL}
$$

This law is valid for thin smoke layers and is the physical basis of all optical smoke meters used in automotive diagnostics.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the transmittance.

$$
T = \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-kL}
$$

**Step 2:** Express opacity in terms of T.

$$
\Phi = (1 - T) \times 100 = \left(1 - e^{-kL}\right) \times 100\,\%
$$

**Step 3:** Express the Hartridge Smoke Unit N, defined as the numerical percentage of opacity:

$$
N = 100\left(1 - e^{-kL}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Equate Φ and N:

$$
\boxed{N = \Phi = 100\left(1 - e^{-kL}\right)}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $k$:

$$
e^{-kL} = 1 - \frac{N}{100}
$$

$$
-kL = \ln\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{k = -\frac{1}{L}\ln\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right)}
$$

#### Model Solution B.B(b) — Free Acceleration Analysis (7 marks)

**Given Data (0.5 mark):**
$N_1 = 38$, $N_2 = 42$, $N_3 = 50$, $N_4 = 47$, $N_5 = 43$, $N_6 = 40$ (HSU). $L = 0.106$ m. BS-VI limit = 50 % HSU.

**Part (i): Maximum Opacity (1.5 marks)**

$$
N_{max} = \max(38, 42, 50, 47, 43, 40) = 50\,\text{HSU}
$$

$$
\Phi_{max} = 50\,\% \text{ (since HSU numerically equals % opacity)}
$$

**Part (ii): Peak Absorption Coefficient (3 marks)**

**Step 1:** Substitute N = 50 into the formula.

$$
k = -\frac{1}{L}\ln\left(1 - \frac{N}{100}\right) = -\frac{1}{0.106}\ln(1 - 0.5) = -\frac{1}{0.106}\ln(0.5)
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate $\ln(0.5) = -0.6931$.

$$
k = -\frac{(-0.6931)}{0.106} = \frac{0.6931}{0.106}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $k$.

$$
\boxed{k = 6.538\,\text{m}^{-1}}
$$

**Part (iii): Compliance Check (2 marks)**

Per the IS 14682 protocol, the **first 3 readings** (38, 42, 50) are *conditioning* and the **last 3 readings** (47, 43, 40) are *test* values. The official result is the *average* of the test readings:

$$
\bar{N}_{test} = \frac{47 + 43 + 40}{3} = \frac{130}{3} = 43.33\,\text{HSU}
$$

Since $\bar{N}_{test} = 43.33\,\% < 50\,\%$, the engine **PASSES** the BS-VI free acceleration test. However, $N_{max} = 50$ exactly at the limit suggests the engine is **borderline** and may require maintenance soon.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. Using the wrong path length $L$ — students often confuse BSU (0.430 m) and HSU (0.106 m). Always read the problem.
> 2. Reporting $N_{max}$ instead of the protocol-defined average — this loses 1–2 marks because IS 14682 specifies the average of the *test* (not the *conditioning*) accelerations.
> 3. Forgetting to state the *conclusion* in plain language (PASS/FAIL).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this section twice before walking into the ESE exam hall.**

### **A. EGA (Petrol Engine) — Key Facts**

- ✅ Measures **HC, CO, CO₂, O₂** in **ppm** or **%** as appropriate.
- ✅ Sensor principle: **NDIR** for HC/CO/CO₂; **electrochemical** for O₂.
- ✅ Stoichiometric A/F for gasoline = **14.7 : 1**; for diesel = **14.5 : 1**.
- ✅ Lambda λ = (A/F)_actual / (A/F)_stoich; **λ = 1** = stoichiometric (TWC optimum).
- ✅ **BS-VI idle limits** (typical): HC ≤ 200 ppm, CO ≤ 0.5 %.
- ✅ Combustion efficiency formula: $\eta_c = \frac{[\text{CO}_2]}{[\text{CO}_2]+[\text{CO}]+[\text{HC}]} \times 100$.
- ✅ Spindt's equation is the *simplified* lambda formula used for gasoline.
- ✅ High CO + low O₂ → **rich**; low CO + high O₂ → **lean**.

### **B. DSM (Diesel Engine) — Key Facts**

- ✅ Measures **opacity** (% or HSU) — *not* gaseous composition.
- ✅ Sensor principle: **Optical extinction** (Beer-Lambert law).
- ✅ BS-VI free acceleration limit: **50 % HSU / 50 % opacity**.
- ✅ Hartridge: $L = 0.106$ m, range 0–100. Bosch: $L = 0.430$ m, range 0–10.
- ✅ Formula: $N = 100(1 - e^{-kL})$; $k = -(1/L)\ln(1 - N/100)$.
- ✅ Free acceleration test: **6 accelerations**, first 3 conditioning, last 3 test.
- ✅ **White smoke** = unburnt fuel (cold start); **Black smoke** = over-fuelling; **Blue smoke** = oil burning.

### **C. KTU Board Pattern — Strategic Tips**

- ✅ Always show **unit conversions** explicitly (ppm → %, °C → K).
- ✅ Always state the **assumption** (e.g., "assuming C₈H₁₈ fuel for Spindt").
- ✅ Always provide a **conclusion sentence** ("rich"/"lean"/"pass"/"fail").
- ✅ Draw **block diagrams** with labelled arrows (1 free mark is guaranteed for a clean diagram).
- ✅ Use **two significant figures** in the final answer unless otherwise specified.
- ✅ For lab viva: know the *physical meaning* of λ, k, η_c, Φ, HSU, BSU, NDIR, TWC, DPF, EGR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word:** The EGA/DSM lab is not just a *measurement* exercise — it is the bridge between **IC engine theory** and **emission compliance law**. Master the formulas, the units, and the interpretation logic, and you will secure full marks on this experiment in both the lab continuous assessment and the university examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disassembling of Engine Cylinder Block and Inspection/Servicing of Engine Components, Servicing of Components, Find Ovality and Taperness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Disassembling of Engine Cylinder Block and Inspection/Servicing of Engine Components, Servicing of Components, Find Ovality and Taperness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Disassembling of an Engine Cylinder Block** is the systematic, step-by-step procedure of removing the cylinder block from an engine assembly and subsequently stripping it of all internal and external components (such as pistons, connecting rods, crankshaft, bearings, valves, and cylinder liners/sleeves) for the purpose of inspection, cleaning, measurement, and reconditioning.

**Inspection** is the systematic evaluation of engine components using visual examination, dimensional measurement, and non-destructive testing methods to determine their fitness for reuse, repair, or replacement.

**Servicing** refers to the cleaning, measurement, reconditioning, and reassembly procedures applied to engine components to restore them to their original operational specifications.

**Ovality** (also known as out-of-roundness) is defined as the difference between the maximum and minimum diameter of a cylinder bore measured at the same cross-sectional plane, expressed in micrometers (µm) or millimeters (mm).

**Taperness** (or Taper) is the difference in cylinder bore diameter between two different cross-sectional planes (typically the top dead center region and the bottom dead center region), indicating the axial wear profile of the cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is designed to familiarize students with the systematic disassembly of an engine, identification of wear patterns, and the practical application of precision measuring instruments (Bore Gauge, External Micrometer, Telescopic Gauge, Dial Bore Gauge) used in automotive service stations and engine reconditioning workshops.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **circular drinking glass** that has been used thousands of times. The bottom inside (where it sits on a coaster) gets slightly oval due to uneven contact, and the top remains rounder. Similarly, a cylinder bore wears:
- **Ovality** is like a perfectly round coin being pressed on one side — it becomes slightly egg-shaped in cross-section.
- **Taperness** is like a cone forming inside the cylinder because the piston ring pressures are highest at the top of the stroke.

**Intuitive Real-World Analogy:** Think of a **bicycle pump cylinder**. If you use it heavily, the piston ring scrapes against one side more than the other (due to side-thrust from the connecting rod), making it oval. Additionally, the top portion wears more (higher combustion pressure), making it taper — wider at the top than at the bottom.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards and Tolerances

| Component | Standard Wear Limit |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Cylinder Bore Ovality | **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm** (max permissible) |
| Cylinder Bore Taper | **0.05 mm to 0.15 mm** (max permissible) |
| Standard Cylinder Bore (Petrol) | **70-90 mm** typically |
| Standard Cylinder Bore (Diesel) | **80-105 mm** typically |
| Piston-to-Bore Clearance | **0.02-0.08 mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** Cylinder wear directly affects engine compression ratio, oil consumption, blow-by gases, and overall engine power output. Standard industry tolerance for a worn-out cylinder requiring rebore is **>0.10 mm ovality** or **>0.15 mm taper**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Engine Cylinder Block Components

The cylinder block is the foundational structure of an internal combustion engine. It houses critical components:

- **Cylinder Bores** — The hollow cylindrical cavities where pistons reciprocate
- **Water Jackets** — Surrounding cavities for coolant circulation
- **Crankshaft Tunnel** — Lower housing for the crankshaft
- **Cam Bearing Bores** — Upper housing for the camshaft (in overhead cam engines)
- **Head Bolt Holes** — Threaded holes for securing the cylinder head
- **Lifter Bores** — Holes for valve lifters (in OHV engines)

### 2.2 Disassembly Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

The disassembly follows a **logical top-down and outside-in** approach:

1. **Pre-Disassembly Preparation**
   - Drain engine oil and coolant completely
   - Clean the external surface with degreaser
   - Mark reference positions for all mating parts
   - Photograph critical assemblies (timing chain, valve train)

2. **External Component Removal**
   - Remove intake manifold, exhaust manifold
   - Disconnect fuel lines, electrical connections
   - Remove accessories (alternator, starter, water pump)

3. **Cylinder Head Removal**
   - Loosen head bolts in **reverse torque sequence** (outside-to-inside)
   - Lift cylinder head carefully to avoid damaging the gasket surface

4. **Piston and Connecting Rod Assembly Removal**
   - Remove oil pan
   - Rotate crankshaft to bring piston to BDC (Bottom Dead Center)
   - Remove connecting rod cap bolts
   - Push piston out through the top of the cylinder bore

5. **Crankshaft and Main Bearing Removal**
   - Remove main bearing caps in proper sequence
   - Lift crankshaft carefully to avoid damaging bearing journals

6. **Cylinder Block Final Cleaning**
   - Remove all oil gallery plugs
   - Flush oil passages with cleaning solvent
   - Inspect for cracks using dye-penetrant or magna-flux

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Sequence?** The reverse-order disassembly prevents damage to delicate components, maintains the integrity of mating surfaces, and ensures proper documentation for correct reassembly.

### 2.3 Inspection of Engine Components

Inspection is performed in three tiers:

**A. Visual Inspection**
- Cracks in cylinder walls
- Scoring (longitudinal scratches)
- Pitting and corrosion
- Coking (carbon deposits)

**B. Dimensional Inspection**
- Cylinder bore diameter at multiple points
- Piston diameter
- Crankshaft journal diameter
- Valve stem diameter

**C. Surface Finish Inspection**
- Honing pattern quality
- Surface roughness (Ra value)
- Bearing surface condition

### 2.4 Servicing Operations

| Servicing Operation | Purpose | Tool/Method |
|---------------------|---------|-------------|
| Degreasing | Remove oil, dirt, sludge | Solvent bath, ultrasonic cleaner |
| Decarbonizing | Remove carbon deposits | Wire brush, chemical decarbonizer |
| Inspection | Measure wear | Bore gauge, micrometer |
| Honing | Restore cross-hatch pattern | Cylinder hone (glaze breaker) |
| Lapping | Smooth mating surfaces | Lapping compound + plate |
| Re-boring | Enlarge to next oversize | Boring machine |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAM**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| **Ovality = D_max - D_min** | Difference between max and min diameter in **same** cross-section | mm or µm |
| **Taper = D_top - D_bottom** | Difference in diameter between **top and bottom** cross-sections | mm or µm |
| **Average Diameter = (D_max + D_min) / 2** | Mean bore diameter at a specific plane | mm |
| **Wear Rate = (D_worn - D_new) / Hours** | Cylinder wear rate over service hours | mm/hr |
| **Clearance = D_bore - D_piston** | Piston-to-bore clearance | mm |
| **Piston Oversize = Standard + 0.25/0.50/0.75/1.00 mm** | Rebore oversize increments | mm |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the automotive industry, ovality and taper measurements are **mandatory procedures** during:
- **Engine Overhaul** — Every 1,00,000 to 1,50,000 km
- **Insurance Claim Assessment** — After engine seizure or hydrolock
- **Pre-Purchase Inspection (PPI)** — Used car evaluation
- **Quality Control** — In OEM manufacturing
- **Failure Analysis** — Forensics in engine warranty disputes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A cylinder with **ovality > 0.10 mm** or **taper > 0.15 mm** is generally condemned for reboring to the next oversize (commonly +0.25 mm or +0.50 mm).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Ovality Calculation — Detailed Derivation

**Problem Setup:** A cylinder bore is measured at the top plane (Plane A, 10 mm below the deck surface) using a bore gauge. Measurements are taken in three directions:
- Direction 1 (X-X): Along the crankshaft axis
- Direction 2 (Y-Y): Perpendicular to crankshaft axis
- Direction 3 (Z-Z): 45° to both axes

**Typical measured values:**

| Direction | Top Plane A (mm) | Bottom Plane B (mm) |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| X-X | 82.08 | 82.02 |
| Y-Y | 82.03 | 82.01 |
| Z-Z | 82.06 | 82.02 |

**Step 1: Identify Maximum and Minimum Diameters at Top Plane A**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{A,\max} &= \max(82.08, \, 82.03, \, 82.06) = 82.08 \text{ mm} \\
D_{A,\min} &= \min(82.08, \, 82.03, \, 82.06) = 82.03 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Ovality at Top Plane A**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovality}_{\text{Top}} &= D_{A,\max} - D_{A,\min} \\
&= 82.08 \text{ mm} - 82.03 \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.05 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Identify Maximum and Minimum Diameters at Bottom Plane B**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{B,\max} &= \max(82.02, \, 82.01, \, 82.02) = 82.02 \text{ mm} \\
D_{B,\min} &= \min(82.02, \, 82.01, \, 82.02) = 82.01 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Ovality at Bottom Plane B**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovality}_{\text{Bottom}} &= D_{B,\max} - D_{B,\min} \\
&= 82.02 \text{ mm} - 82.01 \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.01 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Ovality of the Cylinder**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ovality}_{\max} &= \max(\text{Ovality}_{\text{Top}}, \, \text{Ovality}_{\text{Bottom}}) \\
&= \max(0.05, \, 0.01) \\
&= 0.05 \text{ mm} = 50 \, \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Taperness Calculation — Detailed Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate Average Diameter at Top Plane A**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{A,\text{avg}} &= \frac{D_{A,\max} + D_{A,\min}}{2} \\
&= \frac{82.08 + 82.03}{2} \\
&= \frac{164.11}{2} \\
&= 82.055 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Average Diameter at Bottom Plane B**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{B,\text{avg}} &= \frac{D_{B,\max} + D_{B,\min}}{2} \\
&= \frac{82.02 + 82.01}{2} \\
&= \frac{164.03}{2} \\
&= 82.015 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Taperness**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Taper} &= D_{A,\text{avg}} - D_{B,\text{avg}} \\
&= 82.055 \text{ mm} - 82.015 \text{ mm} \\
&= 0.040 \text{ mm} = 40 \, \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Comparative Analysis with Permissible Limits**

| Parameter | Measured Value | Permissible Limit | Status |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| Ovality | 0.05 mm | 0.10 mm | ✓ Within Limit |
| Taper | 0.04 mm | 0.15 mm | ✓ Within Limit |

**Conclusion:** The cylinder bore is **serviceable** and does not require reboring. Honing may be done to restore the cross-hatch pattern.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Ovality (mm) | Taper (mm) | Recommended Action |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|
| < 0.05 | < 0.05 | **No action** — Honing only |
| 0.05 – 0.10 | 0.05 – 0.10 | **Hone and deglaze** |
| 0.10 – 0.15 | 0.10 – 0.15 | **Rebore to +0.25 mm** oversize |
| > 0.15 | > 0.15 | **Rebore to +0.50 mm or more** |

### 3.4 Instrumentation Error Analysis

The bore gauge has a least count of **0.01 mm**. The dial indicator has a least count of **0.001 mm**.

**Total Measurement Uncertainty (TMU):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TMU} &= \pm \sqrt{\text{Instrument}^2 + \text{Operator}^2 + \text{Environmental}^2} \\
&= \pm \sqrt{(0.01)^2 + (0.005)^2 + (0.002)^2} \\
&= \pm \sqrt{0.000100 + 0.000025 + 0.000004} \\
&= \pm \sqrt{0.000129} \\
&= \pm 0.0114 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Significance:** Always report the final ovality/taper to **two decimal places** in mm (or in µm), matching the precision of the least precise measuring instrument.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
