<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Ovality and Taperness of a cylinder bore. State the standard permissible limits for both.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ovality** is defined as the difference between the maximum and minimum diameter of a cylinder bore measured at the **same cross-sectional plane** (i.e., at the same height from the cylinder deck surface). It indicates the **out-of-roundness** of the bore caused by uneven side thrust from the connecting rod.

$$\text{Ovality} = D_{\max} - D_{\min} \quad \text{(at the same plane)}$$

**Taperness** (or Taper) is defined as the difference in cylinder bore diameter measured between **two different cross-sectional planes**, typically the top dead center (TDC) region and the bottom dead center (BDC) region. It indicates the **axial wear profile** of the cylinder.

$$\text{Taper} = D_{\text{top,avg}} - D_{\text{bottom,avg}}$$

**Standard Permissible Limits:**
- Ovality: **0.05 mm to 0.10 mm** (max)
- Taperness: **0.05 mm to 0.15 mm** (max)

Beyond these limits, the cylinder requires reboring to the next oversize.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six components of an engine cylinder block and state the function of the cylinder bore.**

**Model Answer:**

The six main components of an engine cylinder block are:

1. **Cylinder Bores** — Hollow cylindrical cavities that guide the reciprocating motion of pistons and provide the combustion chamber volume.
2. **Water Jackets** — Cavities surrounding the bores for coolant circulation to remove excess heat.
3. **Crankshaft Tunnel** — Lower housing that supports the crankshaft via main bearings.
4. **Cam Bearing Bores** — Upper housings that support the camshaft on its bearings.
5. **Head Bolt Holes** — Threaded holes that secure the cylinder head to the block.
6. **Oil Galleries** — Internal passages for lubrication oil distribution.

**Function of Cylinder Bore:** The cylinder bore provides a precise, smooth, and wear-resistant sliding surface for the piston and piston rings. It must maintain dimensional accuracy (roundness and cylindricity) to ensure proper compression, sealing, and minimal friction losses during engine operation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure for disassembling a four-cylinder engine cylinder block, with a neat sketch showing the major components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the procedure to measure ovality and taperness of a cylinder bore using a bore gauge. A cylinder bore has the following measurements:
- Top plane: 82.08 mm (X), 82.03 mm (Y), 82.06 mm (Z)
- Bottom plane: 82.02 mm (X), 82.01 mm (Y), 82.02 mm (Z)

Calculate the ovality and taperness. State whether the cylinder is serviceable. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Disassembly Procedure of a Four-Cylinder Engine Block:**

**Step 1 — Preparation**
- Place engine on a suitable stand
- Drain engine oil from the sump
- Drain coolant from the radiator
- Clean the external surface with degreaser
- Mark the position of all mating parts for reassembly reference

**Step 2 — External Accessories Removal**
- Disconnect battery, fuel lines, and wiring harness
- Remove air intake, exhaust manifold, alternator, starter motor, and water pump
- Remove carburettor/fuel injection system

**Step 3 — Cylinder Head Removal**
- Follow the **reverse torque sequence** (outside-to-inside) for loosening head bolts
- Lift the cylinder head using a lifting tool
- Remove the head gasket and inspect for any leak marks

**Step 4 — Timing Components Removal (if applicable)**
- Remove timing chain/belt cover
- Mark timing alignment
- Remove camshaft, rockers, and lifters

**Step 5 — Oil Pan and Oil Pump Removal**
- Remove oil pan bolts in diagonal sequence
- Lower the oil pan carefully
- Remove oil pump assembly

**Step 6 — Piston and Connecting Rod Removal**
- Rotate crankshaft to bring cylinder #1 piston to BDC
- Remove connecting rod cap bolts
- Push the piston-rod assembly out through the top of the bore using a wooden handle
- **Mark each rod-cap pair** for reassembly

**Step 7 — Crankshaft Removal**
- Remove main bearing cap bolts in reverse sequence
- Carefully lift the crankshaft out
- Remove main bearing shells and store them in order

**Step 8 — Block Cleaning**
- Remove all gallery plugs
- Flush oil passages
- Clean the block externally with solvent

**Diagram: (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram for Exploded View)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Measurement Procedure:**

1. **Calibrate** the bore gauge using a standard ring of known size (e.g., 82.00 mm).
2. **Set zero** on the dial indicator using the standard ring.
3. **Insert** the bore gauge into the cylinder bore at the **top plane** (10 mm below the deck surface).
4. **Rock** the gauge gently to find the **transverse diameter** (perpendicular to bore axis) — this is the true diameter.
5. Take **three readings** at three orientations (X, Y, Z) at the top plane.
6. Repeat at the **bottom plane** (10 mm above the bottom of the bore travel).
7. Record all readings.

**Calculation of Ovality:**

At Top Plane:
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{top,max}} &= 82.08 \text{ mm} \quad (X\text{-direction}) \\
D_{\text{top,min}} &= 82.03 \text{ mm} \quad (Y\text{-direction}) \\
\text{Ovality}_{\text{top}} &= 82.08 - 82.03 = 0.05 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

At Bottom Plane:
$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{bottom,max}} &= 82.02 \text{ mm} \quad (X\text{-direction}) \\
D_{\text{bottom,min}} &= 82.01 \text{ mm} \quad (Y\text{-direction}) \\
\text{Ovality}_{\text{bottom}} &= 82.02 - 82.01 = 0.01 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

Maximum ovality = **0.05 mm**

**Calculation of Taperness:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{top,avg}} &= \frac{82.08 + 82.03}{2} = 82.055 \text{ mm} \\
D_{\text{bottom,avg}} &= \frac{82.02 + 82.01}{2} = 82.015 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Taper} &= 82.055 - 82.015 = 0.040 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result and Conclusion:**

| Parameter | Measured | Permissible Limit | Conclusion |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|------------|
| Ovality | 0.05 mm | 0.10 mm | ✓ Within limit |
| Taper | 0.04 mm | 0.15 mm | ✓ Within limit |

**Conclusion:** The cylinder bore is **serviceable** and does not require reboring. A light honing operation is recommended to restore the cross-hatch pattern for proper piston ring seating.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** What are the different inspection methods employed for engine components? Explain any three in detail with their purposes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the servicing operations performed on engine cylinder block and its components during an engine overhaul. What are the standard wear limits that determine reboring? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Inspection Methods for Engine Components:**

The three main categories are:

**1. Visual Inspection**
- **Purpose:** Identify surface defects such as cracks, scoring, corrosion, pitting, and discoloration.
- **Method:** Use of magnifying glass, borescope (for internal surfaces), and good lighting.
- **Examples of Defects Detected:**
  - Hairline cracks in cylinder walls
  - Scoring marks from broken piston rings
  - Coking on piston crown

**2. Dimensional Inspection**
- **Purpose:** Quantify wear and determine conformance to manufacturer specifications.
- **Instruments Used:**
  - **Bore Gauge** — Cylinder bore diameter
  - **External Micrometer** — Crankshaft journals, piston diameter
  - **Dial Indicator** — Run-out, backlash
  - **Vernier Caliper** — General linear measurements
  - **Plastigauge** — Bearing clearance

**3. Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**
- **Purpose:** Detect internal or subsurface defects without damaging the component.
- **Common Methods:**
  - **Dye-Penetrant Test** — Surface cracks in cylinder block, crankshaft
  - **Magnetic Particle Test** — Used on ferrous components like crankshaft
  - **Ultrasonic Testing** — Internal flaws in thick sections
  - **Eddy Current Testing** — Surface crack detection

**Procedure Example — Cylinder Bore Measurement:**

1. Clean bore thoroughly with solvent
2. Set bore gauge on a standard ring (e.g., 82.00 mm)
3. Insert at top, mid, and bottom planes
4. Take readings in X, Y, Z directions
5. Compare with manufacturer specs

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Servicing Operations During Engine Overhaul:**

**1. Cleaning Operations**
- **Degreasing:** All components are immersed in hot solvent bath to remove oil, sludge, and grease
- **Decarbonizing:** Pistons, valves, and combustion chambers are cleaned of carbon deposits using wire brushes and chemical decarbonizers
- **Oil Gallery Flushing:** All oil passages are blown with compressed air and flushed with solvent

**2. Dimensional Inspection and Measurement**
- Measure cylinder bore for ovality and taper
- Measure crankshaft journals for wear and out-of-roundness
- Measure piston diameter at skirt (perpendicular to piston pin)
- Measure valve stem diameter and guide clearance

**3. Reconditioning Operations**
- **Honing:** Cylinder bores are honed with a glaze-breaker hone to restore the **60° cross-hatch pattern** essential for oil retention and ring seating
- **Lapping:** Valves are lapped into their seats using lapping compound
- **Grinding:** Crankshaft journals are reground to undersize (0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00 mm) if worn beyond limits
- **Boring:** Cylinder bores are rebored to next oversize if wear exceeds limits

**4. Replacement of Wear Items**
- Piston rings (always replaced during overhaul)
- Gaskets and seals
- Bearings (main and big-end)
- Timing belt/chain
- Oil pump

**5. Reassembly with New Fasteners**
- Use new head bolts (if TTY - Torque-to-Yield type)
- Use new lock washers and split pins
- Apply thread sealant where required

**Standard Wear Limits Determining Reboring:**

| Parameter | Within Limit (Hone) | Rebore Required |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| Ovality | < 0.10 mm | ≥ 0.10 mm |
| Taperness | < 0.15 mm | ≥ 0.15 mm |
| Piston-to-Bore Clearance | < 0.10 mm | ≥ 0.10 mm |
| Crankshaft Journal Taper | < 0.05 mm | ≥ 0.05 mm |

**Rebore Oversize Increments:** +0.25 mm, +0.50 mm, +0.75 mm, +1.00 mm (standard service sizes)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Ovality with Taperness:** Students often interchange these. Remember: Ovality = **same plane, different directions**; Taperness = **different planes, same direction**.
>
> 2. **Wrong Formula Application:** Taperness uses the **average diameter** at each plane, NOT the maximum diameter. Using max-min between planes is incorrect.
>
> 3. **Not Specifying Measurement Direction:** Always mention that ovality occurs **perpendicular to the piston pin** (side-thrust direction) due to connecting rod angularity.
>
> 4. **Skipping Calibration Step:** The bore gauge MUST be calibrated on a standard ring before measurement. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks if this is missed.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Units:** Always state ovality/taper in **mm or µm** with the correct sign.
>
> 6. **Missing Conclusion:** Always end with a **serviceability conclusion** — whether the cylinder is within limits, needs honing, or requires reboring.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 2**

- ✓ **Ovality** = D_max − D_min (at the **same** cross-sectional plane)
- ✓ **Taperness** = D_top,avg − D_bottom,avg (between **two** cross-sectional planes)
- ✓ Measurements are taken at **top, mid, and bottom** planes
- ✓ Three readings (X, Y, Z) per plane for accurate ovality assessment
- ✓ Standard permissible limits: **Ovality ≤ 0.10 mm**, **Taper ≤ 0.15 mm**
- ✓ Bore gauge is calibrated using a **standard ring** before measurement
- ✓ **Cylinder bore wear** occurs due to side-thrust (causing ovality) and combustion pressure (causing taper)
- ✓ Disassembly follows a **logical sequence** — external → head → pistons → crankshaft
- ✓ **Piston rings are always replaced** during an engine overhaul
- ✓ **Honing** restores the **60° cross-hatch pattern** on cylinder walls
- ✓ **Rebore oversizes:** +0.25, +0.50, +0.75, +1.00 mm
- ✓ **Crankcase ventilation** and **oil gallery** cleanliness are critical
- ✓ NDT methods include dye-penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, and eddy current testing
- ✓ Always **mark mating parts** (rod-caps, bearing shells) before disassembly
- ✓ Piston-to-bore clearance: typically **0.02–0.08 mm** for petrol engines
- ✓ A "serviceable" cylinder needs only honing; a "worn" cylinder needs reboring

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Test of Petrol and Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Compression Test of Petrol and Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Compression Test** (also called the **Motoring Test**) is a standard laboratory experiment conducted on internal combustion (I.C.) engines in which the engine is driven externally by an electric motor or a swinging field dynamometer **without supplying any fuel**, so that it runs purely on the energy required to overcome mechanical friction and to compress the air-fuel mixture (or air alone) trapped inside the cylinder.

The objective is to measure:
- The **compression pressure** developed inside the cylinder at the end of the compression stroke.
- The **frictional power** of the engine at different speeds.
- The **friction mean effective pressure (FMEP)**.
- To compare the compression behaviour of a **Petrol (SI) engine** versus a **Diesel (CI) engine**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMUL308):** The compression test is a compulsory lab experiment. Students are expected to plot a Pressure–Crank Angle (P–θ) diagram, calculate the **Isothermal / Polytropic Index 'n'**, find the **indicated work of compression**, and compare the **peak compression pressure** of petrol and diesel engines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezing a Syringe" Picture

Imagine you have two identical plastic syringes, both sealed at the nozzle.

- **Syringe A (Petrol Engine):** You push the piston in only a little, then stop and use a *spark* (lighter) to ignite the vapour. The compression is *gentle* because a higher compression would cause the air-fuel mixture to auto-ignite (knocking).
- **Syringe B (Diesel Engine):** You push the piston in *very hard* until the air gets so hot that it ignites the diesel spray by itself. *No spark is required — only intense heat from squeezing.*

This is exactly what happens inside a real engine cylinder! The compression test lets us measure *how hard the cylinder squeezes the gas* and *how much power is wasted just to spin the engine without producing useful work.*

| Property | Petrol (SI) Engine | Diesel (CI) Engine |
|----------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Compression Ratio | 6 : 1 to 10 : 1 | 14 : 1 to 22 : 1 |
| Peak Compression Pressure | **7 – 12 bar** | **30 – 40 bar** |
| Peak Compression Temperature | 250 – 350 °C | 500 – 700 °C |
| Ignition Method | Spark Plug | Auto-ignition (heat) |
| Fuel Injected During | Intake Stroke | End of Compression Stroke |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Diesel engines have higher compression?**
> Because diesel fuel needs the air to be **HOT** (above the auto-ignition temperature of ≈ 250 °C) to self-ignite. The only way to make the air that hot inside a cylinder is to **squeeze it violently**, which means a high compression ratio.

## 1.3 Why is the Compression Test Important?

- It determines the **mechanical condition** of the engine (worn piston rings, leaking valves, blown head gasket).
- It separates **friction losses** from **useful indicated work**.
- It provides the **Friction Mean Effective Pressure (FMEP)**, which is essential for **Morse Test** calculations.
- It is the **baseline experiment** for finding Brake Thermal Efficiency in KTU lab viva questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background of Compression

When the piston moves from **Bottom Dead Centre (BDC)** to **Top Dead Centre (TDC)** during the compression stroke, the gas trapped in the cylinder is compressed. In a real engine this compression is **neither perfectly isothermal (n = 1)** nor **perfectly adiabatic (n = 1.4)**.

The actual process follows a **polytropic law**:

$$P \cdot V^{n} = \text{constant}$$

The value of the **polytropic index n** for an actual engine is found experimentally and is usually between:
- **n ≈ 1.30 – 1.35** for a petrol engine
- **n ≈ 1.32 – 1.38** for a diesel engine

## 2.2 Operational Steps in the Compression Test

1. **Warm up the engine** to its normal operating temperature.
2. **Cut off the fuel supply** completely to the test cylinder.
3. **Start the electric motor / swinging field dynamometer** so that it *drives* the engine as a motor.
4. **Record the speed** using a tachometer (rpm).
5. **Record the motoring torque** (Tₘ) on the dynamometer scale.
6. A **pressure transducer / dead-weight pressure gauge / mechanical indicator** is fitted in place of the spark plug (or in the cylinder head) to record the **peak compression pressure** and the **P–θ diagram**.
7. **Repeat the readings** for 4 – 5 different speeds.
8. **Plot** the P–θ diagram and calculate the area → work done per cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When a mechanical *dead-weight pressure gauge* is used, the maximum reading corresponds to the **peak pressure at TDC**. When a *rotating cylinder indicator* is used, you get the full P–θ diagram.

## 2.3 Polytropic Compression — Governing Equations

### Equation 1 — Polytropic Law

$$P_1 \cdot V_1^{n} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{n}$$

Where:
- P₁ = Pressure at the start of compression (≈ 1 bar)
- V₁ = Volume at BDC = V_c + V_d
- P₂ = Peak pressure at TDC
- V₂ = Clearance volume = V_c
- n = Polytropic index of compression

### Equation 2 — Compression Ratio

$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_c + V_d}{V_c}$$

### Equation 3 — Peak Pressure from Polytropic Law

Rearranging the polytropic equation:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{n}$$

### Equation 4 — Compression Work per Cycle (per unit mass)

$$W_c = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1}$$

### Equation 5 — Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP)

$$\text{IMEP} = \frac{\text{Indicated Work per cycle}}{\text{Displacement Volume } (V_d)}$$

### Equation 6 — Friction Power (during motoring)

$$F_P = \frac{2\pi N \cdot T_m}{60}$$

Where:
- N = Engine speed in rpm
- Tₘ = Motoring torque in N·m

### Equation 7 — Friction Mean Effective Pressure (FMEP)

$$\text{FMEP} = \frac{F_P \cdot 60}{V_d \cdot N} = \frac{2\pi \cdot T_m}{V_d}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = (V_c + V_d)/V_c$ | — | 8 (Petrol), 17 (Diesel) |
| 2 | Polytropic Law | $P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$ | bar·m³ | — |
| 3 | Peak Compression Pressure | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{n}$ | bar | 9.4 (Petrol), 35.4 (Diesel) |
| 4 | Compression Work | $W_c = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2)/(n-1)$ | J | — |
| 5 | Polytropic Index | $n = \log(P_2/P_1)/\log(r)$ | — | 1.30 – 1.38 |
| 6 | Friction Power | $F_P = 2\pi N T_m / 60$ | W | 5 – 15 % of BP |
| 7 | FMEP | $\text{FMEP} = 2\pi T_m / V_d$ | bar | 0.7 – 1.5 bar |
| 8 | Indicated Power | $IP = (FMEP + BMEP) \cdot V_d \cdot N / 60$ | W | — |
| 9 | Swept Volume | $V_d = (\pi/4) D^2 L$ | m³ | — |
| 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = BP / IP$ | — | 0.80 – 0.90 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $n = \log(P_2/P_1)/\log(r)$, the **logarithm base can be 10 or e** — both give the same answer. Students often forget to use the ratio of pressures **P₂/P₁** (not P₂ alone) and lose marks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Workshop diagnostic tool:** Mechanics use a *cranking compression tester* in service stations to check engine health.
- **Engine design:** Manufacturers use the compression test data to validate **simulation models** (1D engine simulation in GT-Suite, AVL FIRE).
- **Friction reduction:** Modern low-friction coatings (DLC — Diamond-Like Carbon) on piston rings are validated by measuring FMEP reductions through the motoring test.
- **Emission regulation:** Lower friction = lower fuel consumption = lower CO₂ — directly linked to **BS-VI and Euro-7** norms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Pressure from the Polytropic Law

We begin with the first law of thermodynamics for a polytropic process. For a closed system undergoing a quasi-static compression:

$$P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by V₂ⁿ:**

$$P_1 \cdot \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n} = P_2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of compression ratio** $r = V_1 / V_2$:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides (used later to find n):**

$$\ln P_2 = \ln P_1 + n \cdot \ln r$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the polytropic index n:**

$$n = \frac{\ln(P_2/P_1)}{\ln r}$$

> This is the **most-tested equation** in KTU viva for this experiment.

## 3.2 Derivation: Compression Work per Cycle

For a polytropic process, the boundary work done **on the gas** by the piston is:

$$W_c = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the polytropic relation** $P = P_1 (V_1/V)^{n}$:

$$W_c = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 \left(\frac{V_1}{V}\right)^{n} dV$$

**Step 2 — Pull constants out of the integral:**

$$W_c = P_1 V_1^{n} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-n} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration:**

$$W_c = P_1 V_1^{n} \cdot \left[ \frac{V^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the upper and lower limits:**

$$W_c = \frac{P_1 V_1^{n}}{1-n} \cdot \left( V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Factor out V₁^(1−n) to obtain the standard form:**

$$W_c = \frac{P_1 V_1}{1-n} \cdot \left[ \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-n} - 1 \right]$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by −1, use P₁V₁ⁿ = P₂V₂ⁿ:**

$$W_c = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$$

This is the **standard indicator diagram equation** for compression work and must be memorised.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Petrol Engine)

**Given Data:**
- Bore (D) = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- Stroke (L) = 110 mm = 0.110 m
- Clearance Volume (V_c) = 60 cm³ = 60 × 10⁻⁶ m³
- P₁ = 1.013 bar (atmospheric)
- Peak Pressure (P₂) = 9.5 bar
- Engine speed (N) = 1500 rpm
- Motoring torque (Tₘ) = 8 N·m

**Step 1 — Swept Volume:**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 (0.110) = 5.529 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Total Volume at BDC:**

$$V_1 = V_d + V_c = 5.529 \times 10^{-4} + 60 \times 10^{-6} = 6.129 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio:**

$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{6.129 \times 10^{-4}}{60 \times 10^{-6}} = 10.22$$

**Step 4 — Polytropic Index (using logarithms):**

$$n = \frac{\log(9.5 / 1.013)}{\log(10.22)} = \frac{\log(9.378)}{\log(10.22)} = \frac{0.9721}{1.0094} = 0.963$$

> ⚠️ This is an unusual (low) value of n because peak pressure 9.5 bar is low for r = 10. The result indicates leakage or worn rings. A healthy engine should give n ≈ 1.30.

**Step 5 — Compression Work per Cycle:**

$$W_c = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} = \frac{(1.013 \times 10^{5})(6.129 \times 10^{-4}) - (9.5 \times 10^{5})(60 \times 10^{-6})}{0.963 - 1}$$

Convert to SI (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa):

$$W_c = \frac{62.09 - 57.00}{-0.037} = \frac{5.09}{-0.037} = -137.6 \, J$$

The negative sign indicates work is done **on the gas** (positive compression work as per sign convention).

**Step 6 — Friction Power:**

$$F_P = \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 8}{60} = 1256.6 \, W \approx 1.257 \, kW$$

**Step 7 — FMEP:**

$$\text{FMEP} = \frac{2\pi \times 8}{5.529 \times 10^{-4}} = 90894 \, Pa = 0.909 \, bar$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Petrol vs Diesel

| Parameter | Petrol (SI) Engine | Diesel (CI) Engine | Ratio (Diesel/Petrol) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Compression Ratio (r) | 8 – 10 | 16 – 20 | ≈ 2× |
| Peak Pressure (P₂) | 9 – 12 bar | 30 – 40 bar | ≈ 3.5× |
| Polytropic Index (n) | 1.30 – 1.35 | 1.32 – 1.38 | ≈ 1.02 |
| Peak Temperature | 300 – 350 °C | 550 – 700 °C | ≈ 2× |
| FMEP (typical) | 0.8 – 1.2 bar | 1.0 – 1.5 bar | ≈ 1.2× |
| Mechanical Efficiency | 0.85 – 0.92 | 0.80 – 0.88 | < 1 |

## 3.5 KTU Examiner's Matrix — Common Calculation Templates

| Quantity to Find | Required Inputs | Master Formula |
|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| Compression Ratio | V_d, V_c | $r = (V_d + V_c)/V_c$ |
| Polytropic Index n | P₁, P₂, r | $n = \log(P_2/P_1)/\log(r)$ |
| Peak Pressure | P₁, r, n | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{n}$ |
| Compression Work | P₁, V₁, P₂, V₂, n | $W_c = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2)/(n-1)$ |
| Friction Power | N, Tₘ | $F_P = 2\pi N T_m / 60$ |
| FMEP | Tₘ, V_d | $\text{FMEP} = 2\pi T_m / V_d$ |
| IMEP | P_θ diagram area, V_d | $\text{IMEP} = \text{Area}/V_d$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Compression Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains Supply] --> B[Variac Voltage Regulator]
    B --> C[Three Phase AC Motor]
    C -- Coupling Shaft --> D[Test Engine SI or CI]
    D --> E[Dead Weight Pressure Indicator]
    D --> F[Rotating Cylinder P theta Indicator]
    G[Electronic Tachometer] --> D
    H[Spring Balance Left] --> C
    I[Spring Balance Right] --> C
    J[Stopwatch for Speed] --> G
    K[Indicator Diagram Output] --> L[Planimeter for Area]
    L --> M[IMEP and FMEP Calculation]
    M --> N[Tabulation and Result]

    style A fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B
    style B fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#B8860B
    style C fill:#A0E7A0,stroke:#2E8B57
    style D fill:#FF9999,stroke:#B22222
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style F fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
    style I fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
    style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
    style K fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C
    style M fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
```

## 4.2 P-θ Diagram Shape Comparison (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start of Compression P1 approx 1 bar] --> P1[Rise along Polytropic Curve n gt 1]
    P1 --> P2A[Petrol Engine Peak approx 9 to 12 bar at TDC]
    P1 --> P2B[Diesel Engine Peak approx 30 to 40 bar at TDC]
    P2A --> P3A[Sharp fall during Power Stroke]
    P2B --> P3B[Sharp fall during Expansion Stroke]
    P3A --> P4A[Exhaust Stroke near Atmospheric]
    P3B --> P4B[Exhaust Stroke near Atmospheric]
    P4A --> P0
    P4B --> P0

    style P0 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#000
    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style P2A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style P2B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style P3A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style P3B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style P4A fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
    style P4B fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of Data Reduction in the Compression Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Indicator Diagram P theta] --> B[Measure Peak Pressure P2]
    A --> C[Measure Compression Curve Slope]
    B --> D[Compute Compression Ratio r]
    C --> E[Compute Polytropic Index n]
    D --> F[Apply Polytropic Law Verification]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Compression Work Wc]
    G --> H[Compute Friction Power Fp]
    H --> I[Compute FMEP]
    I --> J[Compare Petrol vs Diesel Result Table]
    J --> K[Plot Bar Chart of FMEP vs Speed]

    style A fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000
    style B fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00
    style C fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00
    style D fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B
    style E fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B
    style F fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082
    style G fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082
    style H fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22
    style I fill:#F0FFF0,stroke:#228B22
    style J fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
    style K fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Architecture of the Test Bed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER_SOURCE[Power Source Block]
        PS1[Three Phase Induction Motor 2 to 5 HP]
        PS2[Starter and DOL Switch]
    end

    subgraph TRANSMISSION[Transmission Block]
        TR1[Flexible Coupling]
        TR2[Reduction Gearbox Optional]
    end

    subgraph TEST_ENGINE[Test Engine Block]
        TE1[Single Cylinder SI or CI Engine]
        TE2[Fuel Tank with Cut Off Valve]
        TE3[Pressure Indicator Port on Cylinder Head]
    end

    subgraph INSTRUMENTATION[Instrumentation Block]
        IN1[Pressure Transducer or Dead Weight Gauge]
        IN2[Optical Tachometer]
        IN3[Digital Stopwatch]
        IN4[Thermocouple for Cylinder Temperature]
    end

    subgraph DATA_ACQ[Data Acquisition Block]
        DA1[PC with DAQ Card]
        DA2[Software Scope and Logger]
    end

    PS1 --> TR1 --> TE1
    PS2 --> PS1
    TE2 --> TE1
    TE1 --> TE3 --> IN1 --> DA1 --> DA2
    IN2 --> DA1
    IN3 --> DA1
    IN4 --> DA1

    style PS1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
    style PS2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
    style TR1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style TR2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style TE1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style TE2 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style TE3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000
    style IN1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style IN2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style IN3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style IN4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style DA1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
    style DA2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define compression ratio. Why is the compression ratio of a diesel engine higher than that of a petrol engine?**

**Model Answer:**
The compression ratio is defined as the ratio of the total cylinder volume (clearance volume + swept volume) to the clearance volume:

$$r = \frac{V_c + V_d}{V_c}$$

A diesel engine has a higher compression ratio (14:1 to 22:1) compared to a petrol engine (6:1 to 10:1) because diesel relies on **auto-ignition** caused by the high temperature of compressed air (above 250 °C). To achieve this temperature, the air must be compressed to a much smaller volume. A petrol engine, on the other hand, uses a spark plug for ignition, and a high compression ratio would cause **detonation / knocking** of the air-fuel mixture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "diesel fuel is heavier" or "diesel needs more power". The correct reasoning is purely **thermodynamic** — the air must reach the auto-ignition temperature.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the motoring test of an I.C. engine? What is its purpose?**

**Model Answer:**
The motoring test (or compression test) is performed by driving the engine externally using an electric motor **without supplying any fuel**, so the engine acts as an air compressor and a friction machine.

**Purposes:**
1. To determine the **frictional power** and **friction mean effective pressure (FMEP)** of the engine.
2. To measure the **peak compression pressure** at the end of the compression stroke.
3. To verify the **polytropic index of compression**.
4. To provide baseline data for the **Morse test** used in multi-cylinder engines.
5. To assess the **mechanical condition** of the engine (rings, valves, bearings).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the experimental procedure to conduct a compression test on a single-cylinder petrol engine. List the instruments used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 85 mm, stroke of 110 mm, and clearance volume of 70 cm³. During the motoring test, the peak compression pressure recorded is 9.8 bar. Calculate:
1. The compression ratio.
2. The polytropic index of compression (take P₁ = 1.013 bar).
3. The compression work per cycle. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

The experimental setup includes:
- A **single-cylinder petrol engine** coupled to a **3-phase induction motor** through a flexible coupling.
- A **dead-weight pressure gauge** or **rotating cylinder indicator** fitted in the cylinder head.
- A **tachometer** for engine speed measurement.
- A **spring balance** for motoring torque.

**Procedure:**
1. Start the electric motor and warm up the engine to operating temperature.
2. Cut off the fuel supply completely.
3. Vary the speed using the variac and record:
   - Engine speed (N) in rpm
   - Motoring torque (Tₘ) from the spring balance
   - Peak compression pressure (P₂) from the indicator
4. Take 4 – 5 sets of readings.
5. Plot the P–θ diagram and tabulate the results.
6. Calculate the friction power, FMEP, and verify the polytropic index.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- D = 85 mm = 0.085 m
- L = 110 mm = 0.110 m
- V_c = 70 cm³ = 70 × 10⁻⁶ m³
- P₂ = 9.8 bar
- P₁ = 1.013 bar

**Step 1 — Swept Volume:**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.085)^2 (0.110) = 6.241 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Total Volume:**

$$V_1 = V_d + V_c = 6.241 \times 10^{-4} + 70 \times 10^{-6} = 6.941 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$$

**Step 3 — Compression Ratio:**

$$r = \frac{6.941 \times 10^{-4}}{70 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.92 \approx 9.92 : 1$$

**Step 4 — Polytropic Index:**

$$n = \frac{\log(9.8 / 1.013)}{\log(9.92)} = \frac{\log(9.674)}{\log(9.92)} = \frac{0.9857}{0.9965} = 0.989$$

This is a low value of n, suggesting either heat loss or leakage (n should be ~ 1.32 for a healthy engine).

**Step 5 — Compression Work per Cycle (using n = 1.32 assumed for healthy engine):**

$$W_c = \frac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n - 1} = \frac{(1.013 \times 10^{5})(6.941 \times 10^{-4}) - (9.8 \times 10^{5})(70 \times 10^{-6})}{1.32 - 1}$$

$$W_c = \frac{70.31 - 68.60}{0.32} = \frac{1.71}{0.32} = 5.34 \, J$$

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare the compression ratio, peak pressure, and compression work of petrol and diesel engines. Why does a diesel engine develop higher compression pressure even at the same swept volume? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a motoring test on a single-cylinder 4-stroke diesel engine, the following data were recorded at 1800 rpm:
- Motoring torque = 12 N·m
- Bore = 90 mm, Stroke = 100 mm
- Peak compression pressure = 35 bar
- Polytropic index n = 1.35

Calculate:
1. Friction power of the engine.
2. FMEP.
3. Compression work per cycle (V_c = 50 cm³). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

| Parameter | Petrol Engine | Diesel Engine |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| Compression Ratio | 6 – 10 | 14 – 22 |
| Peak Pressure | 7 – 12 bar | 30 – 40 bar |
| Compression Work per Cycle | Lower (1 – 10 J) | Higher (15 – 30 J) |

**Reason for higher compression in diesel:** A diesel engine uses **compression ignition**. The air must be compressed to a small volume so that its temperature rises above the auto-ignition temperature of diesel (≈ 250 °C). The relationship $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{n-1}$ shows that a higher r gives a higher T₂. A petrol engine cannot use such high r because it would cause **knocking** of the air-fuel mixture before the spark occurs.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Swept Volume:**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.090)^2 (0.100) = 6.362 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Friction Power:**

$$F_P = \frac{2\pi N T_m}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800 \times 12}{60} = 2261.9 \, W \approx 2.26 \, kW$$

**Step 3 — FMEP:**

$$\text{FMEP} = \frac{2\pi T_m}{V_d} = \frac{2\pi \times 12}{6.362 \times 10^{-4}} = 118530 \, Pa = 1.185 \, bar$$

**Step 4 — Compression Work per Cycle:**

$$V_1 = V_d + V_c = 6.362 \times 10^{-4} + 50 \times 10^{-6} = 6.862 \times 10^{-4} \, m^3$$

$$W_c = \frac{(1.013 \times 10^{5})(6.862 \times 10^{-4}) - (35 \times 10^{5})(50 \times 10^{-6})}{1.35 - 1}$$

$$W_c = \frac{69.51 - 175.00}{0.35} = \frac{-105.49}{0.35} = -301.4 \, J$$

The magnitude is **301.4 J per cycle** (work done on the gas).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students forget that **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** while substituting into the W_c formula. If pressures are left in bar and volumes in m³, the unit mismatch will make the answer wrong by a factor of 10⁵.
> 2. The negative sign in W_c is **not an error** — it simply indicates work is done *on* the gas. Examiners expect students to state this explicitly.
> 3. The log in $n = \log(P_2/P_1)/\log(r)$ is **base-independent** (10 or e) — students waste time converting. Both give the same numerical value.
> 4. The compression work of a petrol engine (≈ 5 J) is much smaller than that of a diesel engine (≈ 300 J). If a student gets a similar magnitude for both, the calculation is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Must remember for KTU ESE & Lab Viva)**

- **Definition:** The compression / motoring test is performed **without fuel** by driving the engine with an external motor.
- **Polytropic Law:** $P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$ — applicable for the actual compression process.
- **Compression Ratio:** $r = (V_d + V_c)/V_c$ — petrol 8–10, diesel 16–20.
- **Peak Pressure Formula:** $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{n}$.
- **Polytropic Index Formula:** $n = \log(P_2/P_1)/\log(r)$ — typically 1.30–1.38.
- **Compression Work:** $W_c = (P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2)/(n-1)$ — negative sign means work is done **on** the gas.
- **Friction Power:** $F_P = 2\pi N T_m / 60$ — derived from the motoring torque.
- **FMEP:** $\text{FMEP} = 2\pi T_m / V_d$ — independent of speed in the formula.
- **Petrol vs Diesel:** Diesel has roughly **2× compression ratio, 3.5× peak pressure, 2× temperature**, and **10–60× compression work**.
- **Practical Indicators Used:** Dead-weight pressure gauge, rotating cylinder indicator, modern piezoelectric pressure transducer.
- **Applications:** (i) Workshop diagnosis of engine health; (ii) Friction data for **Morse test**; (iii) Validation of engine simulation software; (iv) BS-VI / Euro-7 emission compliance.
- **Key Instruments in Lab:** Tachometer, spring balance, stopwatch, dead-weight pressure gauge, indicator drum with paper.
- **Why Compression Test is important for KTU Lab:** It satisfies the **Course Outcome CO2** ("Perform experiments on I.C. engines and analyse performance parameters") and **CO3** ("Apply the concept of compression ratio to engine design").

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disassembling Cylinder Head, Decarbonizing and Study of Valve Actuating Mechanisms - Checking/Setting Correct Valve Timing and Valve Clearance Adjustment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Disassembling Cylinder Head, Decarbonizing and Study of Valve Actuating Mechanisms - Checking/Setting Correct Valve Timing and Valve Clearance Adjustment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Cylinder Head** is the most complex and heavily loaded component of an internal combustion engine, bolted to the top of the cylinder block to seal the combustion chamber from above. It houses the **valve train mechanism**, spark plug (SI engines) or injector ports (CI engines), and coolant passages. The cylinder head must withstand peak cylinder pressures of **40–80 bar** and gas temperatures exceeding **1500 °C** at the flame front, while maintaining dimensional stability for proper valve sealing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment trains the student to dismantle the cylinder head, identify every part of the **valve actuating mechanism (OHV / SOHC / DOHC)**, decarbonize the combustion chamber, verify and adjust **valve timing** (IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC) and reset **valve clearance (lash)** per manufacturer specification.

**Valve Actuating Mechanism:** A kinematic linkage that transfers rotary motion from the **camshaft** (driven by the crankshaft through a timing belt/chain/gear) to the poppet valves, opening them at the correct instant and for the correct duration relative to piston position.

**Decarbonizing:** The process of mechanically or chemically removing **hard carbon deposits (coke)** from the combustion chamber walls, piston crown, valve heads, valve stems, and exhaust ports — deposits formed due to incomplete combustion and oil blow-by.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Valve Timing:** The precise synchronization, measured in degrees of crankshaft rotation, at which each intake and exhaust valve opens and closes relative to TDC and BDC of the corresponding piston stroke.

**Valve Clearance (Lash):** The small measured gap between the back of the cam lobe and the valve train follower (rocker arm tip / tappet / shim) when the cam lobe is on its base circle. This gap compensates for **thermal expansion** of valve train components at operating temperature.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Picture)

Imagine a **bicycle pump connected to a seesaw**. Every time you push the pump handle down, a small valve opens on the other end of the seesaw, lets some air in, and then snaps shut before air escapes back. That little valve must open and close at **exactly the right moment** in the pump cycle — too early and pressure is wasted; too late and you don't get enough air in.

Your engine's cylinder head works the same way, except the "seesaw" is the **camshaft lobe** turning continuously, and the pump is the **piston moving up and down**. The **valve is the small door** that opens to let fresh air-fuel mixture in (intake) and lets burnt gases out (exhaust). The **camshaft turns at exactly half the speed of the crankshaft** because the piston needs two full turns (one for intake-compression, one for power-exhaust) before the same valve should open again.

Now imagine that door has been opening and closing **millions of times** while very hot gases brush past it. It gets **gummed up with black soot (carbon)** and the hinges get a tiny bit looser as everything heats up and expands. We need to:
1. **Open the door assembly** (dismantle the cylinder head)
2. **Scrape off the soot** (decarbonize)
3. **Make sure the door opens at the exact right moment** (check/set valve timing)
4. **Re-tighten the hinge so the door doesn't stick when hot** (adjust valve clearance)

> [!TIP]
> **The Three Golden Specs of Every Cylinder Head Job:**
> 1. **Camshaft-to-crankshaft synchronization** (timing — must be in degrees)
> 2. **Valve-to-lobe gap** (clearance — usually **0.15–0.30 mm** cold)
> 3. **Head bolt tightening pattern and torque** (typically **30–80 Nm** in stages)

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical SI Engine | Typical CI (Diesel) Engine |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Combustion Pressure | **30–60 bar** | **60–80 bar** |
| Peak Gas Temperature | **2000–2500 °C** | **1800–2200 °C** |
| Valve Clearance (Intake, cold) | **0.15–0.25 mm** | **0.20–0.30 mm** |
| Valve Clearance (Exhaust, cold) | **0.25–0.35 mm** | **0.30–0.40 mm** |
| Valve Spring Free Length | **40–55 mm** | **45–60 mm** |
| Valve Seat Angle | **45°** (some 30°/60° multi-angle) | **45°** |
| Decarbonizing Soak Time | **15–30 min** | **20–45 min** |
| Head Bolt Torque (4-cyl) | **60–90 Nm** | **80–120 Nm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Operational Logic of the Experiment

### Stage 1 — Cylinder Head Disassembly
- Drain engine coolant, disconnect HT leads, fuel lines, exhaust manifold, and intake manifold.
- Remove rocker cover (valve cover), rocker shaft assembly (OHV) or cam cover (OHC).
- Unbolt head bolts in the **reverse of the tightening sequence** (outside-in) to avoid warping.
- Lift the head straight up; **never pry** sideways — the aluminum alloy warps easily.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A warped head (flatness > **0.05 mm / 100 mm**) will blow head gasket. Always check with a **straight-edge and feeler gauge** before reassembly.

### Stage 2 — Component Identification (Valve Actuating Mechanism)
- **OHV (Overhead Valve) — Pushrod type:** Camshaft in block, pushrods, rocker arms on a single shaft.
- **SOHC (Single Overhead Cam):** Camshaft in head, acts directly or through rockers on valves.
- **DOHC (Double Overhead Cam):** Separate camshafts for intake and exhaust, often with direct-acting bucket tappets.
- **DVVT / VVT-i:** Continuously variable cam phasing — adds a phaser between cam and cam drive.

### Stage 3 — Decarbonizing (Decoking)
- **Mechanical:** Wire brush, scraper, soft abrasive pad on combustion chamber walls and piston crown.
- **Chemical:** Soak in **carbon solvent (kerosene, decarbonizing fluid, or 10% ammonia solution)** for 15–30 min.
- **Pneumatic:** Walnut shell / soda blasting for delicate aluminum parts.
- **Never use sharp tools on the piston crown** — the carbon layer actually insulates; aggressive scraping can score the aluminum.

### Stage 4 — Valve Timing Verification & Clearance Adjustment
- Align **camshaft and crankshaft timing marks** at TDC of compression stroke.
- Check that the **intake valve opens** at the manufacturer-specified **BTDC** value.
- Adjust clearance via **screw-and-locknut** (OHV rocker), **shim selection** (bucket tappet), or **eccentric roller** adjustment.
- Recheck after a **warmed-up idle** because thermal expansion closes the gap by **0.05–0.10 mm**.

---

## 2.2 Valve Timing — The Critical Angles

The complete valve timing diagram identifies **four events** per valve per cycle, all expressed in **degrees of crankshaft rotation**:

- **IVO (Intake Valve Opens)** — typically **5°–20° BTDC** of intake stroke.
- **IVC (Intake Valve Closes)** — typically **40°–70° ABDC** of intake stroke (ram-effect filling).
- **EVO (Exhaust Valve Opens)** — typically **40°–60° BBDC** of power stroke.
- **EVC (Exhaust Valve Closes)** — typically **5°–15° ATDC** of exhaust stroke.
- **Valve Overlap** = IVO (as BTDC) + EVC (as ATDC) — typically **5°–25°** of overlap when both valves are simultaneously off their seats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Overlap** is intentional: inertia of fresh charge and exhaust gases continues the scavenging process. High-performance engines have larger overlap; low-speed diesels have **zero or negative overlap** (asymmetric or desmodromic designs).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Rule | Symbolic Form | Numerical Example | Engineering Use |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Cam follows crank at half speed | $N_{cam} = \dfrac{N_{crank}}{2}$ | 2000 rpm crank → 1000 rpm cam | Basic gear/pulley ratio |
| Valve lift from cam profile | $L = L_{max} \cdot \left[1 - \cos\theta\right]$ for simple harmonic | $L_{max} = 8$ mm typical | Lift at any cam angle $\theta$ |
| Valve opening duration (in crank deg) | $\alpha_{total} = 180° + IVO_{BTDC} + EVC_{ATDC}$ | $180 + 12 + 10 = 202°$ | Defines cam lobe base circle width |
| Mean piston speed | $\bar{V}_p = \dfrac{2 \cdot L_s \cdot N}{60}$ | $L_s = 0.08$ m, $N = 3000$ → $\bar{V}_p = 8$ m/s | Engine breathing limit |
| Cam profile (parabolic flank) | $y = k \cdot x^2$ for ramp | ramp lift over ramp angle | Velocity / acceleration control |
| Thermal growth of valve stem | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6}/°C$, $L_0 = 0.25$ m, $\Delta T = 200°C$ → $\Delta L = 0.6$ mm | Why valve clearance exists |
| Head bolt tightening torque | $T = K \cdot d \cdot F$ | $K = 0.2$ (nut factor), $d = 0.012$ m, $F = 40000$ N → $T = 96$ Nm | Clamp load estimation |
| Volumetric efficiency (timing effect) | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$ | well-tuned: 85–95%; mistimed: 60–75% | Why timing matters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** Valve clearance values are quoted **COLD** by most manufacturers and **HOT** for some (Honda, Toyota). Always follow the workshop manual. Using HOT values when COLD is specified will cause **valves to remain open at operating temperature** → burnt valves and loss of compression.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emission Control:** Correct valve timing directly governs EGR overlap, HC and CO tailpipe emissions. A 5° timing error can increase HC by **15–20%**.
- **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** Properly set timing improves BSFC by **3–5%** — meaningful for fleet operators.
- **Engine Longevity:** Correct clearance prevents **valve seat recession** (diesel) and **stretched timing chains** (over-retarded intake).
- **Field Diagnostics:** A simple timing-light + degree wheel on a disassembled head is a standard garage test to diagnose worn timing chains, slipped sprockets, or stretched belts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Cam Duration and Crank Angle

The camshaft rotates at half the crankshaft speed. Therefore, if a cam lobe profile occupies an angular width $\phi_{cam}$ (in cam degrees), the corresponding crank angle $\alpha_{crank}$ is given by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{crank} &= 2 \cdot \phi_{cam} \\
\phi_{cam} &= \dfrac{\alpha_{crank}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The crankshaft makes one revolution ($360°$) in the time the cam makes half a revolution ($180°$).
2. Therefore every cam degree corresponds to exactly **2 crank degrees**.
3. If a manufacturer specifies an intake event spanning **$220°$ of crank rotation**, the cam lobe base width is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{cam} &= \dfrac{220°}{2} \\
\phi_{cam} &= 110° \text{ of cam rotation}
\end{aligned}$$

This is why cam manufacturers always quote "duration @ 0.050 inch lift" in **crank degrees** — it's the industry-standard reference.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Valve Clearance Accounting for Thermal Expansion

The valve clearance specified by the manufacturer must be large enough to absorb the differential thermal growth of the **valve stem (heated by combustion)** versus the **cylinder head / rocker (cooled by coolant)**.

$$\Delta L_{valve} = \alpha_{steel} \cdot L_{stem} \cdot \Delta T_{valve}$$

$$\Delta L_{head} = \alpha_{Al} \cdot L_{head\,path} \cdot \Delta T_{head}$$

$$\text{Required clearance} = \Delta L_{valve} - \Delta L_{head} + \text{operating margin}$$

**Numerical walkthrough (typical motorcycle / small car):**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{stem} &= 0.090 \text{ m} \\
\alpha_{steel} &= 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C} \\
\Delta T_{valve} &= 400°\text{C (intake stem, hot soak)} \\
\Delta L_{valve} &= 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.090 \times 400 \\
\Delta L_{valve} &= 0.000414 \text{ m} = 0.414 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{head\,path} &= 0.040 \text{ m (rocker to valve tip)} \\
\alpha_{Al} &= 23 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C} \\
\Delta T_{head} &= 120°\text{C} \\
\Delta L_{head} &= 23 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.040 \times 120 \\
\Delta L_{head} &= 0.000110 \text{ m} = 0.110 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net gap closure at hot} &= 0.414 - 0.110 \\
&= 0.304 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

So the **cold clearance** is set to about **0.30 mm** to leave zero clearance and full seating at operating temperature — consistent with a real manufacturer spec of **0.28–0.32 mm cold intake**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Valve Timing Diagram Geometry (Overlapping Region)

The valve overlap angle $\beta$ is the sum of two advance angles:

$$\beta = \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC} + \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC}$$

**Example:** $\theta_{IVO} = 12°$ BTDC, $\theta_{EVC} = 10°$ ATDC:

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta &= 12° + 10° \\
\beta &= 22° \text{ of crank rotation}
\end{aligned}$$

The crank rotates through $\beta$ degrees while **both valves are off their seats** at the end of the exhaust stroke / beginning of the intake stroke.

The corresponding **cam overlap** is half this:

$$\beta_{cam} = \dfrac{22°}{2} = 11° \text{ of cam rotation}$$

This is the dimension that cam designers must shape into the **base circle of the cam lobe**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Engine Families

| Engine Family | Cam Drive | Valve Clearance Adjustment | Typical Cold Clearance (mm) | Timing Mark Location |
|---------------|-----------|----------------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Maruti 800 (F8D) | Chain, OHV | Screw & locknut on rocker | 0.15 I / 0.25 E | Crank pulley + cam gear |
| Hyundai Santro (G4HG) | Belt, SOHC | Shim-on-bucket (select shim) | 0.20 I / 0.25 E | Cam gear + crank gear |
| Toyota Innova (2GD-FTV) | Chain, DOHC | Lash adjuster (auto) | 0 (hydraulic) | Crank gear + both cam gears |
| Royal Enfield 350 (OHV) | Gear, OHV | Screw & locknut on rocker | 0.10 I / 0.15 E | TDC mark on flywheel + cam gear |
| Honda Activa (110cc) | Chain, SOHC | Shim-under-bucket | 0.10 I / 0.10 E | "T" mark on flywheel + cam sprocket |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Most modern engines use **hydraulic lash adjusters (HLA)** that require **ZERO manual clearance adjustment** — the oil-filled plunger self-compensates for thermal growth. KTU lab engines (older training rigs) are mostly **OHV with screw adjustment** because they are educational, robust, and visualize the principle clearly.

---

## 3.5 Decarbonizing — Quantitative Approach

The rate of carbon buildup is roughly proportional to oil consumption and blow-by. A simple empirical model:

$$m_{carbon} = k \cdot BSFC \cdot \eta_{blow-by} \cdot t_{operating}$$

Where:
- $m_{carbon}$ = mass of carbon deposited (g)
- $k$ = empirical constant ≈ $0.05$ g per gram of burnt oil
- $BSFC$ = brake specific fuel consumption (g/kWh)
- $\eta_{blow-by}$ = blow-by fraction (~0.1% of air mass)
- $t_{operating}$ = operating time (h)

A typical small car accumulates **5–15 g of carbon per 10,000 km** in the combustion chamber. Above **30 g**, decoke is mandatory to prevent pre-ignition and knock.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Valve Actuating Mechanism (OHV Pushrod Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crankshaft] --> B[Timing Gear]
    B --> C[Camshaft in Block]
    C --> D[Cam Lobe Lifts Tappet]
    D --> E[Pushrod Rises]
    E --> F[Rocker Arm Pivots on Shaft]
    F --> G[Valve Stem Pushed Down]
    G --> H[Valve Opens Against Spring Force]
    H --> I[Combustion Chamber Filled / Emptied]
    I --> J[Exhaust Manifold]
    I --> K[Intake Manifold]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DOHC Layout (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph H[Cylinder Head]
        IC[Intake Camshaft] --> IV[Intake Valves x4]
        EC[Exhaust Camshaft] --> EV[Exhaust Valves x4]
        S1[Spark Plug / Injector] --> CH[Combustion Chamber]
        IV --> CH
        EV --> CH
    end
    CRK[Crankshaft] --> CBT[Chain / Belt]
    CBT --> IC
    CBT --> EC
    CH --> PIST[Piston]
    PIST --> CON[Connecting Rod]
    CON --> CRK
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Valve Timing Diagram (Conceptual Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Intake Stroke]
        S1A[IVO 12 deg BTDC] --> S1B[Piston Descends]
        S1B --> S1C[IVC 45 deg ABDC]
    end
    subgraph S2[Compression]
        S2A[Piston Rises] --> S2B[TDC Compression]
    end
    subgraph S3[Power Stroke]
        S3A[Ignition] --> S3B[EVO 50 deg BBDC]
    end
    subgraph S4[Exhaust Stroke]
        S4A[Piston Rises Exhaust] --> S4B[EVC 10 deg ATDC]
        S4B --> S4C[Overlap Region]
    end
    S1C --> S2A
    S2B --> S3A
    S3B --> S4A
    S4C --> S1A
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Procedure Flowchart — Cylinder Head Decarbonizing & Reassembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Engine Cool for 4 Hours] --> P2[Disconnect Battery, HT Leads, Hoses]
    P2 --> P3[Remove Rocker Cover / Cam Cover]
    P3 --> P4[Loosen Head Bolts in Reverse Pattern]
    P4 --> P5[Lift Head and Place on Bench]
    P5 --> P6[Identify All Valve Train Components]
    P6 --> P7[Inspect Valves Seats and Springs]
    P7 --> P8[Soak in Decarbonizing Fluid 20 min]
    P8 --> P9[Wire Brush Combustion Chamber and Piston Crown]
    P9 --> P10[Lap Valves with Grinding Paste]
    P10 --> P11[Check Head Flatness with Straight Edge]
    P11 --> P12[Install New Head Gasket]
    P12 --> P13[Set Crank to TDC Compression]
    P13 --> P14[Align Cam Timing Marks]
    P14 --> P15[Set Valve Clearance to Spec]
    P15 --> P16[Torque Head Bolts in 3 Stages]
    P16 --> P17[Reassemble and Test Run]
```

---

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Clearance Adjustment Methods (Block Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TYPEA[Screw Locknut Type OHV]
        A1[Loosen Locknut] --> A2[Turn Adjuster Screw]
        A2 --> A3[Insert Feeler Gauge]
        A3 --> A4[Hold Screw and Torque Locknut]
    end
    subgraph TYPEB[Shim Under Bucket Type]
        B1[Depress Valve with Tool] --> B2[Remove Old Shim]
        B2 --> B3[Measure Shim Thickness]

    end

    end

        D2 --> D3[Oil Fills Plunger Automatically]

    end
```
