# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is valve overlap? Why is it provided in a four-stroke petrol engine? What are its disadvantages at low engine speeds?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Valve overlap is the angular interval in degrees of crank rotation during which **both intake and exhaust valves remain open simultaneously**, occurring at the end of the exhaust stroke and start of the intake stroke. It is computed as:
$$\text{Overlap} = \theta_{IVO}^{BTDC} + \theta_{EVC}^{ATDC}$$
- **Purpose (1 mark):** Overlap exploits the **inertia of exhaust gases** leaving the cylinder and the **ram effect of fresh incoming charge** to improve volumetric efficiency. It also helps scavenge residual burnt gases from the combustion chamber.
- **Disadvantage at low speed (1 mark):** At low engine speeds, gas inertia is low, so the fresh charge can **back-flow out of the open exhaust valve**, causing poor idle quality, increased **HC and CO emissions**, and unstable combustion. This is why engines with high overlap have poor low-end torque.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is valve clearance necessary? What happens if it is set too tight or too loose?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Why necessary (1 mark):** Valve clearance compensates for **differential thermal expansion** of the valve stem (hot, ~400°C) and the cylinder head / rocker (cooler, ~120°C). Without clearance, the valve would not seat properly when hot.
- **If too tight (1 mark):** Valve remains slightly open at operating temperature → combustion gases leak past the seat → **valve burns**, loss of compression, misfire, and possible piston damage.
- **If too loose (1 mark):** Cam profile impact is damped by the gap → **reduced effective valve lift**, delayed opening, noisy valve train, accelerated cam and tappet wear, and rough running.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **OHV valve actuating mechanism** and trace the motion path from the crankshaft to the valve. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for checking and setting valve timing** on a four-cylinder petrol engine, including the use of timing marks, a degree wheel, and a timing light. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — OHV Valve Actuating Mechanism (7 marks)

**Working Mechanism (3 marks):**
In an **Overhead Valve (OHV)** arrangement, the camshaft is mounted inside the **cylinder block**, beside or above the crankshaft. The crankshaft drives the camshaft through a pair of **timing gears** at a **2:1 speed ratio** (crank:camshaft = 2:1). As each **cam lobe** rotates, it lifts the **tappet (follower)** vertically; this upward motion is transferred through a **pushrod** to a **rocker arm** pivoted on a rocker shaft. The rocker arm converts the pushrod's upward motion into a **downward push on the valve stem**, opening the valve against the **return spring**. When the cam lobe rotates past the tappet, the spring closes the valve.

**Component Roles (4 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Camshaft** | Converts rotary motion to a precisely timed reciprocating motion via its lobes |
| **Timing gears** | Synchronize camshaft with crankshaft at 2:1 ratio |
| **Tappet (follower)** | Slides up/down on cam lobe, follows its profile |
| **Pushrod** | Transmits motion from tappet (in block) to rocker (in head) across the long distance |
| **Rocker arm** | Pivots on rocker shaft, reverses direction of force, pushes valve open |
| **Rocker shaft** | Provides pivot axis; sometimes carries oil to rockers |
| **Valve spring** | Returns valve to closed position when cam lobe allows |
| **Valve stem seal** | Prevents oil from being sucked down valve guide into combustion chamber |

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram of OHV).**

---

#### Solution to (b) — Procedure for Checking & Setting Valve Timing (7 marks)

**Step-by-step Procedure:**

1. **Preparation (1 mark):** Bring engine to TDC of **No. 1 cylinder compression stroke**. Verify by feeling compression with thumb over spark plug hole or by aligning the **TDC mark on the flywheel / crank pulley** with the pointer on the timing cover. Both **intake and exhaust valves of No. 1 cylinder must be closed**.

2. **Inspection of Timing Marks (1 mark):** Remove the timing cover. Observe the **crank timing mark** and the **camshaft timing mark**. On most engines, when No. 1 piston is at TDC compression, the cam timing mark should align with the **cast-in pointer on the head/cover**. Reference the workshop manual for the specific engine.

3. **Use of Degree Wheel (2 marks):** Attach a **magnetic degree wheel** to the crank pulley. Fit a **pointer (dial indicator or TDC gauge)** to the engine block. Slowly rotate the crank **forward** (direction of normal rotation) by hand and observe when the intake valve of No. 1 begins to open. Read the degree wheel — this is the actual **IVO (Intake Valve Open) angle**.

4. **Comparison to Spec (1 mark):** Compare measured IVO with manufacturer spec (e.g., **12° BTDC**). If within ±2°, timing is correct. If not, the **timing chain/belt has stretched** or the **camshaft sprocket is mis-indexed**.

5. **Adjustment (1 mark):** Loosen the **camshaft sprocket bolt**, rotate the cam relative to the chain to advance or retard the intake event, then re-torque. Recheck with degree wheel.

6. **Verification with Timing Light (1 mark):** Reconnect HT leads, start engine at idle. With a **timing light (stroboscope)**, aim at the crank timing marks. The mark should appear stationary and aligned with the pointer at the spec **BTDC value** (e.g., 10° BTDC at idle). If the advance curve is electronic, varying engine speed should cause the marks to move dynamically, confirming the **centrifugal and vacuum advance** is working.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **TDC compression** with **TDC exhaust stroke**. The flywheel mark looks the same at both! Always confirm with the **valve overlap check** — at TDC compression of No. 1, both intake and exhaust of No. 1 are closed; at TDC exhaust, the intake is just starting to open. A wrong reference gives a **180° error in timing interpretation**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **procedure for decarbonizing a cylinder head** in detail. List the tools, chemicals, and safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **two common methods of valve clearance adjustment**: (i) screw and locknut type, and (ii) shim type. State typical clearance values for a 4-cylinder petrol engine. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Decarbonizing Procedure (7 marks)

**Tools and Materials (2 marks):**
- Decarbonizing solvent (kerosene, commercially available **"Carbon Clean" / "Decoke" fluid**, or 10% ammoniated solution)
- Soft brass wire brush, nylon brush, wooden scraper
- Old toothbrush for valve stem guides
- Valve lapping tool with suction cup
- Valve lapping compound (fine grade, 240–400 grit)
- Compressed air line, lint-free cloth
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** rubber gloves, safety goggles, apron

**Step-by-Step Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Disassembly:** Remove cylinder head from engine as described in Section 2.1. Mark all components for reassembly.
2. **Visual Inspection:** Identify carbon buildup pattern. Heavy deposits on **piston crown and exhaust valves** indicate rich mixture or oil consumption. Light grey dust is normal.
3. **Chemical Soak:** Submerge the head in a **decarbonizing bath** for **15–30 minutes**. The solvent softens hard carbon, converting it to a sludgy film.
4. **Mechanical Cleaning:** Use **brass wire brush** on cylinder head combustion chamber walls, valve seats, and exhaust ports. Use **wooden scraper** (NOT steel) on the piston crown to avoid scoring the soft aluminum. Clean **valve stems** with a brush dipped in solvent to remove gum.
5. **Valve Lapping:** Apply a thin film of **valve lapping compound** to the valve seat face. Using the **lapping tool**, rotate the valve with light pressure in alternating half-turns for 2–3 minutes. A uniform **mat grey ring** of about 1.5–2 mm width on the valve face and seat indicates a good seal. Wash off all compound with solvent.
6. **Final Inspection:** Check that the valve seat width is even all around (no taper). Wipe dry and re-lubricate valve stems with assembly oil.

**Safety Precautions (1 mark):**
- Work in a **well-ventilated area**; decarbonizing solvents are toxic and flammable.
- Wear **chemical-resistant gloves and safety goggles** — solvents cause skin and eye irritation.
- **Dispose of used solvent and carbon sludge** as per local hazardous-waste rules; do not pour down the drain.
- Ensure all **compound is removed** before reassembly — any residue will rapidly destroy the valve and seat in service.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Valve Clearance Adjustment Methods (7 marks)

**Method 1: Screw-and-Locknut Type (used in OHV engines) (3 marks):**
- The rocker arm has a **threaded adjuster screw** at the pushrod end and is **locked** by a **locknut** (typically 10–13 mm).
- Procedure:
  1. Rotate crankshaft to TDC compression of the cylinder being adjusted (use **firing order**: 1-3-4-2 for most 4-cyl).
  2. Insert the correct thickness **feeler gauge** between the rocker arm tip and the valve stem tip.
  3. **Loosen the locknut.**
  4. Turn the **adjuster screw** until the feeler gauge slides with a slight, even drag.
  5. Hold the screw stationary and **torque the locknut** to spec (typically 8–15 Nm).
  6. **Recheck** the clearance after tightening — it usually tightens up slightly.

**Method 2: Shim Type (used in OHC engines) (3 marks):**
- The valve is opened by a **bucket tappet** that has either a **shim underneath** (under-bucket) or **above** (over-bucket) the bucket.
- The shim is a precisely sized disc, available in **increments of 0.025 mm or 0.05 mm**, in a thickness range of e.g. **1.20–2.40 mm**.
- Procedure:
  1. Bring the cam lobe of the valve to be adjusted to **base circle (lobe pointing away from tappet)**.
  2. Use a **shim removal tool** (a forked lever) to depress the bucket and expose the shim. For over-bucket, a special plier depresses the cam follower.
  3. **Remove and measure** the old shim with a **micrometer** or **shim gauge**.
  4. Calculate new shim thickness:
$$t_{new} = t_{old} - (\text{measured clearance} - \text{specified clearance})$$
  5. Install new shim, reassemble, and verify clearance with feeler gauge.

**Typical Clearance Values (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Petrol Engine (Cold) | Diesel Engine (Cold) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Intake valve | **0.15 – 0.25 mm** | **0.20 – 0.30 mm** |
| Exhaust valve | **0.25 – 0.35 mm** | **0.30 – 0.40 mm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write clearance values **without specifying** whether they are **hot or cold**. Always mention the condition, e.g., "0.20 mm cold, intake." A common practical mistake is **tightening the locknut with the feeler gauge still in place** — this pulls the adjuster screw and tightens the clearance. Always remove the gauge AFTER final locknut torque, and re-verify.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Cylinder head** carries valves, camshaft, rockers, springs, spark plug/injector, and must be flat to within **0.05 mm / 100 mm**; machined or replaced if warped.
- **Valve timing** is measured in **degrees of crank rotation** and includes IVO, IVC, EVO, EVC, and the **overlap** angle.
- **Camshaft speed = half crankshaft speed**; all cam angles in spec sheets can be converted: $\phi_{cam} = \alpha_{crank} / 2$.
- **Valve clearance** compensates for **differential thermal expansion** — exhaust valves always need **more clearance than intake** because they run hotter.
- **Decarbonizing** uses chemical soaking + mechanical brushing; never use sharp steel on aluminum pistons.
- **Timing check** uses TDC mark + degree wheel + timing light; a 1-tooth error on the cam sprocket = **~10–15° timing error** depending on engine.
- **Clearance adjustment methods:** Screw-locknut (OHV), shim-under-bucket or shim-over-bucket (OHC), and hydraulic self-adjusters (modern engines).
- **Cylinder head bolt tightening** is done in **3 incremental stages** (e.g., 30% → 70% → 100% of final torque) in a **spiral outside-in pattern** to prevent warping.
- **Valve overlap** improves breathing at high speed but degrades idle quality and emissions at low speed.
- **Head gasket** is a one-time-use part — always replace.
- **Hydraulic lash adjusters** require the engine to be at operating temperature, with oil pressure, to self-set; you **must not** manually compress them with the engine off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Fuel Injector and Fuel Pump - Testing the Pressure of Mechanical Diesel Injector, Spray Pattern, Adjusting of Injection Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing of Fuel Injector and Fuel Pump - Testing the Pressure of Mechanical Diesel Injector, Spray Pattern, Adjusting of Injection Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **mechanical diesel injector** is a precision-metering device used in Compression Ignition (CI) engines that delivers finely atomized diesel fuel into the combustion chamber at a precisely controlled high pressure, controlled timing, and defined spray pattern. The injector essentially consists of a **nozzle holder** (barrel assembly), a **spring-loaded needle valve**, a **pressure-adjusting shim**, and a **high-pressure fuel inlet connection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot**
> The **testing of a mechanical diesel injector** involves three primary diagnostic operations performed on a calibrated **Injector Nozzle Tester (Hand-Operated or Motorised Pump Test Bench)**:
> 1. **Opening Pressure Test (Pop Pressure Test)** – Verifies the static pressure at which the needle valve lifts off its seat.
> 2. **Spray Pattern Test** – Visually inspects the spray geometry, atomization, and symmetry.
> 3. **Chatter / Back Leakage Test** – Evaluates dynamic sealing and the seating condition of the needle valve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of the diesel injector like a **pressure-triggered garden spray nozzle with a calibrated safety valve**:

- The **spring** acts like your thumb pressing down on a spray trigger — the harder it is pressed (more tension), the higher the water pressure must rise before the nozzle opens.
- When the fuel pressure inside the injector overcomes the spring force, the **needle valve pops open** (this is the *opening pressure* or *pop pressure*).
- The fuel then sprays out in a defined **pattern** — like ink droplets leaving an atomizer. If the pattern looks broken, asymmetrical, or "wet," it indicates worn or damaged internals.
- The **shim** is a thin washer placed under the spring; adjusting it (adding/removing shims) changes the spring preload — and thus the opening pressure — just like turning a screw to tighten or loosen a clamp.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in an Engine:** Modern common-rail systems have replaced these mechanical injectors, but **the vast majority of KTU-syllabus diesel engines (CI engines in tractors, trucks, gensets)** still depend on the **conventional jerk-pump + mechanical injector** system (e.g., Bosch P, PE, PFR series). Hence testing these injectors is a *highly examinable* lab skill.

## 1.3 Key Components of a Mechanical Diesel Injector

| S.No | Component | Function |
|:----:|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | **Nozzle Body (Barrel)** | Houses the needle valve and contains the spray holes |
| 2 | **Needle Valve (Nozzle Needle)** | Sliding precision pin that opens/closes the spray orifices |
| 3 | **Nozzle Holder (Body)** | Contains the spring, shim, and high-pressure inlet |
| 4 | **Nozzle Spring** | Provides downward seating force on the needle |
| 5 | **Shim (Adjusting Washer)** | Sets spring preload → directly controls opening pressure |
| 6 | **Pressure Cap / Rocker Arm Connection** | Interface for pump cam-actuated fuel supply |
| 7 | **Overflow / Leak-back Port** | Returns excess fuel to tank via return line |
| 8 | **Nozzle Nut (Cap Nut)** | Clamps the nozzle to the holder |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (Industry Benchmarks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Injection Pressure Range (Mechanical CI Injectors)**
> - **Indirect Injection (IDI) engines:** **100 – 175 bar** (typical ~ **125 bar**)
> - **Direct Injection (DI) engines:** **175 – 350 bar** (typical ~ **200 – 250 bar**)
> - **High-speed modern DI:** up to **400 – 600 bar**
>
> **Acceptable Spray Pattern Characteristics:**
> - **Atomization:** fine, uniform mist (droplet size **5 – 30 μm**)
> - **Symmetry:** symmetric plume at 90° to nozzle axis
> - **Sound:** crisp, sharp *chatter* during pumping

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Does the Injector Open?

The needle valve remains **spring-loaded shut** against its seat. As the **Jerks Pump Plunger** compresses the fuel, the pressure inside the high-pressure chamber rises progressively. When the upward **hydraulic force** on the differential needle area exceeds the **spring preload**, the needle lifts (lifts about **0.4 – 0.5 mm**) and fuel sprays through the orifices.

> [!NOTE]
> The **needle tip is precision-lapped** to its seat — clearance is typically **0.002 – 0.004 mm**. This is why fuel cleanliness is non-negotiable in CI systems.

## 2.2 Sequence of Testing Operations (Logical Steps)

### Step 1 — Visual & Physical Pre-Inspection
- Clean the injector externally with **kerosene**.
- Inspect nozzle tip for **carbon deposits**, **worn orifices** (asymmetry), **sticking needle**.
- Verify **nozzle nut** torque (typically **60 – 80 Nm**).

### Step 2 — Mounting the Injector on the Test Bench
- Clamp the injector firmly on the **Injector Nozzle Tester** (Hand Pump / Motorised Pump).
- Connect the **high-pressure pipe** (test bench hose) from the pump to the injector inlet.
- **Bleed air** from the injector by opening the pressure relief knob and operating the pump gently.
- Ensure the **overflow port** discharges into a measuring beaker for leakage testing.

### Step 3 — Opening Pressure (Pop Pressure) Test
- Operate the pump handle **slowly and steadily**.
- Observe the **pressure gauge** (range 0 – 600 bar typical).
- Note the pressure at which fuel **first sprays** from the nozzle — this is the **opening pressure / pop pressure**.
- Compare with manufacturer's specification:
  - **Multi-hole nozzle (DI):** 200 – 300 bar
  - **Pintle nozzle (IDI):** 110 – 175 bar
- Repeat **3 – 4 strokes** to confirm consistency.

> [!TIP]
> The reading should be **reproducible within ± 2 bar**. Large variation indicates a **weak spring**, **damaged needle seat**, or **air in the system**.

### Step 4 — Spray Pattern Test
- Operate pump rapidly (approx. **1 stroke/sec**) at a pressure slightly **above the opening pressure**.
- Inspect spray on a **white paper** held **300 mm** away or against a **transparent shield**.
- Examine:
  - **Atomization:** should be fine mist, no droplets or streams.
  - **Symmetry:** plume axis should be **perpendicular** to nozzle axis.
  - **Spread:** uniform cone angle.
  - **Sound:** sharp, metallic, dry "tick" / chatter.

### Step 5 — Back Leakage (Nozzle Seat Leakage) Test
- Maintain a constant pressure of about **20 bar below** the opening pressure.
- Hold for **10 seconds**.
- Collect any fuel that drips from the **overflow port** (not from the spray holes).
- **Acceptance criterion:** typically **≤ 2 drops per minute** (or **≤ 0.5 ml / min**, depending on manufacturer spec).

### Step 6 — Chatter Test
- Operate pump at slow stroke rate.
- The needle should produce a **clear, audible chatter (ticking)** indicating proper seating and movement.
- **No chatter** → worn seat or weak spring.
- **Continuous chatter** is the desired healthy sound.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these three equations — they cover ~80% of numerical problems in this experiment.**

$$
P_{open} \;=\; \frac{4 \, F_{spring}}{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{needle}}
$$

Where:
- $P_{open}$ = nozzle opening pressure in **Pa** (divide by $10^{5}$ for bar)
- $F_{spring}$ = spring preload force in **N**
- $d_{needle}$ = needle seat diameter in **m**
- $A_{needle} = \pi d^{2}/4$ = differential needle area in **m²**

$$
F_{spring} \;=\; k \, x \;=\; k \, \left( x_{0} + t_{shim} \right)
$$

Where:
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m)
- $x_0$ = free spring preload (m)
- $t_{shim}$ = total shim thickness (m) — **adding shim → increases pressure**

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \frac{4 \, k \, \Delta t_{shim}}{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta P$ = change in opening pressure (Pa)
- $\Delta t_{shim}$ = change in shim thickness (m)

| Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Engineering Use |
|:--------|:-----------------|:------------------------|
| $P_{open} = F_{spring}/A_{needle}$ | Hydrostatic force balance on needle | Determining pop pressure from spring spec |
| $F_{spring} = k \, x$ | Hooke's Law for spring preload | Shim adjustment calculation |
| $\Delta P = (4k\,\Delta t)/\pi d^{2}$ | Pressure change due to shim variation | **Most asked** numerical in KTU |
| $\dot{V}_{leak} \le 0.5\,\text{mL/min}$ | Back leakage acceptance | Quality acceptance testing |
| $t_{atomize} \le t_{spec}$ | Spray atomization timing | Spray pattern classification |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Trap:** $1\,\text{bar} = 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} = 0.1\,\text{MPa} \approx 14.5\,\text{psi}$. Always convert spring force / pressure to **SI (N, Pa)** before substituting into equations.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Diesel tractor and genset service centres** in Kerala (Mahindra, John Deere, Kirloskar) use this exact procedure daily.
- **Bosch, Denso, Stanadyne, Delphi** all publish injector test specifications in **bar**, with tolerance bands.
- A poorly calibrated injector increases **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)** by 5–15%, raises **smoke opacity**, and causes **cylinder knocking**.
- Modern **CRDi (Common Rail Direct Injection)** systems have largely replaced mechanical injectors in passenger cars, but **commercial vehicles, marine diesels, and tractors** still use these — making this knowledge highly relevant for KTU Mechanical and Automobile students targeting **OEM service roles**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Nozzle Opening Pressure Equation

### Free Body Diagram of the Needle Valve

Consider the needle valve at the moment it is just about to lift. Two opposing forces act on it:

1. **Downward (Closing) Force:** $F_{spring}$ exerted by the nozzle spring acting on the needle's differential area seat.
2. **Upward (Opening) Force:** $F_{hydraulic}$ = fuel pressure × effective needle area, acting to lift the needle off its seat.

### Force Balance Derivation

At the critical *opening pressure* $P_{open}$, the needle is in **static equilibrium** — meaning the upward hydraulic force just equals the downward spring force. Mathematically:

$$
F_{hydraulic} \;=\; F_{spring}
$$

The hydraulic force is the pressure acting on the **differential needle area** $A_{needle}$ (the projected area at the seat):

$$
F_{hydraulic} \;=\; P_{open} \cdot A_{needle}
$$

The needle area is circular with diameter $d_{needle}$:

$$
A_{needle} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}}{4}
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
P_{open} \cdot \frac{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}}{4} \;=\; F_{spring}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{open} \;=\; \frac{4 \, F_{spring}}{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}}\,}
$$

This is the **master equation** used to determine the opening pressure of a mechanical diesel injector.

### Hooke's Law for Spring Preload

For a helical compression spring with stiffness $k$ and free length $L_0$, compressed to length $L$:

$$
F_{spring} \;=\; k \cdot \left( L_{0} - L \right)
$$

The compression $\left( L_{0} - L \right)$ is the **installed preload** $x$, which includes both the inherent preload and any **shim thickness** $t_{shim}$ added beneath the spring:

$$
x \;=\; x_{0} + t_{shim}
$$

$$
\therefore \quad F_{spring} \;=\; k \left( x_{0} + t_{shim} \right)
$$

### Combined Pressure-Shim Equation

Substituting $F_{spring}$ into the opening pressure equation:

$$
P_{open} \;=\; \frac{4 \, k \left( x_{0} + t_{shim} \right)}{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}}
$$

For a **change** in shim thickness $\Delta t_{shim}$ (with all other parameters fixed):

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \frac{4 \, k \, \Delta t_{shim}}{\pi \, d_{needle}^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation tells us that **adding a shim of thickness $\Delta t$ increases the opening pressure linearly** — a beautifully simple relationship that allows calibration by adding or removing shims of known thickness (typical shims are **0.05 mm, 0.10 mm, 0.20 mm, 0.50 mm**).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Scenarios

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation |
|:-------------|:------|:-----|:-------------|
| **Type A** — Find opening pressure | $F_{spring}$, $d_{needle}$ | $P_{open}$ | $P_{open} = 4F/\pi d^{2}$ |
| **Type B** — Find required shim | $P_{spec}$, $k$, $d$, $x_0$ | $t_{shim}$ | $t = (P\pi d^{2}/4k) - x_{0}$ |
| **Type C** — Pressure change | $k$, $\Delta t$, $d$ | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = 4k\Delta t/\pi d^{2}$ |
| **Type D** — Verify spring | $P_{open}$, $d$ | $F_{spring}$ | $F = P_{open} \pi d^{2}/4$ |
| **Type E** — Back-leakage rate | drops counted, time | $\dot{V}$ | $\dot{V} = V_{drop} \times n / t$ |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example Problem [14-mark KTU style]:**
> *"A diesel engine injector has a needle seat diameter of $4\,\text{mm}$. The nozzle spring has stiffness $k = 25\,\text{N/mm}$ and is installed with a free preload of $x_0 = 8\,\text{mm}$. The manufacturer specifies an opening pressure of $200\,\text{bar}$. Find:*
> *(a) The shim thickness required to achieve this pressure.*
> *(b) The new opening pressure if the shim is reduced by $0.20\,\text{mm}$."*

### Solution — Part (a)

**Given data:**
- $d_{needle} = 4\,\text{mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $k = 25\,\text{N/mm} = 25 \times 10^{3}\,\text{N/m}$
- $x_0 = 8\,\text{mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $P_{open} = 200\,\text{bar} = 200 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Calculate needle area:**

$$
A_{needle} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, d^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi \left( 4 \times 10^{-3} \right)^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 16 \times 10^{-6}}{4} \;=\; 12.566 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
A_{needle} \;=\; 1.2566 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate required spring force:**

$$
F_{spring} \;=\; P_{open} \cdot A_{needle} \;=\; \left(2 \times 10^{7}\right) \times \left(1.2566 \times 10^{-5}\right) \;=\; 251.33\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total preload displacement:**

$$
x_{total} \;=\; \frac{F_{spring}}{k} \;=\; \frac{251.33}{25 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 0.01005\,\text{m} \;=\; 10.053\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate required shim thickness:**

$$
t_{shim} \;=\; x_{total} - x_{0} \;=\; 10.053 - 8.000 \;=\; 2.053\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{shim} \;\approx\; 2.05\,\text{mm}\,}
$$

### Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** Shim reduced by $\Delta t = 0.20\,\text{mm} = 0.20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$

**Step 5 — Calculate change in preload (decrease):**

$$
\Delta x \;=\; -\Delta t \;=\; -0.20 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate change in spring force:**

$$
\Delta F \;=\; k \cdot \Delta x \;=\; \left(25 \times 10^{3}\right) \times \left(-0.20 \times 10^{-3}\right) \;=\; -5.0\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 7 — Calculate change in opening pressure:**

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\Delta F}{A_{needle}} \;=\; \frac{-5.0}{1.2566 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; -3.9789 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\Delta P \;\approx\; -3.98\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 8 — Calculate new opening pressure:**

$$
P_{new} \;=\; 200 - 3.98 \;=\; 196.02\,\text{bar}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{new} \;\approx\; 196.0\,\text{bar}\,}
$$

### Verification (Logical Sanity Check)

> Reducing the shim reduces spring compression → reduces spring force → reduces opening pressure. The result $196.0 < 200$ **bar** is consistent with intuition. ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Injector Testing Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start of Test]) --> s1A[Visual Inspection of Injector]
    s1A --> s2A{Clean Nozzle Tip?}
    s2A -->|No| s3A[Clean with Kerosene]
    s3A --> s2A
    s2A -->|Yes| s4A[Mount on Injector Test Bench]
    s4A --> s5A[Connect High Pressure Hose]
    s5A --> s6A[Bleed Air from System]
    s6A --> s7A[Test 1: Opening Pressure]
    s7A --> s8A{Pressure Matches Spec?}
    s8A -->|No| s9A[Adjust Shim Thickness]
    s9A --> s7A
    s8A -->|Yes| s10A[Test 2: Spray Pattern]
    s10A --> s11A{Atomization OK?}
    s11A -->|No| s12A[Clean or Replace Nozzle]
    s12A --> s10A
    s11A -->|Yes| s13A[Test 3: Back Leakage]
    s13A --> s14A{Leak within Limit?}
    s14A -->|No| s15A[Re-lap Needle Seat]
    s15A --> s13A
    s14A -->|Yes| s16A[Test 4: Chatter Test]
    s16A --> s17A{Sharp Audible Chatter?}
    s17A -->|No| s18A[Replace Nozzle Assembly]
    s18A --> s16A
    s17A -->|Yes| endA([Injector PASS - Fit to Engine])
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of a Mechanical Diesel Injector

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA[High Pressure Fuel Inlet from Jerk Pump] --> capA[Pressure Cap Nut]
    capA --> bodyA[Nozzle Holder Body]
    bodyA --> springA[Nozzle Spring]
    springA --> shimA[Shim Adjusting Washer]
    shimA --> retainerA[Spring Retainer]
    retainerA --> needleA[Needle Valve]
    needleA --> seatA[Needle Seat - Lapped Surface]
    seatA --> orifA[Spray Orifices - Multi Hole or Pintle]
    bodyA --> overA[Overflow Port - Leak Back Line]
    bodyA --> lockA[Nozzle Lock Nut]
    lockA --> nozzleA[Nozzle Body Barrel]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force Balance on Needle Valve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph forceA[Forces on Needle Valve]
        f1A[Downward Force: F spring]
        f2A[Upward Force: F hydraulic]
    end
    f1A --> eqA[Equilibrium Condition]
    f2A --> eqA
    eqA --> outA[P open = 4F / PI d squared]
    outA --> stateA{Is P open Reached?}
    stateA -->|Yes| openA[Needle Lifts and Fuel Sprays]
    stateA -->|No| closedA[Needle Remains Seated]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Cross-Sectional View (Block-Level)

| Section (Top → Bottom) | Component Description | Functional Role |
|:-----------------------|:----------------------|:----------------|
| **1. Topmost** | Rocker arm / Pressure cap interface | Receives high-pressure fuel |
| **2. Upper body** | Fuel gallery + filter screen | Distributes fuel around needle |
| **3. Spring chamber** | Helical nozzle spring | Provides closing force |
| **4. Shim zone** | Adjusting shim washer | Sets opening pressure |
| **5. Needle chamber** | Precision needle valve | Controls spray onset/cessation |
| **6. Nozzle tip** | Lapped seat + spray holes | Atomizes fuel into cylinder |

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Convention Used:** Since the actual injector cross-section involves overlapping concentric components, a top-down *functional block* representation is used (instead of Mermaid's limited geometric drawing capability). This is standard practice in KTU lab manuals.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: Define "Opening Pressure" of a mechanical diesel injector. What is its typical range for a multi-hole nozzle used in direct injection engines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **opening pressure** (also called *pop pressure* or *injection pressure*) of a mechanical diesel injector is the **minimum fuel pressure at which the needle valve lifts off its seat** and fuel begins to spray through the orifices. It is determined by the balance between the upward hydraulic force on the needle's differential area and the downward spring preload:

$$
P_{open} \;=\; \frac{4 F_{spring}}{\pi d_{needle}^{2}}
$$

**Typical Range (1 mark):** For **multi-hole nozzles in direct injection (DI) engines**, the opening pressure typically ranges from **200 – 300 bar**, with **250 bar** being a very common specification.

**Significance (0.5 marks):** Correct opening pressure ensures proper atomization, correct injection timing, and prevents both cylinder knocking (over-pressure) and smoke (under-pressure).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: What is a "spray pattern test"? List any four observations that indicate a faulty spray pattern.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **spray pattern test** is a qualitative diagnostic procedure in which the diesel injector is operated on a test bench and the emitted fuel spray is visually inspected for **atomization quality, symmetry, and sound**.

**Four Observations Indicating a Faulty Spray Pattern (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Asymmetric spray** — plume deviates from perpendicular axis → indicates worn or blocked orifice.
2. **Wet / streaming spray** — visible streams rather than fine mist → indicates poor atomization or low pressure.
3. **Side spray leakage** — fuel escapes from nozzle nut or body → indicates damaged sealing surfaces.
4. **No audible chatter** — silent operation → indicates worn needle seat or weak spring.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"spray pattern test"** with **"opening pressure test."** They are different — opening pressure is a *quantitative* pressure measurement, while spray pattern is a *qualitative* visual/auditory inspection. KTU examiners specifically look for this distinction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a mechanical diesel injector**. Also describe the **Injector Nozzle Tester** used for testing it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel injector has a needle seat diameter of **5 mm**. The nozzle spring has stiffness **20 N/mm** and installed free preload **10 mm**. Determine: **(i)** The opening pressure of the injector. **(ii)** The new opening pressure if a shim of thickness **0.5 mm** is removed from the spring seat. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**

A mechanical diesel injector consists of the following parts:

1. **Nozzle holder (body)** — houses all internal components and provides the high-pressure inlet.
2. **Nozzle spring** — helical compression spring that holds the needle on its seat.
3. **Spring shim** — adjusting washer to set spring preload.
4. **Needle valve** — precision-lapped sliding pin that controls fuel flow.
5. **Nozzle barrel** — contains the spray orifices (multi-hole / pintle).
6. **Pressure cap / lock nut** — secures assembly and provides fuel inlet.

**Working (3 marks):**

High-pressure fuel from the **Jerks Pump** enters through the pressure cap. It fills the nozzle holder's internal gallery and exerts upward force on the needle's differential area. When this hydraulic force exceeds the spring preload, the needle lifts about 0.4 mm, and fuel sprays through the orifices into the combustion chamber. When the pump plunger completes its delivery stroke, pressure drops, the spring re-seats the needle, and injection ends abruptly (hence "jerk pump").

**Injector Nozzle Tester Description (1 mark):**

The **Injector Nozzle Tester** is a hand-operated or motorised hydraulic pump test bench fitted with:
- A high-pressure hose to connect the injector
- A calibrated **pressure gauge** (0–600 bar typical)
- A **pressure-relief valve** for bleeding
- A **transparent spray shield / white paper** for visual inspection
- A **measuring beaker** for back-leakage collection

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $d_{needle} = 5\,\text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $k = 20\,\text{N/mm} = 20 \times 10^{3}\,\text{N/m}$
- $x_0 = 10\,\text{mm} = 10 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- Initial opening pressure = to find
- Shim removed: $\Delta t = 0.5\,\text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Calculate needle area:**

$$
A_{needle} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, (5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} \;=\; 1.9635 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate spring force at initial preload:**

$$
F_{spring} \;=\; k \cdot x_{0} \;=\; (20 \times 10^{3}) \times (10 \times 10^{-3}) \;=\; 200\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial opening pressure:**

$$
P_{open,1} \;=\; \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{needle}} \;=\; \frac{200}{1.9635 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 1.0186 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa}
$$

$$
P_{open,1} \;\approx\; 101.86\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate change in opening pressure after shim removal:**

Reducing shim by $\Delta t = 0.5\,\text{mm}$ reduces preload by the same amount:

$$
\Delta x \;=\; -0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}
$$

$$
\Delta F \;=\; k \cdot \Delta x \;=\; (20 \times 10^{3}) \times (-0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \;=\; -10\,\text{N}
$$

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \frac{\Delta F}{A_{needle}} \;=\; \frac{-10}{1.9635 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; -5.093 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} \;\approx\; -5.09\,\text{bar}
$$

**Step 5 — New opening pressure:**

$$
P_{open,2} \;=\; 101.86 - 5.09 \;=\; 96.77\,\text{bar}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{open,1} \;\approx\; 101.9\,\text{bar} \quad ; \quad P_{open,2} \;\approx\; 96.8\,\text{bar}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A common mistake is forgetting to convert units correctly. $k$ in **N/mm** must be multiplied by $x$ in **mm** to get $F$ in **N**, OR $k$ must be converted to **N/m** with $x$ in **m**. Mixing units causes 10²–10³ errors and full mark loss.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Alternative Internal Choice]

**Q: (a)** List and explain **any four tests** performed on a diesel injector using an Injector Nozzle Tester. State the acceptance criteria for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During testing, the observed opening pressure of a diesel injector was **180 bar** with a needle seat diameter of **6 mm**. The manufacturer specifies **200 bar**. If the spring stiffness is **30 N/mm**, calculate: **(i)** The shim thickness to be added to achieve the specified pressure. **(ii)** The percentage increase in opening pressure per mm of shim added. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Test 1: Opening Pressure (Pop Pressure) Test (1.75 marks):**

The injector is operated slowly on the test bench. The pressure gauge reading at which fuel first sprays from the nozzle is the opening pressure. **Acceptance:** within **± 5 bar** of manufacturer's specification. Repeatable over 3–4 strokes.

**Test 2: Spray Pattern Test (1.75 marks):**

The injector is pumped rapidly at a pressure slightly above opening pressure. Spray is observed on a white paper held 300 mm away. **Acceptance:** fine uniform mist, symmetric plume perpendicular to nozzle axis, sharp audible "chatter."

**Test 3: Back Leakage Test (1.75 marks):**

A constant pressure of about 20 bar below opening pressure is held for 10 seconds. Any fuel leaking from the overflow port (not the spray holes) is collected. **Acceptance:** typically **≤ 2 drops / minute** (or **≤ 0.5 mL / min**).

**Test 4: Chatter Test (1.75 marks):**

The injector is pumped slowly and the sound is observed. **Acceptance:** sharp, clear, metallic "tick-tick" sound indicating the needle is properly seating and lifting. No chatter indicates worn needle seat or weak spring.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $P_{open,1} = 180\,\text{bar} = 180 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$
- $P_{spec} = 200\,\text{bar} = 200 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$
- $d_{needle} = 6\,\text{mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $k = 30\,\text{N/mm} = 30 \times 10^{3}\,\text{N/m}$

**Step 1 — Needle area:**

$$
A_{needle} \;=\; \frac{\pi \, (6 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4} \;=\; 2.827 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Current spring force (at 180 bar):**

$$
F_{1} \;=\; P_{1} \cdot A \;=\; (180 \times 10^{5}) \times (2.827 \times 10^{-5}) \;=\; 509\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Required spring force (at 200 bar):**

$$
F_{2} \;=\; P_{2} \cdot A \;=\; (200 \times 10^{5}) \times (2.827 \times 10^{-5}) \;=\; 565.5\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Additional force needed:**

$$
\Delta F \;=\; F_{2} - F_{1} \;=\; 565.5 - 509 \;=\; 56.5\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Shim thickness required:**

$$
t_{shim} \;=\; \frac{\Delta F}{k} \;=\; \frac{56.5}{30 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 1.883 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \;\approx\; 1.88\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Pressure increase per mm of shim:**

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{k}{A_{needle}} \;=\; \frac{30 \times 10^{3}}{2.827 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 1.061 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa/m}
$$

Converting per mm:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} \;=\; 1.061 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa/mm} \;\approx\; 10.61\,\text{bar/mm}
$$

**Step 7 — Percentage increase per mm:**

$$
\% \, \text{increase per mm} \;=\; \frac{10.61}{180} \times 100 \;\approx\; 5.90\,\%\,\text{per mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{shim} \;\approx\; 1.88\,\text{mm} \quad ; \quad \text{Rate} \;\approx\; 10.6\,\text{bar/mm} \;\approx\; 5.9\,\%\,\text{per mm}\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often confuse "shim thickness to add" with "shim thickness to remove." The problem states current pressure is **180 bar** and target is **200 bar** — so we must **add** a shim (not remove). Read the problem statement twice before solving. KTU board examiners deduct **2 marks** for this directional mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

- ☐ **Opening pressure** is set by spring preload balanced against needle differential area: $P_{open} = 4F/\pi d^{2}$.
- ☐ **Adding shim → increases opening pressure**; **removing shim → decreases it** (linear relationship).
- ☐ **Typical pressure ranges:** IDI 100–175 bar; DI 200–300 bar.
- ☐ **Spray pattern** must be **symmetric, finely atomized**, with **sharp chatter sound**.
- ☐ **Back leakage** acceptance: **≤ 2 drops/min** from overflow port at ~20 bar below opening pressure.
- ☐ **Injector tester** has: pump, pressure gauge, hose, bleed valve, spray shield, leakage beaker.
- ☐ **Needle clearance** at seat: typically **2–4 μm** — extreme precision.
- ☐ **Needle lift** during injection: **0.4 – 0.5 mm**.
- ☐ **Hydraulic force** acts on **differential needle area** (not total cross-section).
- ☐ **$1\,\text{bar} = 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$** — most common unit conversion error in KTU papers.
- ☐ **Multi-hole nozzles** for DI engines; **pintle nozzles** for IDI / pre-chamber engines.
- ☐ **Chatter test failure** → either weak spring, worn seat, or air in system (bleed first!).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Tip (For University Lab Exam Viva):** When asked *"What is the first thing you do before testing an injector?"* — answer: **"Clean the nozzle tip with kerosene, then mount it on the test bench and bleed the air from the high-pressure line before taking any readings."** Examiners love this answer because it shows lab discipline.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brake Force Measurements Using a Two-Wheeler Brake Tester
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Brake Force Measurements Using a Two-Wheeler Brake Tester

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Two-Wheeler Brake Tester** is a specialized diagnostic instrument used in automotive workshops and testing stations to evaluate the braking performance of motorcycles, scooters, and mopeds under controlled, simulated dynamic conditions. It measures the **maximum braking force** developed by the friction brake system of each wheel (front and rear) at a predefined roller speed, and then computes the **brake efficiency** as a percentage of the static vertical load supported by that wheel.

The tester essentially consists of a pair of **motor-driven steel rollers** (one set for the front wheel and another for the rear wheel), a **hydraulic or electromagnetic braking mechanism** to load the rollers, **load cells** (force transducers) that sense the tangential reaction force transmitted from the braked wheel to the roller, an **RPM/speed sensor**, and a **digital display/console** that records the peak brake force and computes efficiency in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment directly evaluates **CO2 – Apply diagnostic procedures to measure performance parameters of automotive systems** and is conducted in the laboratory as part of the continuous internal evaluation under the **PCMUL308 – Automotive Systems Lab (2024 Scheme)** course.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The "Treadmill of Justice" for Brakes)

Imagine a child sitting on a tiny treadmill. If the child suddenly grips the handrails very tightly, the treadmill motor will feel a sudden pull — the harder the child grips, the more strain the motor experiences. The treadmill is calibrated to read this "gripping effort" as a number.

A **two-wheeler brake tester works on exactly the same principle**:

- The **rollers** are the treadmill.
- The **motorcycle wheel** is the child.
- The **brake applied at the wheel** is the "gripping effort."
- The **load cell on the roller** measures how hard the wheel is fighting back to keep rolling.

So when the rider squeezes the brake lever, the brake pad clamps the disc/drum, the wheel tries to stop, the roller feels a **tangential braking force**, and the tester displays this force digitally. Simple, elegant, and brutally honest — a bad brake cannot hide on a brake tester.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **Glossary of Core Terms (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)**
>
> - **Brake Force ($F_b$):** The tangential frictional force generated at the tire–roller contact patch when the brake is applied. Measured in **N (Newton)** or **kgf**.
> - **Vertical Wheel Load ($W$):** The static weight borne by the wheel being tested. Measured in **N** or **kgf**.
> - **Brake Efficiency ($\eta_b$):** The ratio of brake force to vertical wheel load, expressed as a percentage.
> - **Test Speed ($v$):** The peripheral speed of the roller during the test, typically **2.5 km/h to 5 km/h** (very low-speed test) as per **AIS-052 / IS 14664** standards.
> - **Slip:** The relative velocity difference between the roller surface and the tire surface during braking.
> - **Static vs Dynamic Test:** Static test (vehicle stationary) measures only hand/pedal effort; dynamic test (this experiment) measures true kinetic braking performance.

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

Brake-force measurement is a **mandatory statutory test** under the **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR), 1989** and is required for:

1. **Vehicle Fitness Certification** (for transport vehicles)
2. **Type Approval** of new vehicle models (ARAI testing)
3. **Periodic Pollution Under Control (PUC) + Brake Check** in some states
4. **Insurance Claim Validation** after accidents
5. **OEM Quality Control** at manufacturing line-end

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle — Newton's Third Law in Action

The two-wheeler brake tester exploits **Newton's Third Law of Motion**: *for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.*

**Step-by-step physics:**

- The roller rotates at a constant test speed (e.g., 5 km/h) driven by a motor.
- The motorcycle wheel rests on the roller; the wheel is free to rotate (drive wheel is on a free-roller or the chain is decoupled on most testers).
- The rider applies the brake lever. The brake pad/drum friction generates a **braking torque** $T_b$ on the wheel hub.
- The wheel, instead of rotating freely, now resists rotation. To stay in equilibrium with the rotating roller, the tire–roller contact patch must develop a **tangential friction force** $F_b$.
- This force is transmitted back to the roller shaft, which is mounted on a **strain-gauge load cell**.
- The load cell deflects proportionally to $F_b$, and the digital indicator displays the peak force.

The energy balance is:

$$\text{Energy absorbed by brake} \;=\; \text{Mechanical work done at the roller}$$

$$F_b \times v_{roller} \;=\; T_b \times \omega_{wheel}$$

where $\omega_{wheel}$ is the wheel's angular speed in rad/s and $v_{roller}$ is the linear speed of the roller surface.

## 2.2 Test Procedure — The Lab-Standard Protocol

The standard sequence followed in the KTU lab is:

1. **Pre-test Inspection**
   - Check tire condition (tread depth, pressure).
   - Inspect brake pads/shoes for wear.
   - Ensure the brake tester rollers are clean and free of oil.

2. **Vehicle Positioning**
   - Place the motorcycle on the platform with **front wheel** on the front roller set.
   - Use wheel guides/clamps to centralize the wheel.
   - Start the rollers and warm up for 30 seconds.

3. **Front Wheel Test**
   - Accelerate roller to test speed (e.g., 4–5 km/h).
   - Apply front brake **gradually and continuously** until maximum reading is obtained.
   - Record **peak brake force** $F_{bf}$ and the **vertical wheel load** $W_f$.

4. **Rear Wheel Test**
   - Repeat the procedure for the rear wheel.
   - Record $F_{br}$ and $W_r$.

5. **Combined Brake Test (if equipped)**
   - Apply both brakes simultaneously.
   - Record combined brake force $F_{bc}$.

6. **Calculation**
   - Compute individual and overall brake efficiencies using formulas in the table below.

7. **Comparison with Standards**
   - Compare with AIS / CMVR minimum limits.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table — it covers 80% of KTU numerical questions on this experiment.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Front Wheel Brake Efficiency | $\eta_f = \dfrac{F_{bf}}{W_f} \times 100$ | % | $F_{bf}$ is peak front brake force |
| 2 | Rear Wheel Brake Efficiency | $\eta_r = \dfrac{F_{br}}{W_r} \times 100$ | % | $F_{br}$ is peak rear brake force |
| 3 | Overall (Average) Brake Efficiency | $\eta_{avg} = \dfrac{\eta_f + \eta_r}{2}$ | % | Used for two-wheeler with independent brakes |
| 4 | Overall (Weighted) Brake Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{F_{bf}+F_{br}}{W_f+W_r} \times 100$ | % | More accurate; uses total vehicle weight |
| 5 | Braking Distance (theoretical) | $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ | m | Derived from $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $a=\mu g$ |
| 6 | Deceleration of Wheel | $a = \dfrac{F_b}{m} = \mu g$ | m/s² | $m$ = mass on braked wheel |
| 7 | Brake Torque (from brake force) | $T_b = F_b \times r_{roller}$ | N·m | $r_{roller}$ is the radius of the test roller |
| 8 | Co-efficient of Friction (implied) | $\mu = \dfrac{F_b}{W}$ | dimensionless | Equal to brake efficiency in fraction |
| 9 | Percentage Slip at Peak | $\lambda = \dfrac{v_{roller} - v_{wheel}}{v_{roller}} \times 100$ | % | Ideally between 5–25% at peak brake force |
| 10 | Test Speed (typical) | $v = 2.5$ to $5$ km/h | km/h | Per AIS-052 / IS 14664 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Reminder (Asked frequently in KTU viva):**
> $1 \, \text{kgf} = 9.81 \, \text{N}$ (use **$g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$** unless stated otherwise).
> $1 \, \text{kN} = 1000 \, \text{N}$.

## 2.4 CMVR / AIS Standard Minimum Brake Efficiency (India)

| Vehicle Configuration | Minimum Brake Efficiency |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Two-wheeler — Service brake (combined front + rear) | **50 %** |
| Two-wheeler — Front brake alone | **30 %** |
| Two-wheeler — Rear brake alone | **25 %** |
| Three-wheeler — Service brake | **45 %** |
| M1 category car — Service brake | **50 %** |
| Parking brake (all) | **18 % to 20 %** |

If a vehicle falls below these values during inspection, it is **deemed to have defective brakes** under Section 53 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **ARAI Type Approval:** Every new two-wheeler model sold in India must demonstrate compliance with AIS-052 brake efficiency limits before mass production.
- **Public Safety:** A 1% drop in brake efficiency can increase stopping distance by 3–4% at city speeds, which is the difference between a near-miss and a fatality.
- **Workshop Diagnostics:** A garage mechanic can pinpoint whether the issue is in the master cylinder, brake pad wear, drum scoring, or hydraulic line air-locking by comparing front vs rear efficiencies.
- **Insurance & Forensics:** Brake tester data logs are admissible as evidence in accident reconstruction cases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Brake Efficiency Formula

The brake efficiency of a wheel is defined as the ratio of the maximum frictional braking force developed at the tire–road (or tire–roller) contact to the normal reaction (vertical load) on that wheel.

**Step 1: Consider a free-body diagram of a single braked wheel.**

Forces acting on the wheel:
- Vertical load $W$ acting downward through the wheel center.
- Normal reaction $N$ acting upward from the roller (where $N = W$ under static equilibrium).
- Tangential brake force $F_b$ acting opposite to the direction of roller rotation at the contact patch.

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law in the tangential direction.**

The wheel, just before locking, decelerates. The maximum tangential force the tire can transmit equals the product of the normal load and the coefficient of friction between tire and roller:

$$F_b^{max} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot W$$

**Step 3: Define the coefficient of friction as the ratio of tangential to normal force.**

$$\mu = \dfrac{F_b}{W}$$

**Step 4: Express as a percentage (this is the "brake efficiency").**

$$\boxed{\eta_b \;=\; \dfrac{F_b}{W} \times 100 \;=\; \mu \times 100 \;[\%]}$$

Hence, brake efficiency is **dimensionally and numerically equivalent to the tire–road friction coefficient expressed as a percentage.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Overall Brake Efficiency (Weighted Form)

For a two-wheeler with weight distributed as $W_f$ on the front and $W_r$ on the rear:

$$\text{Total weight} \; W_{total} \;=\; W_f + W_r$$

$$\text{Total brake force available} \; F_{total} \;=\; F_{bf} + F_{br}$$

The vehicle's maximum deceleration is:

$$a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{m_{total}} = \dfrac{F_{bf} + F_{br}}{m_{total}}$$

Since $W = mg$, we have $m_{total} = \dfrac{W_f + W_r}{g}$. Substituting:

$$a_{max} = \dfrac{(F_{bf} + F_{br}) \cdot g}{W_f + W_r}$$

The corresponding effective friction coefficient is:

$$\mu_{eff} = \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} = \dfrac{F_{bf} + F_{br}}{W_f + W_r}$$

Therefore, the **weighted overall brake efficiency** is:

$$\boxed{\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{F_{bf} + F_{br}}{W_f + W_r} \times 100 \; [\%]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **simple-average formula** $\dfrac{\eta_f + \eta_r}{2}$ is mathematically equivalent to the weighted formula **only when** $W_f = W_r$, which is rarely the case for a real two-wheeler (typically $W_f / W_r \approx 1.3$ to $1.5$). The weighted formula is therefore more accurate and is what KTU examiners prefer in numerical answers.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A motorcycle of total kerb weight **180 kgf** is tested on a two-wheeler brake tester. The front wheel carries **60 %** of the total weight and the rear wheel carries **40 %**. The peak brake forces recorded are:
- Front wheel: **$F_{bf} = 80$ kgf**
- Rear wheel: **$F_{br} = 50$ kgf**

Calculate:
1. The individual brake efficiencies of the front and rear wheels.
2. The overall weighted brake efficiency.
3. The vehicle's maximum deceleration.
4. Whether the vehicle passes the CMVR brake efficiency standard.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$W_{total} = 180 \text{ kgf}, \quad W_f = 0.6 \times 180 = 108 \text{ kgf}, \quad W_r = 0.4 \times 180 = 72 \text{ kgf}$$

$$F_{bf} = 80 \text{ kgf}, \quad F_{br} = 50 \text{ kgf}, \quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Part (a): Individual Brake Efficiencies**

$$\eta_f = \dfrac{F_{bf}}{W_f} \times 100 = \dfrac{80}{108} \times 100 = 74.07 \;\%$$

$$\eta_r = \dfrac{F_{br}}{W_r} \times 100 = \dfrac{50}{72} \times 100 = 69.44 \;\%$$

**Part (b): Overall Weighted Brake Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{F_{bf} + F_{br}}{W_f + W_r} \times 100 = \dfrac{80 + 50}{108 + 72} \times 100 = \dfrac{130}{180} \times 100 = 72.22 \;\%$$

**Part (c): Maximum Deceleration of the Vehicle**

Total mass of the vehicle:

$$m_{total} = \dfrac{180 \text{ kgf}}{g} = \dfrac{180 \times 9.81}{9.81} = 180 \text{ kg}$$

Total brake force in Newtons:

$$F_{total} = (F_{bf} + F_{br}) \times 9.81 = (80 + 50) \times 9.81 = 130 \times 9.81 = 1275.3 \text{ N}$$

Maximum deceleration:

$$a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{m_{total}} = \dfrac{1275.3}{180} = 7.085 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Part (d): Compliance with CMVR Standards**

- Front brake alone: 74.07 % ≥ 30 %  **PASS**
- Rear brake alone: 69.44 % ≥ 25 %  **PASS**
- Combined (overall): 72.22 % ≥ 50 %  **PASS**

**Conclusion:** The motorcycle's braking system satisfies the CMVR / AIS-052 minimum brake efficiency requirements and is **legally roadworthy**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Numerical Variants in KTU Papers

| Variant | What is Given | What is Asked | Key Formula to Use |
|---------|---------------|---------------|---------------------|
| A | $F_b$, $W$ | Efficiency $\eta$ | $\eta = F_b / W \times 100$ |
| B | $\eta$, $W$ | Brake force $F_b$ | $F_b = \eta \cdot W / 100$ |
| C | $W_{total}$, load distribution %, $F_{bf}$, $F_{br}$ | Overall $\eta$, deceleration $a$ | $\eta_{overall} = (F_{bf}+F_{br})/(W_f+W_r) \times 100$ |
| D | $v$, $a$ | Stopping distance $s$ | $s = v^2 / (2a)$ |
| E | $s$, $v$ | Required $\mu$ | $\mu = v^2 / (2 g s)$ |
| F | $\eta_f$, $\eta_r$, $W_f$, $W_r$ | Overall weighted $\eta$ | Use weighted formula, not average |
| G | Roller radius $r$, $F_b$ | Brake torque $T_b$ | $T_b = F_b \times r$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Layout of a Two-Wheeler Brake Tester

The diagram below maps the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a typical commercial two-wheeler brake tester, showing all subsystems and signal flows between them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Motorcycle Mounted Platform] --> B1{Front Wheel Roller Set}
    A1 --> B2{Rear Wheel Roller Set}
    B1 --> C1[Electric Drive Motor 1.5 kW]
    B2 --> C2[Electric Drive Motor 1.5 kW]
    C1 --> B1
    C2 --> B2
    B1 --> D1[Load Cell Force Transducer]
    B2 --> D2[Load Cell Force Transducer]
    B1 --> E1[RPM Encoder]
    B2 --> E2[RPM Encoder]
    D1 --> F1[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    D2 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Microcontroller Data Acquisition]
    G1 --> H1[Digital Display Console]
    G1 --> I1[Peak Hold Memory]
    G1 --> J1[Efficiency Calculator Module]
    J1 --> H1
    H1 --> K1[Printout Serial Port]
    B1 --> L1[Wheel Centering Clamp]
    B2 --> L1
    L1 --> A1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **motorcycle** is positioned on the platform with the front wheel on one roller set and the rear on another.
- Each roller set is driven by its own electric motor (typically **1.5 kW to 2.2 kW**, 3-phase AC).
- The **load cell** under each roller senses the horizontal reaction force (the brake force).
- The **encoder** measures the roller RPM for slip calculation.
- Both signals are amplified, digitized by the **microcontroller**, and pushed to a **digital display** that shows real-time force, peak force, speed, and calculated efficiency.

## 4.2 Signal Flow Diagram — From Brake Lever to Digital Reading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rider Squeezes Brake Lever] --> B[Master Cylinder Pressurizes Fluid]
    B --> C[Brake Pad Clamps Disc or Drum]
    C --> D[Friction Generates Braking Torque on Wheel]
    D --> E[Wheel Tries to Slow on Roller]
    E --> F[Tangential Friction Force Fb at Contact Patch]
    F --> G[Reaction Force on Roller Shaft]
    G --> H[Strain Gauge Load Cell Deflects]
    H --> I[Wheatstone Bridge Output in mV]
    I --> J[Amplifier Converts to 0-10 V Signal]
    J --> K[ADC Converts to Digital Count]
    K --> L[Microcontroller Applies Calibration Factor]
    L --> M[Digital Display Shows Fb in kgf or N]
    M --> N[Efficiency Calculator Outputs eta in percentage]
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Flow for Pass / Fail Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Test] --> P2[Measure Front Brake Force Fbf]
    P2 --> P3[Measure Rear Brake Force Fbr]
    P3 --> P4[Measure Wheel Loads Wf and Wr]
    P4 --> P5[Compute eta_f = Fbf over Wf times 100]
    P4 --> P6[Compute eta_r = Fbr over Wr times 100]
    P5 --> P7{Is eta_f greater than 30 percent}
    P6 --> P8{Is eta_r greater than 25 percent}
    P7 --> P9[Front Result Recorded]
    P8 --> P10[Rear Result Recorded]
    P9 --> P11[Compute Overall Efficiency]
    P10 --> P11
    P11 --> P12{Is Overall greater than 50 percent}
    P12 --> P13[VEHICLE PASSES]
    P12 --> P14[VEHICLE FAILS Inspection]
    P13 --> P15[Issue Fitness Certificate]
    P14 --> P16[Recommend Brake Service]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define brake efficiency. State the minimum brake efficiency required for a two-wheeler as per CMVR / AIS-052 standards.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Brake efficiency is defined as the ratio of the maximum braking force developed at the tire–roller contact patch to the vertical load on the braked wheel, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \dfrac{F_b}{W} \times 100 \; [\%]$$

As per **CMVR / AIS-052**, the minimum brake efficiency requirements for a two-wheeler are:
- **Service brake (combined front + rear): 50 %**
- **Front brake alone: 30 %**
- **Rear brake alone: 25 %**

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — standards.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a static brake test and a dynamic brake test on a two-wheeler brake tester.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Static Brake Test | Dynamic Brake Test |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Roller motion | Roller is stationary | Roller is rotating at test speed (2.5–5 km/h) |
| Brake force | Measured by hydraulic pressure or hand-lever effort | Measured at tire–roller contact patch via load cell |
| Realism | Lower; only effort is recorded | Higher; simulates actual road braking |
| Speed effect | Not captured | Captures fade, heat, and velocity effects |
| Regulatory use | Workshop preliminary check | Statutory fitness test as per AIS-052 |

*(1.5 marks — table, 1.5 marks — explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Detailed Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has **internal choice** (either-or). Solve **any one** of the two options provided below.

### **Question B1 — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a two-wheeler brake tester. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A motorcycle with a total weight of 195 kgf has a load distribution of 55% on the front and 45% on the rear. The peak brake forces recorded on a brake tester are: front = 95 kgf, rear = 65 kgf. Determine: (i) individual brake efficiencies, (ii) overall weighted brake efficiency, (iii) whether the vehicle passes CMVR standards. **\[7 marks\]**

#### **Model Solution for B1(a):**

**Construction:** A two-wheeler brake tester consists of the following main components:

1. **Roller assembly** — Two pairs of smooth steel rollers, one pair for the front wheel and one for the rear. Roller diameter is typically 100–150 mm with surface knurling to prevent tire slip.
2. **Drive motors** — Two separate 1.5–2.2 kW three-phase induction motors, one for each roller set, providing constant test speed.
3. **Load cells** — Strain-gauge-based force transducers mounted on the roller shaft pillow blocks to sense the tangential brake force.
4. **Speed sensor (encoder)** — Optical or magnetic pickup on the roller shaft to measure RPM and hence surface speed.
5. **Wheel clamps/guides** — To centralize the wheel on the roller.
6. **Digital control console** — Microcontroller-based unit with LCD display, peak-hold memory, and printout facility.
7. **Frame and platform** — Heavy fabricated steel base supporting all subsystems.

**Working:**

- The motorcycle is placed on the platform with front and rear wheels on their respective rollers.
- The roller motors are started, accelerating the rollers to the test speed (typically 5 km/h).
- The rider applies the front brake lever. The brake pad clamps the rotor, generating a tangential force at the tire–roller interface.
- This force is transmitted through the roller shaft to the load cell, which produces a proportional millivolt signal.
- The signal is amplified, digitized, and displayed as brake force in kgf.
- The peak value is held in memory. The same procedure is repeated for the rear brake and for the combined brake.
- The microcontroller computes brake efficiency using $\eta = (F_b / W) \times 100$ and displays it on the console.

*(3.5 marks — construction list + diagram, 3.5 marks — working steps.)*

#### **Model Solution for B1(b):**

**Given:**

$$W_{total} = 195 \text{ kgf}, \quad W_f = 0.55 \times 195 = 107.25 \text{ kgf}, \quad W_r = 0.45 \times 195 = 87.75 \text{ kgf}$$

$$F_{bf} = 95 \text{ kgf}, \quad F_{br} = 65 \text{ kgf}$$

**(i) Individual Efficiencies:**

$$\eta_f = \dfrac{95}{107.25} \times 100 = 88.58 \;\%$$

$$\eta_r = \dfrac{65}{87.75} \times 100 = 74.07 \;\%$$

**(ii) Overall Weighted Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{95 + 65}{107.25 + 87.75} \times 100 = \dfrac{160}{195} \times 100 = 82.05 \;\%$$

**(iii) CMVR Compliance Check:**

- Front alone: 88.58 % ≥ 30 % **PASS**
- Rear alone: 74.07 % ≥ 25 % **PASS**
- Overall combined: 82.05 % ≥ 50 % **PASS**

**Conclusion:** The motorcycle **passes** the CMVR brake efficiency standards and is roadworthy.

*(2 marks — part (i), 2 marks — part (ii), 2 marks — part (iii) with conclusion, 1 mark — proper units/notation.)*

---

### **Question B1 — Option B (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure for conducting a brake force test on a two-wheeler using a brake tester. List the safety precautions to be observed. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A two-wheeler has a front brake efficiency of 75 % and a rear brake efficiency of 60 %. The kerb weight is 150 kgf distributed as 60 % front and 40 % rear. Find: (i) the front and rear brake forces, (ii) the maximum deceleration of the vehicle, and (iii) the theoretical stopping distance from 40 km/h. **\[7 marks\]**

#### **Model Solution for B1(a) — Option B:**

**Procedure:**

1. Inspect the two-wheeler visually — tire pressure, brake pad thickness, fluid level, and chain tension.
2. Switch on the brake tester and run the rollers idle for 30 seconds for warm-up.
3. Place the motorcycle on the platform. Engage front wheel in the front roller set and apply the wheel clamp.
4. Start the roller. Allow it to reach the test speed of 4–5 km/h.
5. Instruct the rider to apply the front brake gradually until maximum force is indicated.
6. Record the peak brake force $F_{bf}$ and the static front wheel load $W_f$ from the console.
7. Repeat steps 3 to 6 for the rear wheel.
8. Repeat the test with both brakes applied simultaneously (combined brake test) if required.
9. Calculate efficiencies and compare with AIS-052 standards.
10. Switch off the motor, release clamps, and remove the vehicle carefully.

**Safety Precautions:**

- The operator must wear **safety shoes and gloves**.
- The rider must wear a **helmet** and sit firmly on the vehicle.
- Ensure **no loose clothing** or dangling straps near rollers.
- Keep hands and feet away from roller pinch points.
- The motorcycle must be **centered** on the rollers to avoid fall-off.
- Do not apply brake when the roller is **stationary** — this will stall the motor.
- Use a **fire extinguisher** nearby in case of hydraulic fluid leak or fire.
- The tester should be **earthed properly** to prevent electrical shock.

*(3.5 marks — procedure steps, 2.5 marks — safety precautions, 1 mark — diagram reference.)*

#### **Model Solution for B1(b) — Option B:**

**Given:**

$$\eta_f = 75 \;\%, \quad \eta_r = 60 \;\%, \quad W_{total} = 150 \text{ kgf}$$

$$W_f = 0.6 \times 150 = 90 \text{ kgf}, \quad W_r = 0.4 \times 150 = 60 \text{ kgf}$$

$$v = 40 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{40 \times 1000}{3600} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}$$

**(i) Front and Rear Brake Forces:**

$$F_{bf} = \dfrac{\eta_f \times W_f}{100} = \dfrac{75 \times 90}{100} = 67.5 \text{ kgf}$$

$$F_{br} = \dfrac{\eta_r \times W_r}{100} = \dfrac{60 \times 60}{100} = 36.0 \text{ kgf}$$

**(ii) Maximum Deceleration:**

Total brake force:

$$F_{total} = F_{bf} + F_{br} = 67.5 + 36.0 = 103.5 \text{ kgf} = 103.5 \times 9.81 = 1015.3 \text{ N}$$

Vehicle mass:

$$m = \dfrac{W_{total}}{g} = \dfrac{150 \times 9.81}{9.81} = 150 \text{ kg}$$

Maximum deceleration:

$$a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{m} = \dfrac{1015.3}{150} = 6.77 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(iii) Theoretical Stopping Distance from 40 km/h:**

Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ with $v = 0$:

$$s = \dfrac{u^2}{2a} = \dfrac{(11.11)^2}{2 \times 6.77} = \dfrac{123.4}{13.54} = 9.11 \text{ m}$$

**Conclusion:** The vehicle will come to a halt in approximately **9.11 metres** from a speed of 40 km/h under maximum braking.

*(2 marks — part (i), 2 marks — part (ii), 2 marks — part (iii) with conclusion, 1 mark — proper units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing percentage and fraction:** Writing $\eta = 0.75$ instead of $75 \;\%$ — costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $g$** when converting between kgf and N. The question often switches units mid-way; keep a sharp eye.
> 3. **Using the simple average** $\dfrac{\eta_f + \eta_r}{2}$ when the load distribution is unequal — this is mathematically wrong unless $W_f = W_r$. The **weighted formula** $\dfrac{F_{bf}+F_{br}}{W_f+W_r} \times 100$ is correct.
> 4. **Not stating the standards** (CMVR / AIS-052 / IS 14664) when asked for compliance. Examiners award 1 mark just for the standard reference.
> 5. **Mixing up test speed units:** Test speed is in **km/h** but deceleration is in **m/s²**. Convert using $\dfrac{\text{km/h} \times 1000}{3600}$.
> 6. **Omitting safety precautions** in Part B answers — even if not asked, mentioning 2–3 safety points adds 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Not drawing the block diagram** in B1(a) — diagram carries **1.5 to 2 marks** of the 7.
> 8. **Stopping distance derivation:** Using the formula $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$ is correct **only if** deceleration $a = \mu g$. If the question gives a specific $a$, use $s = v^2 / (2a)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Brake efficiency $\eta = (F_b / W) \times 100 \;\%$.
- [ ] **CMVR Standards for Two-Wheeler:** Combined **50 %**, Front alone **30 %**, Rear alone **25 %**.
- [ ] **Test Speed:** 2.5 to 5 km/h (low-speed dynamic test per AIS-052).
- [ ] **Weighted overall efficiency formula:** $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{F_{bf}+F_{br}}{W_f+W_r} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Simple average** is only valid when $W_f = W_r$ (rare in practice).
- [ ] **Stopping distance formula:** $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2a}$ where $a = \mu g$.
- [ ] **Deceleration formula:** $a = F_{total} / m$, with $m = W_{total} / g$.
- [ ] **Unit conversion:** $1 \, \text{kgf} = 9.81 \, \text{N}$; $1 \, \text{km/h} = \dfrac{5}{18} \, \text{m/s}$.
- [ ] **Components of tester:** Roller, motor, load cell, encoder, microcontroller, display, clamps.
- [ ] **Working principle:** Newton's third law — brake force at tire is reacted as tangential force on roller.
- [ ] **Peak force** is held in memory; only the **maximum** value during the test is recorded.
- [ ] **Slip** at peak brake force is typically 5–25 %.
- [ ] **Friction coefficient** is numerically equal to brake efficiency in fraction.
- [ ] **Safety:** Helmet for rider, gloves for operator, no loose clothing, earth the tester, fire extinguisher nearby.
- [ ] **A vehicle that fails** is deemed to have defective brakes under Section 53 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988.
- [ ] **Block diagram** is mandatory in long-answer questions; draw with labeled arrows.
- [ ] **Mention the standard** (CMVR / AIS-052 / IS 14664) in every compliance-related answer for easy marks.
- [ ] **Sample numerical pattern** that KTU loves: Given $W_{total}$, load distribution %, peak $F_{bf}$, $F_{br}$ → ask for $\eta_f$, $\eta_r$, $\eta_{overall}$, $a_{max}$, and stopping distance.
- [ ] **Examiner loves** neat tables in answer sheets — use them wherever comparison is required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Check Ignition Timing of Battery/Magneto Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMUL308 - AUTOMOTIVE SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Check Ignition Timing of Battery/Magneto Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ignition Timing** is the precise crankshaft angular position, measured in degrees of rotation **Before Top Dead Centre (BTDC)**, at which the spark plug fires to initiate combustion of the compressed air-fuel mixture inside the engine cylinder.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the experiment *“Check Ignition Timing of Battery / Magneto Ignition System”* is classified under **PCO1: Ability to dismantle, inspect, and assess the working condition of major automotive subsystems using standard service tools and manufacturer service manuals.**

**Definition – Battery Ignition System:**
A battery-supplied DC ignition system in which a 6 V / 12 V battery feeds the ignition coil primary; the contact breaker (CB points) interrupts the primary current, and the resulting self-induced high-tension EMF (typically **20,000 V – 30,000 V**) is delivered across the spark plug gap.

**Definition – Magneto Ignition System:**
A self-contained ignition system in which a permanent-magnet rotating armature (or inductor) generates the primary current directly; no external battery is required. It is widely used in **two-wheelers, small gensets, and aviation piston engines**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Board Pattern Highlight:** Examiners frequently demand a *contrast table* comparing the two systems along with the *step-by-step timing-check procedure* (mark distribution: 7 + 7).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of ignition timing like **clapping in rhythm with a falling glass**:

- The **piston pushing up** is the glass falling.
- If you clap **too early** (advanced timing), the spark fires before the piston has compressed the mixture properly → poor combustion, *pinking/knocking*.
- If you clap **too late** (retarded timing), the spark fires after the piston has started moving down → wasted energy, overheated exhaust valve, *loss of power*.
- Clapping **just at the right moment** (typically 4°–10° BTDC) gives the flame kernel enough time (≈ 2 ms) to build up peak pressure **just after TDC** — perfect combustion.

> [!TIP]
> The phrase *"fire the spark, but let the flame spread"* is the heart of ignition timing logic.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Petrol SI Engine) |
|---|---|
| Static Ignition Advance | **4° – 8° BTDC** |
| Total Centrifugal Advance | **20° – 30°** |
| Vacuum Advance | **5° – 15°** |
| Spark Plug Gap | **0.6 mm – 1.0 mm** |
| Secondary Voltage Required | **10 kV – 30 kV** |
| Dwell Angle (CB Point System) | **35° – 45°** of cam rotation |
| Ignition Coil Primary Resistance | **1.5 Ω – 4 Ω** (ballast) |
| Ignition Coil Secondary Resistance | **5 kΩ – 15 kΩ** |

---
