# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Ignition Timing

Combustion is **not instantaneous**. After the spark, a finite *flame kernel development time* (≈ 2–3 ms) is required before peak cylinder pressure develops. Therefore, the spark must occur **before TDC** so that peak pressure coincides with **≈ 12° – 15° ATDC** — the position of maximum leverage on the crank.

**Three mechanical/physical factors that dictate timing:**
1. **Engine speed** → flame travel time (ms) is roughly constant, so crank-degrees of advance must *increase* with RPM. ⇒ *Centrifugal advance mechanism.*
2. **Load (manifold vacuum)** → low load ⇒ low mixture density ⇒ slow flame ⇒ more advance. ⇒ *Vacuum advance mechanism.*
3. **Fuel octane** → low octane ⇒ prone to knock ⇒ less advance. ⇒ *Manual initial advance + octane selector.*

## 2.2 Battery Ignition System – Functional Anatomy

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery (6 V / 12 V) | DC energy source |
| 2 | Ignition Switch | ON/OFF control |
| 3 | Ballast Resistor | Limits current at low RPM, bypassed during cranking |
| 4 | Ignition Coil (Step-up Transformer) | Steps up 12 V → 20–30 kV |
| 5 | Contact Breaker (CB Points) | Mechanical switch to break primary circuit |
| 6 | Condenser (0.20 – 0.25 µF) | Suppresses contact arcing; speeds up collapse of magnetic flux |
| 7 | Distributor (with centrifugal & vacuum advance) | Routes HT pulse to correct cylinder, provides advance |
| 8 | Rotor Arm + Distributor Cap | HT distribution |
| 9 | Spark Plug | Creates ionised arc across electrode gap |
| 10 | HT Cables | Carry 20–30 kV from coil/distributor to plug |

## 2.3 Magneto Ignition System – Functional Anatomy

The magneto is essentially an **ignition coil + generator combined in one housing**. It has its own rotating magnet, primary & secondary windings, and contact breaker.

**Working Principle (Shed-of-Flux Method):**
1. Permanent magnet rotates past a soft-iron pole piece.
2. As the magnetic flux rises to maximum, the CB points **close** and primary current builds up.
3. As flux collapses (magnet moves past), CB points **open** → flux through primary collapses → high EMF is induced in secondary.
4. HT pulse is routed via the distributor / HT cable to the spark plug.

**Types of Magneto Ignition:**
- **High-Tension (HT) Magneto** – generates and delivers HT directly to plug (e.g., 2-wheeler engines).
- **Low-Tension (LT) Magneto** – generates HT in a separate coil (older systems).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are *examinable* and frequently appear in either Part A (3-mark) derivations or Part B (14-mark) numericals.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Coil Step-Up Ratio} \quad N \;=\; \frac{N_s}{N_p} \quad \text{(typical 1 : 60 to 1 : 100)} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(2) Induced EMF (Faraday's Law)} \quad E_s \;=\; -N_s \,\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \;\; [\text{Volts}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(3) Energy Stored in Coil} \quad W \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, L \, I_p^{\,2} \;\; [\text{Joules}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(4) Mean Coil Primary Current} \quad I_p \;=\; \frac{V}{R}\,\Bigl(1 - e^{-Rt/L}\Bigr) \;\; [\text{Amp}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(5) Ignition Advance Relationship} \quad \theta_{total} \;=\; \theta_{initial} + \theta_{centrifugal} + \theta_{vacuum} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(6) Spark Plug Firing Voltage (required)} \quad V_{req} \;=\; k \,\frac{p \, d}{\ln\!\bigl(\tfrac{A}{d}\bigr)} \;\; [\text{kV}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(7) Dwell Time} \quad t_d \;=\; \frac{\alpha_d}{6 \,\omega} \;\; [\text{seconds}] \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(8) Flame Travel Angle} \quad \theta_{flame} \;=\; 6 \, n \, \tau \;\; [\text{degrees}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $N_s$ / $N_p$ = turns in secondary / primary winding
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux linkage in Weber
- $L$ = primary inductance in Henry (typically 5 – 10 mH)
- $R$ = primary circuit resistance in Ohm
- $\alpha_d$ = dwell angle (degrees of cam rotation)
- $\omega$ = camshaft angular velocity in rad/s
- $n$ = engine speed in RPM
- $\tau$ = flame kernel development time in seconds (≈ 2 × 10⁻³ s)
- $p$ = cylinder pressure in kPa
- $d$ = spark plug gap in mm
- $A$ = plug electrode cross-section area
- $k$ = dielectric strength of air-fuel mixture

| Symbol | Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_s$ | Secondary induced EMF | Volt (V) |
| $W$ | Energy stored in coil magnetic field | Joule (J) |
| $I_p$ | Peak primary current | Ampere (A) |
| $L$ | Inductance | Henry (H) |
| $t_d$ | Dwell time | second (s) |
| $\theta$ | Angular advance | degree (°) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In modern EMS (Engine Management Systems), these formulas are encoded inside the ECU's lookup maps for *optimum spark advance vs. RPM vs. load*. The *mechanical* advance methods we study here were the **direct ancestors** of those ECU maps.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation – Peak Secondary Voltage from Ignition Coil

We derive the peak voltage induced in the secondary winding of an ignition coil using **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, applying it to a step-down in primary current (CB point opening).

**Given:**
- Primary winding inductance = $L_p$ (Henry)
- Mutual inductance between primary and secondary = $M$ (Henry)
- Step-up ratio = $N_s / N_p$
- Peak primary current at the instant of break = $I_{p,\max}$ (A)
- CB point opening time (flux collapse) = $t_c$ (s)

**Step 1: Faraday's Law for the primary**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_p &= -L_p \frac{dI_p}{dt} = -L_p \frac{(0 - I_{p,\max})}{t_c} \\
E_p &= +\frac{L_p \, I_{p,\max}}{t_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: The negative sign indicates induced EMF opposes the change; we drop the sign because we are interested in magnitude.)*

**Step 2: Apply transformer action (mutual flux linkage)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_s}{E_p} &= \frac{N_s}{N_p} = n \quad \text{(step-up ratio)} \\
E_s &= n \cdot E_p = n \cdot \frac{L_p \, I_{p,\max}}{t_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: In an ideal transformer, the induced voltage ratio equals the turns ratio.)*

**Step 3: Substitute typical values for verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Take } L_p = 8 \,\text{mH},\; I_{p,\max} = 4 \,\text{A},\; t_c = 80 \,\mu\text{s},\; n = 80 \\
&L_p \, I_{p,\max} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 = 32 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{V·s} \\
&\frac{L_p \, I_{p,\max}}{t_c} = \frac{32 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 400 \,\text{V} \\
&E_s = 80 \times 400 = 32{,}000 \,\text{V} \;=\; \mathbf{32 \,kV}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: This 32 kV comfortably exceeds the 10–25 kV required to bridge the spark plug gap under all engine operating conditions — confirming the design.)

**Result:** $E_s = 32 \, \text{kV}$ is the theoretical peak secondary voltage.

## 3.2 Derivation – Required Spark Advance Angle for a Given Engine Speed

**Given:**
- Flame development time: $\tau = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s (typical)
- Engine speed: $N$ (RPM)

**Step 1: Convert RPM to angular velocity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\; [\text{rad/s}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate flame travel angle in crank degrees**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{flame} \;[\text{deg}] &= \omega \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} \\
&= \frac{2\pi N}{60} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \\
&= 0.012 \, N \;\; \text{degrees}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: After simplification, the per-1000-RPM rule of thumb: ~12° of advance needed per 1000 RPM.)*

**Step 3: Numerical check for $N = 3000 \,\text{RPM}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{flame} &= 0.012 \times 3000 = 36 \degree \\
\text{Initial advance (typical)} &= 6 \degree \, \text{BTDC} \\
\text{Total advance needed} &= 36 + 6 = 42 \degree \;\; \text{BTDC}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: At high RPM, ignition must be advanced substantially — this is exactly what the *centrifugal advance mechanism* does.*

**Result:** Spark must lead TDC by **≈ 42°** at 3000 RPM for a typical petrol engine.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Timing-Check Decision Tree

| Observed Symptom | Likely Timing Defect | Probable Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pinging / Knocking on acceleration | Over-advanced timing | Stuck centrifugal weights, vacuum line leak | Retard initial timing; service advance mechanism |
| Loss of power, overheating | Retarded timing | Worn CB points (smaller dwell), slipped distributor | Replace points; reset initial timing |
| Hard starting, misfire | No spark / wrong rotor phasing | Cracked distributor cap, burned points | Replace cap; check rotor phasing |
| Erratic timing (jumps) | Worn advance mechanism, worn cam ring | End-of-life mechanical components | Rebuild distributor or replace |
| High fuel consumption, no knock | Slightly retarded | Default OEM spec, not actually a defect | Verify against service manual; recheck |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *initial timing* (set at idle, static) with *total advance* (under running conditions). The lab experiment checks **initial timing** with a timing light; total advance is a *computed* quantity.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Functional Architecture of a Battery Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    bat[Battery 12V] --> sw[Ignition Switch]
    sw --> br[Ballast Resistor]
    br --> coilP[Ignition Coil Primary]
    coilP --> cb[Contact Breaker Points]
    cb --> cond[Condenser 0.22uF]
    cond --> gnd1[Ground]
    coilP -.Coupled Magnetic Flux.-> coilS[Ignition Coil Secondary]
    coilS --> dist[Distributor Cap and Rotor]
    dist --> sp1[Spark Plug Cyl 1]
    dist --> sp2[Spark Plug Cyl 2]
    dist --> sp3[Spark Plug Cyl 3]
    dist --> sp4[Spark Plug Cyl 4]
    cam[Camshaft Driven] -.Drives.-> cb
    adv1[Centrifugal Advance] -.Acts on.-> dist
    adv2[Vacuum Advance Diaphragm] -.Acts on.-> dist
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram shows the **functional architecture flow**: energy path on the left, mechanical coupling arrows on the right. The .Coupled Magnetic Flux. edge represents the electromagnetic step-up action of the coil (no physical conductor).

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Magneto Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pm[Permanent Magnet Rotor] --> pole[Soft Iron Pole Piece]
    pole --> primW[Primary Winding]
    pole -.Mutual Flux.-> secW[Secondary Winding]
    primW --> cbM[CB Points and Condenser]
    cbM --> gndM[Ground Return]
    camM[Cam on Rotor Shaft] -.Operates.-> cbM
    secW --> rotM[HT Rotor]
    rotM --> capM[Distributor Cap]
    capM --> plugM[Spark Plug]
```

## 4.3 Procedure Flowchart – Checking Ignition Timing (Static Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Warm up engine to operating temperature] --> B[Locate timing marks on crank pulley and timing cover]
    B --> C[Connect timing light to Cyl 1 HT cable]
    C --> D[Disconnect and plug vacuum advance hose]
    D --> E[Idle engine at manufacturer specified RPM]
    E --> F[Strobe timing light at Cyl 1]
    F --> G{Top mark aligned with specified mark?}
    G -- No --> H[Loosen distributor clamp bolt]
    H --> I[Rotate distributor body to advance or retard]
    I --> F
    G -- Yes --> J[Tighten distributor clamp bolt]
    J --> K[Reconnect vacuum advance hose]
    K --> L[Road test if required]
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional View – Magneto Ignition Timing Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pos1[Position A: Magneto Aligned with Pole Piece Flux Maximum] --> cbclosed[CB Points Closed: Primary Current Builds]
    cbclosed --> pos2[Position B: Magneto Near Neutral Flux]
    pos2 --> cbopen[CB Points Open: Flux Collapses]
    cbopen --> pos3[Position C: Spark Delivered to Plug]
    pos3 -->|Piston nearing TDC| pos4[Position D: Combustion Peak 12 to 15 deg ATDC]
    pos4 --> end[End of Power Stroke]
```

> [!TIP]
> In **magneto** systems, the *CB-point opening* is mechanically fixed relative to the magneto shaft, so the **physical angular position of the magneto rotor** at the moment of spark defines ignition timing. Adjusting timing = rotating the magneto body in its housing.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer with Model Answers)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define ignition timing. Why is the spark delivered before TDC and not exactly at TDC?**

**Model Answer:**
Ignition timing is the crank angular position (in degrees BTDC) at which the spark plug fires to initiate combustion. The spark is delivered *before* TDC (typically 4°–10° BTDC) because combustion is not instantaneous — a finite *flame kernel development time* (≈ 2–3 ms) is needed. Advancing the spark ensures that peak cylinder pressure — and hence maximum torque — occurs at the optimal position **12°–15° ATDC**, which is the crank angle of maximum mechanical leverage on the piston.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner allocates 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for *flame kernel* concept, 1 mark for *peak pressure at 12°–15° ATDC*.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**List any four components of a battery ignition system and state the function of the condenser.**

**Model Answer:**
Components:
1. **Battery** – DC source.
2. **Ignition coil** – Steps up voltage 12 V → 20–30 kV.
3. **Contact breaker** – Mechanical switch interrupting primary current.
4. **Distributor** – Routes HT to correct cylinder.

**Function of condenser (0.20–0.25 µF):** Connected across CB points, it (a) suppresses the inductive arc that would otherwise burn the contact surfaces, and (b) accelerates the *collapse* of primary magnetic flux, thereby increasing the rate of flux change (dΦ/dt) and hence the induced secondary EMF (per Faraday's law).

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Either/Or choice within a module**. Either Question A or Question B must be answered, NOT both. Each carries **14 marks** split typically as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **battery ignition system**. List any **four** advantages of electronic ignition over conventional battery ignition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure to check and adjust initial ignition timing** of a 4-stroke petrol engine using a **timing light (stroboscopic method)**. State two safety precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Q A(a)

**Working of Battery Ignition System:**

1. With ignition switch **ON**, current flows from the 12 V battery → ballast resistor → ignition coil primary winding → contact breaker points → ground.
2. As the **camshaft rotates**, the cam lobe opens the contact breaker (CB) points. The instant they open, the **primary current is interrupted**.
3. The sudden collapse of magnetic flux in the primary winding induces a high EMF in the secondary (Faraday's law) of the order of **20 kV – 30 kV**.
4. This HT pulse travels through the **distributor rotor → distributor cap → HT cable → spark plug gap**, where it ionises the gap and creates a spark.
5. The spark ignites the compressed air-fuel mixture. The **condenser** (0.22 µF, connected across CB points) prevents arcing and speeds up flux collapse.

**Block Diagram:** Refer Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Advantages of Electronic Ignition (any 4):**
- No contact-breaker wear → maintenance-free.
- Higher & more consistent secondary voltage (up to 40 kV).
- Precise timing control via ECU map.
- Better cold-start performance.
- Improved fuel economy and lower emissions.
- No dwell-angle adjustment required.

#### Model Solution – Q A(b)

**Procedure to Check & Adjust Initial Ignition Timing:**

1. **Warm up** the engine to normal operating temperature (cooling fan cycles twice).
2. **Locate timing marks**: timing pointer on timing cover and degree marks on the crankshaft pulley/flywheel.
3. **Connect timing light**: inductive pickup clamp on **No. 1 cylinder HT cable**; power leads to battery ±.
4. **Disconnect and plug** the vacuum-advance hose at the distributor.
5. **Idle the engine** at manufacturer's specified RPM (typically 650–850 RPM idle).
6. **Aim timing light** at the timing marks. The strobe will freeze the mark position.
7. **Compare** the frozen mark position with the manufacturer's specification (e.g., 4° BTDC).
8. If misaligned, **loosen the distributor clamp bolt** and slowly rotate the distributor body (clockwise to retard, counter-clockwise to advance on most engines).
9. Recheck, then **tighten** the clamp bolt.
10. **Reconnect** the vacuum-advance hose, switch off, and record the value.

**Safety Precautions (any 2):**
- Keep hands, clothing, and timing-light cables clear of the **rotating fan and belts**.
- Avoid touching HT leads with bare hands when engine is running (≈ 30 kV lethal).
- Ensure good ventilation — petrol fumes are flammable.
- Never crank the engine with HT cable disconnected into open air without a proper grounding tool.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Option Set 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a magneto ignition system** with a suitable diagram. Compare it with a battery ignition system (any 4 points). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder 4-stroke SI engine runs at 3500 RPM. The flame kernel development time is 2 ms. Calculate the **(i)** flame travel angle and **(ii)** total ignition advance required if initial timing is 6° BTDC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – Q B(a)

**Construction of Magneto Ignition System:**

A magneto combines the functions of an ignition coil and an electric generator in one housing. Major parts:
1. **Permanent-magnet rotor** (rotates at ½ crank speed via gears)
2. **Laminated soft-iron pole piece / core**
3. **Primary winding** (a few hundred turns of thick wire) wound on the core
4. **Secondary winding** (≈ 15,000 – 20,000 turns of fine wire)
5. **Contact breaker (CB) points + condenser**
6. **Cam on the rotor shaft** that operates the CB points
7. **Distributor / HT rotor + cap** + HT cable + spark plug

**Working:**
- The rotating magnet sweeps its magnetic field across the pole piece; the **flux** through the core first rises to a maximum, then collapses through zero to a negative maximum.
- At the **flux peak**, CB points close → primary current builds up (→ magnetic energy stored in core, $W = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$).
- At the **flux-zero (neutral) point**, CB points open → primary current is cut off → flux collapses rapidly → large EMF is induced in the secondary (Faraday's law) → HT pulse is sent to the plug.
- Timing is varied by **rotating the magneto body** relative to the engine (advancing = rotate in direction opposite to drive; retarding = rotate in direction of drive).

**Diagram:** Refer Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Comparison Table – Magneto vs Battery Ignition:**

| S.No. | Parameter | Battery Ignition | Magneto Ignition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy source | External 12 V battery required | Self-contained (permanent magnet) |
| 2 | Reliability at low RPM | Strong spark even at low speed | Weak spark at very low speed/cold start |
| 3 | Weight & bulk | Heavier (battery + wiring) | Lighter, compact |
| 4 | Maintenance | Periodic CB point, condenser replacement | Similar CB maintenance |
| 5 | Application | Cars, larger vehicles | 2-wheelers, gensets, small aircraft |
| 6 | Cold-start | Excellent (battery always at full voltage) | Difficult (weak spark below 100 RPM) |
| 7 | Spark intensity consistency | Drops with low battery | Drops sharply with low RPM |

#### Model Solution – Q B(b)

**Given:**
- $N = 3500 \,\text{RPM}$
- $\tau = 2 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{s} = 2 \,\text{ms}$
- Initial timing $\theta_i = 6°$ BTDC

**Step (i) — Flame Travel Angle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3500}{60} = 366.52 \;\; \text{rad/s} \\[6pt]
\theta_{flame} &= \omega \times \tau \times \frac{180}{\pi} \;\; \text{degrees} \\[6pt]
&= 366.52 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \\[6pt]
&= 366.52 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 57.296 \\[6pt]
&= 41.99 \degree \;\;\approx\;\; \mathbf{42 \degree}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: At 3500 RPM, the crank rotates ≈ 42° during the 2 ms flame kernel build-up — hence the spark must lead by at least 42°.)*

**Step (ii) — Total Ignition Advance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{total} &= \theta_{initial} + \theta_{flame} \\[6pt]
&= 6° + 42° \\[6pt]
&= \mathbf{48°} \;\; \text{BTDC}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Text: In practice, the centrifugal advance mechanism would have already added ≈ 28°–30° to the initial 6°, giving the correct running total advance of ~48° BTDC.)*

**Result:** (i) Flame travel angle ≈ **42°**, (ii) Total ignition advance ≈ **48° BTDC**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't** write "advance" without specifying **BTDC** — examiner deducts **0.5 mark** for missing unit direction.
> - **Don't** confuse *flame travel angle* with *ignition advance*; they are different quantities that are *added* to get the total.
> - **Don't** skip stating the formula for $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ — it is a frequently tested derived step.
> - **Don't** forget to convert $\tau = 2 \,\text{ms}$ to **seconds** before substituting.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Ignition timing** = crank angle (° BTDC) at which spark occurs.
- [x] Three timing elements: **Initial + Centrifugal + Vacuum** = Total advance.
- [x] **Battery ignition** uses external 12 V battery; **Magneto** is self-contained.
- [x] **Condenser (0.20–0.25 µF)** suppresses CB arcing & speeds up flux collapse.
- [x] Secondary voltage required = **10 – 30 kV**; coil turns ratio ≈ **1 : 80**.
- [x] Static timing check: **timing light, idle, vacuum hose plugged, distributor clamp loosened**.
- [x] Clockwise rotation of distributor body on most engines = **retards** timing.
- [x] Peak cylinder pressure should occur at **12°–15° ATDC** for max torque.
- [x] Flame kernel development time ≈ **2 ms** → at 3000 RPM → ≈ **36°** crank rotation.
- [x] **Knocking** = timing too advanced OR low-octane fuel; **Overheating** = timing too retarded.
- [x] In **magneto**, timing is adjusted by **rotating the magneto body** in its mounting.
- [x] **Dwell angle** (35°–45°) = primary current ON-time, controlled by CB gap.
- [x] Always **disconnect & plug vacuum hose** when checking *initial* timing.
- [x] **No. 1 cylinder** is the reference for timing on virtually all engines.
- [x] HT leads carry **20–30 kV** — *never* touch when engine is running.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "IC-VD" for advance components:**
> **I**nitial (static) → **C**entrifugal (RPM-based) → **V**acuum (load-based) → **D**istributor body (manual knob).
